1.text: In the first year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, king Mosiah had passed away. He had fought the good fight and walked uprightly before God, leaving no one to reign after him. But he had established laws that the people accepted, so they were required to follow the laws he had made.
In the first year of Alma's time as chief judge, a man was brought before him to be judged. This man was large and well known for his great strength.

He had been going among the people, preaching what he called the word of God, speaking against the church. He told the people that every priest and teacher should become popular and should not work with their hands but should be supported by the people.

He also told the people that all mankind would be saved in the end, and that they did not need to fear or worry, but could hold their heads high and be happy. He said the Lord had created all people and had also redeemed all people, and that in the end, everyone would have eternal life.
He taught these things so much that many people believed his words, so many that they began to support him and give him money.
And he began to be lifted up in pride, wearing very expensive clothing, and he even began to establish a church based on his preaching.

As he was going to preach to those who believed his words, he met a man who belonged to the church of God, one of their teachers. He began to argue with him fiercely, trying to lead the people of the church away. But the man stood firm against him, correcting him with the words of God.
The man's name was Gideon, and he was the same person who had been an instrument in God's hands in freeing the people of Limhi from bondage.

Because Gideon opposed him with the words of God, the man became furious with Gideon and drew his sword and began to strike him. Gideon was advanced in years and could not withstand the blows, so he was killed by the sword.
The man who killed him was seized by the people of the church and brought before Alma to be judged for the crimes he had committed.
He stood before Alma and defended himself with great boldness.

But Alma said to him: "This is the first time that priestcraft has been introduced among this people. And you are not only guilty of priestcraft, but you have tried to enforce it by the sword. If priestcraft were to be enforced among this people, it would lead to their complete destruction."
"You have shed the blood of a righteous man who did much good among this people. If we were to spare you, his blood would cry out against us for justice."
"Therefore you are condemned to die, according to the law given to us by Mosiah, our last king. It has been accepted by this people, and so the people must follow the law."

They took him—his name was Nehor—and carried him to the top of the hill Manti. There, between the heavens and the earth, he admitted that what he had taught the people was contrary to the word of God. And there he suffered a shameful death.
However, this did not stop priestcraft from spreading through the land. Many people loved worldly things and went around preaching false teachings for the sake of wealth and honor.
They did not dare lie openly, for fear of the law, since liars were punished. So they pretended to preach according to their beliefs, and the law could not punish anyone for their beliefs.
They did not dare steal, for fear of the law, since thieves were punished. They did not dare rob or murder either, for murderers were punished with death.
But those who did not belong to the church of God began to persecute those who did belong to the church and had taken upon themselves the name of Christ.

They persecuted them and hurt them with all kinds of harsh words, and this was because of their humility—because they were not proud, and because they shared the word of God with one another freely, without charging money.
There was a strict rule in the church that no member should persecute those outside the church, and that there should be no persecution among themselves.
Nevertheless, many among them became proud and began to argue heatedly with their opponents, even to the point of hitting each other with their fists.
This was in the second year of the reign of the judges, and it caused great sorrow and difficulty for the church.
Many people's hearts became hardened, and their names were removed from the records so they were no longer counted among the people of God. Many others also left the church.
This was a great trial for those who remained faithful, but they were steadfast and unmovable in keeping God's commandments, and they patiently endured the persecution heaped upon them.

When the priests left their work to share the word of God with the people, the people also left their work to hear it. After the priest had taught them the word of God, everyone returned diligently to their work. The priest did not consider himself above his listeners, for the preacher was no better than the listener, and the teacher was no better than the student. They were all equal, and everyone worked according to their ability.
They shared what they had with the poor, the needy, the sick, and the suffering. They did not wear expensive clothing, yet they were clean and presentable.
In this way they established the affairs of the church and began to have lasting peace again, despite all their persecutions.

Because of the steadiness of the church, they became very prosperous, having an abundance of everything they needed—plenty of flocks and herds and fattened animals of every kind, along with plenty of grain, gold, silver, precious things, silk, fine linen, and all kinds of quality cloth.

In their prosperity, they did not turn away anyone who was without clothing, or hungry, or thirsty, or sick, or in need of care. They did not set their hearts on riches. They were generous to everyone—old and young, slave and free, male and female, whether in the church or not—showing no favoritism toward anyone in need.
In this way they prospered and became far wealthier than those who did not belong to their church.

Those who did not belong to the church gave themselves over to sorcery, idolatry, laziness, gossip, jealousy, and conflict. They wore expensive clothing and were lifted up in pride. They persecuted, lied, stole, robbed, committed sexual sins, and murdered, along with all kinds of wickedness. Nevertheless, the law was enforced against all who broke it, as much as possible.

By enforcing the law and having every person suffer the consequences of what they had done, the people became more restrained and did not dare commit wickedness if it could be discovered. So there was much peace among the people of Nephi until the fifth year of the reign of the judges.

2.text: At the beginning of the fifth year of the reign of the judges, a conflict arose among the people. A man named Amlici, who was very cunning and wise in worldly ways and who followed the teachings of the man who killed Gideon (who had been executed according to the law)—
This Amlici had, through his cleverness, drawn many people to follow him—so many that they became very powerful. They began trying to make Amlici king over the people.

This alarmed the people of the church and all those who had not been drawn away by Amlici's persuasions, because they knew that according to their law, such matters had to be decided by the voice of the people.
If Amlici could win the vote of the people, he would, being a wicked man, take away their rights and the privileges of the church. It was his intention to destroy the church of God.

The people gathered together throughout the entire land, every person according to their own opinion, whether for or against Amlici, in separate groups, having fierce debates and remarkable arguments with each other.
They assembled to cast their votes on the matter, and the votes were brought before the judges.
The voice of the people came out against Amlici, and he was not made king over the people.
This brought great joy to those who were against him, but Amlici stirred up his followers to be angry against those who opposed him.
His followers gathered together and appointed Amlici to be their king.
When Amlici was made king over his followers, he commanded them to take up weapons against their fellow Nephites, so that he might bring them under his control.
The people of Amlici were called Amlicites, and the rest were called Nephites, or the people of God.
The Nephites were aware of the Amlicites' intentions, so they prepared to meet them. They armed themselves with swords, curved blades, bows, arrows, stones, slings, and all kinds of weapons of war.
They were prepared to meet the Amlicites when they came. Captains and higher captains and chief captains were appointed according to their numbers.
Amlici armed his men with every kind of weapon of war and also appointed rulers and leaders over his people to lead them to war against their fellow Nephites.
The Amlicites came to the hill Amnihu, which was east of the river Sidon near the land of Zarahemla, and there they began to make war against the Nephites.
Alma, being the chief judge and governor of the people of Nephi, went out with his people—with his captains and chief captains—at the head of his armies to battle against the Amlicites.
They began to fight the Amlicites on the hill east of Sidon. The Amlicites fought with great strength, and many of the Nephites fell before them.
Nevertheless, the Lord strengthened the Nephites, and they defeated the Amlicites with great slaughter, until the Amlicites began to flee before them.

The Nephites chased the Amlicites all that day, killing many of them. Twelve thousand five hundred and thirty-two Amlicites were killed, and six thousand five hundred and sixty-two Nephites were killed.

When Alma could chase the Amlicites no further, he had his people set up camp in the valley of Gideon, which was named after the Gideon who had been killed by Nehor with a sword. The Nephites camped there for the night.
Alma sent scouts to follow the remaining Amlicites so he could learn their plans and protect his people from being destroyed.
The scouts he sent to watch the Amlicite camp were named Zeram, Amnor, Manti, and Limher. They went out with their men to observe the Amlicite camp.
The next day, the scouts returned to the Nephite camp in a great hurry, shocked and filled with fear, saying:

"We followed the Amlicite camp, and to our great astonishment, in the land of Minon above the land of Zarahemla, toward the land of Nephi, we saw a huge army of Lamanites! The Amlicites have joined them!"

"They are attacking our people in that land, and our people are fleeing before them with their flocks, their wives, and their children, toward our city. Unless we hurry, they will take our city, and our families will be killed."
The people of Nephi packed up their tents and left the valley of Gideon, heading toward their city, the city of Zarahemla.
As they were crossing the river Sidon, the Lamanites and Amlicites, nearly as numerous as the sands of the sea, attacked them to destroy them.

But the Nephites were strengthened by the Lord. They had prayed intensely that He would deliver them from their enemies, and the Lord heard their cries and strengthened them. The Lamanites and Amlicites fell before them.
Alma fought Amlici face to face with the sword, and they battled fiercely against each other.
Alma, being a man of God and full of faith, cried out: "O Lord, have mercy and spare my life, so that I may be an instrument in your hands to save and protect this people."
After Alma said these words, he fought Amlici again and was strengthened so that he killed Amlici with the sword.
He also fought against the king of the Lamanites, but the Lamanite king retreated from Alma and sent his guards to fight him instead.
But Alma and his guards fought the Lamanite king's guards until they killed or drove them all back.

He cleared the ground—the riverbank on the west side of the river Sidon—throwing the bodies of the slain Lamanites into the waters of Sidon, so his people would have room to cross and fight the Lamanites and Amlicites on the west side of the river.
After all of them had crossed the river Sidon, the Lamanites and Amlicites began to flee before them, even though they were too numerous to count.
They fled from the Nephites toward the wilderness to the west and north, far beyond the borders of the land. The Nephites chased them with all their might and killed many of them.

They were attacked on every side, killed, and driven until they were scattered to the west and north, reaching the wilderness called Hermounts, which was a part of the wilderness infested with wild and dangerous beasts.
Many died in the wilderness from their wounds and were eaten by the beasts and vultures. Their bones have been found and piled up on the ground.

3.text: The Nephites who survived the war buried those who had been killed—the number of dead was too great to count—and then they all returned to their lands, their homes, their wives, and their children.
Many women and children had been killed with the sword, and many of their flocks and herds were also lost. Many of their grain fields were destroyed, trampled down by the armies of men.
Many of the Lamanites and Amlicites who had been killed on the bank of the river Sidon were thrown into the waters of Sidon, and their bones are in the depths of the river. There are many of them.

The Amlicites could be told apart from the Nephites because they had marked themselves with red on their foreheads, following the custom of the Lamanites. However, they had not shaved their heads like the Lamanites.
The Lamanites' heads were shaved, and they wore only a leather covering around their waists, along with their armor, bows, arrows, stones, and slings.

The Lamanites had dark skin, which was a mark set upon their ancestors as a curse because of their disobedience and rebellion against their brothers Nephi, Jacob, Joseph, and Sam, who were righteous and holy men.
Their brothers had tried to destroy them, so they were cursed. The Lord God set a mark upon Laman, Lemuel, the sons of Ishmael, and the Ishmaelite women.

This was done so that their descendants could be distinguished from the descendants of their brothers, so the Lord God could preserve His people and keep them from mixing with those who believed in false traditions that would lead to their destruction.
Whoever mixed their descendants with the Lamanites brought the same curse upon their children.
Therefore, whoever allowed himself to be led away by the Lamanites was counted among them, and a mark was set upon him.

Whoever did not believe in the traditions of the Lamanites, but instead believed in the records brought from Jerusalem and in the correct traditions of their ancestors—those who believed in God's commandments and kept them—were called Nephites, or the people of Nephi, from that time on.
They are the ones who have kept the true records of their people and also of the Lamanites.
Now let us return to the Amlicites, for they also had a mark set upon them. They set the mark upon themselves—a mark of red upon their foreheads.

In this way, the word of God was fulfilled, for these are the words He spoke to Nephi: "I have cursed the Lamanites, and I will set a mark on them so that they and their descendants may be separated from you and your descendants, from now and forever, unless they repent of their wickedness and turn to me so I can have mercy on them."
"And I will also set a mark upon anyone who mixes their descendants with your brothers, so they may be cursed as well."
"And I will set a mark upon anyone who fights against you and your descendants."

"And whoever leaves you shall no longer be called your descendant. I will bless you and everyone who is called your descendant from now and forever." These were the promises of the Lord to Nephi and to his descendants.

The Amlicites did not realize they were fulfilling the words of God when they began marking themselves on their foreheads. Nevertheless, they had come out in open rebellion against God, and so it was fitting that the curse should fall upon them.
I want you to see that they brought the curse upon themselves, and in the same way, every person who is cursed brings their own condemnation upon themselves.

Not many days after the battle fought in the land of Zarahemla between the Lamanites and Amlicites, another army of Lamanites came against the people of Nephi in the same place where the first army had met the Amlicites.
An army was sent to drive them out of the land.
Alma himself was suffering from a wound and could not go up to battle against the Lamanites at that time.
But he sent a large army against them, and they killed many of the Lamanites and drove the rest out of their borders.
Then they returned and began to establish peace in the land, not being troubled by their enemies for a time.
All of these things—all these wars and conflicts—began and ended in the fifth year of the reign of the judges.

In one year, thousands and tens of thousands of souls were sent to the eternal world to receive their rewards according to their works—whether good or bad—receiving eternal happiness or eternal misery, depending on which spirit they chose to follow, whether good or evil.

For every person receives their wages from the one they choose to obey, and this is according to the words of the spirit of prophecy. So let it be according to the truth. And so ended the fifth year of the reign of the judges.

4.text: In the sixth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, there were no conflicts or wars in the land of Zarahemla.
But the people were deeply troubled because of the loss of their loved ones, their flocks and herds, and their fields of grain that had been trampled and destroyed by the Lamanites.

Their suffering was so great that every person had reason to mourn. They believed it was the judgments of God sent upon them because of their wickedness and sinful behavior, and so they were reminded of their duty.

They began to build up the church more fully. Many were baptized in the waters of Sidon and joined the church of God. They were baptized by Alma, who had been appointed the high priest over the people of the church by his father, Alma.

In the seventh year of the reign of the judges, about three thousand five hundred people joined the church of God and were baptized. And so ended the seventh year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, and there was continual peace during all that time.

In the eighth year of the reign of the judges, the people of the church began to grow proud because of their great wealth—their fine silks, their fine linen, their many flocks and herds, their gold, their silver, and all kinds of precious things they had gained through hard work. In all these things, they became filled with pride, and they began to wear very expensive clothing.

This caused great sorrow to Alma and to many of the people he had appointed as teachers, priests, and elders over the church. Many of them were deeply grieved by the wickedness they saw growing among their people.

They saw with great sorrow that the people of the church were becoming proud, setting their hearts on riches and the empty things of the world. They began to look down on one another and to persecute those who did not agree with them.

In this eighth year of the reign of the judges, great conflicts arose among the people of the church. There was jealousy, fighting, hostility, persecution, and pride—even greater pride than among those who did not belong to the church of God.
And so ended the eighth year of the reign of the judges. The wickedness of the church was a major stumbling block to those who did not belong to the church, and the church began to lose its progress.

At the beginning of the ninth year, Alma saw the wickedness of the church. He also saw that the church's bad example was leading unbelievers from one sin to another, bringing destruction upon the people.

He saw great inequality among the people. Some were lifting themselves up in pride, looking down on others, and turning their backs on the needy, the unclothed, the hungry, the thirsty, the sick, and the suffering.

This caused great sorrow among some of the people, while others humbled themselves and helped those in need—sharing what they had with the poor and needy, feeding the hungry, and enduring all kinds of hardships for the sake of Christ, who would come according to the spirit of prophecy.

They looked forward to that day, keeping a remission of their sins, and were filled with great joy because of the resurrection of the dead, through the will, power, and deliverance of Jesus Christ from the chains of death.

When Alma saw the suffering of the humble followers of God and the persecution heaped upon them by the rest of his people, and saw all their inequality, he became very sorrowful. Nevertheless, the Spirit of the Lord did not fail him.

He chose a wise man from among the elders of the church and gave him authority, by the voice of the people, to make and enforce laws according to the laws that had been given, based on the wickedness and crimes of the people.
This man's name was Nephihah, and he was appointed chief judge. He sat in the judgment seat to judge and govern the people.
Alma did not give him the office of high priest over the church but kept that position for himself. He handed the judgment seat over to Nephihah.

He did this so that he could go out among his people—the people of Nephi—to preach the word of God to them, to remind them of their duty, and to use the word of God to tear down all the pride, craftiness, and conflict among his people. He saw no other way to reclaim them except by bearing pure testimony to them.

At the beginning of the ninth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, Alma gave up the judgment seat to Nephihah and devoted himself entirely to the high priesthood of the holy order of God, to testify of the word through the spirit of revelation and prophecy.

5.text: Alma began to preach the word of God to the people, first in the land of Zarahemla, and then throughout all the land.
These are the words he spoke to the people of the church in the city of Zarahemla, from his own record:

"I, Alma, having been appointed by my father Alma to be a high priest over the church of God—he having power and authority from God to do these things—I say to you that he began to establish a church in the land on the borders of Nephi, the land called the land of Mormon. He baptized his brothers and sisters in the waters of Mormon."
"And they were delivered from the people of king Noah by the mercy and power of God."

"After that, they were brought into bondage by the Lamanites in the wilderness. I tell you, they were in captivity, and again the Lord delivered them from bondage by the power of His word. We were brought into this land, and here we also began to establish the church of God throughout this land."

"And now, my brothers and sisters, you who belong to this church, have you remembered well enough the captivity of your ancestors? Have you remembered His mercy and patience toward them? And have you remembered that He delivered their souls from hell?"

"He changed their hearts. He woke them from a deep sleep, and they awoke to God. They were in the middle of darkness, yet their souls were lit up by the light of the everlasting word. They were surrounded by the chains of death and the bonds of hell, and everlasting destruction waited for them."
"And now I ask you, my brothers and sisters, were they destroyed? I say to you, no, they were not."

"And again I ask, were the chains of death broken, and the bonds of hell that surrounded them—were they released? I say to you, yes, they were released, and their souls expanded, and they sang of redeeming love. And I say to you that they are saved."
"And now I ask you, on what conditions are they saved? What reason did they have to hope for salvation? What caused them to be freed from the chains of death and the bonds of hell?"
"I can tell you—did not my father Alma believe in the words spoken by the prophet Abinadi? Was Abinadi not a holy prophet? Did he not speak the words of God, and my father Alma believe them?"
"Because of his faith, a mighty change was made in his heart. I tell you that this is all true."

"And he preached the word to your ancestors, and a mighty change was also made in their hearts. They humbled themselves and put their trust in the true and living God. They were faithful to the end, and therefore they were saved."
"And now I ask you, my brothers and sisters in the church: have you been spiritually born of God? Have you received His image in your faces? Have you experienced this mighty change in your hearts?"

"Do you have faith in the redemption of Him who created you? Do you look forward with an eye of faith to see this mortal body raised in immortality, and this decaying body raised in a form that cannot decay, to stand before God and be judged according to what you have done in this mortal body?"
"I say to you, can you imagine hearing the voice of the Lord saying to you on that day: 'Come to me, you blessed ones, for your works have been works of righteousness on the earth'?"
"Or do you imagine that you can lie to the Lord on that day and say, 'Lord, our works have been righteous works on the earth,' and that He will save you?"

"Or can you imagine yourselves brought before the judgment seat of God with your souls filled with guilt and remorse, remembering all your guilt perfectly—every act of wickedness—remembering that you have defied the commandments of God?"
"I say to you, can you look up to God on that day with a pure heart and clean hands? I say to you, can you look up with the image of God engraved upon your faces?"
"I say to you, can you think of being saved when you have given yourselves over to the devil?"

"I say to you, you will know on that day that you cannot be saved, for no one can be saved unless their garments are washed white. Their garments must be purified until they are cleansed from every stain, through the blood of Him spoken of by our ancestors, who would come to redeem His people from their sins."

"And now I ask you, my brothers and sisters, how will you feel if you stand before the judgment bar of God with your garments stained with blood and all kinds of filthiness? What will these things testify against you?"
"Will they not testify that you are murderers, and that you are guilty of all kinds of wickedness?"

"My brothers and sisters, do you suppose that such a person can have a place in the kingdom of God, sitting with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and all the holy prophets whose garments are cleansed, spotless, pure, and white?"

"I say to you, no—unless you would make our Creator a liar from the beginning, you cannot suppose that such people can have a place in the kingdom of heaven. They will be cast out, for they are the children of the kingdom of the devil."
"And now I say to you, my brothers and sisters, if you have experienced a change of heart, and if you have felt the desire to sing the song of redeeming love, I ask you: can you feel that way now?"

"Have you lived blamelessly before God? Could you say, if you were called to die right now, that you have been humble enough? That your garments have been cleansed and made white through the blood of Christ, who will come to redeem His people from their sins?"

"Have you been stripped of pride? I say to you, if you have not, you are not prepared to meet God. You must prepare quickly, for the kingdom of heaven is soon at hand, and those who are not prepared will not have eternal life."

"I ask, is there anyone among you who has not been stripped of envy? I say to you that such a person is not prepared, and I urge them to prepare quickly, for the hour is close at hand, and they do not know when the time will come. Such a person will not be found guiltless."
"And again I say to you, is there anyone among you who mocks their neighbor, or heaps persecution upon them?"
"Woe to such a person, for they are not prepared, and the time is at hand when they must repent or they cannot be saved!"
"Yes, woe to all who work wickedness! Repent, repent, for the Lord God has spoken it!"
"He sends an invitation to all people, for the arms of mercy are stretched out toward them, and He says: 'Repent, and I will receive you.'"
"He says: 'Come to me and you will share the fruit of the tree of life. You will eat and drink of the bread and the waters of life freely.'"
"Come to me and bring forth works of righteousness, and you will not be cut down and thrown into the fire—"
"For the time is at hand when whoever does not bring forth good fruit, or whoever does not do works of righteousness, will have reason to wail and mourn."

"O you workers of wickedness! You who are puffed up by the empty things of the world, you who have claimed to know the ways of righteousness but have gone astray like sheep without a shepherd—even though a shepherd has called after you and is still calling after you—yet you will not listen to his voice!"

"I say to you that the good shepherd is calling you. In His own name He calls you, which is the name of Christ. If you will not listen to the voice of the good shepherd, to the name by which you are called, then you are not the sheep of the good shepherd."

"And if you are not the sheep of the good shepherd, whose flock do you belong to? I say to you that the devil is your shepherd, and you are of his flock. Who can deny this? Whoever denies this is a liar and a child of the devil."
"For I say to you that whatever is good comes from God, and whatever is evil comes from the devil."

"Therefore, if a person does good works, they are listening to the voice of the good shepherd and following Him. But whoever does evil works becomes a child of the devil, because they listen to his voice and follow him."
"And whoever does this must receive their wages from him. Therefore, as their wages, they receive death as far as righteousness is concerned, being dead to all good works."

"And now, my brothers and sisters, I want you to listen to me, for I speak with all the energy of my soul. I have spoken plainly to you so you cannot misunderstand, and I have spoken according to the commandments of God."

"For I am called to speak in this way, according to the holy order of God, which is in Christ Jesus. I am commanded to stand and testify to this people the things spoken by our ancestors about what is to come."
"And this is not all. Do you think I don't know these things myself? I testify to you that I do know that the things I have spoken are true. And how do you think I know they are true?"

"They are made known to me by the Holy Spirit of God. I have fasted and prayed many days to know these things for myself. And now I do know for myself that they are true, for the Lord God has revealed them to me by His Holy Spirit, and this is the spirit of revelation that is in me."

"Moreover, I tell you that it has been revealed to me that the words spoken by our ancestors are true, according to the spirit of prophecy that is in me, which is also through the manifestation of the Spirit of God."

"I say to you that I know for myself that whatever I shall say to you about what is to come is true. I know that Jesus Christ shall come—the Son, the Only Begotten of the Father, full of grace, mercy, and truth. He is the one who will come to take away the sins of the world—the sins of every person who steadfastly believes on His name."

"And now I say to you that this is the calling I have received—to preach to my beloved brothers and sisters, and to everyone who lives in the land. I am to preach to all—old and young, slave and free. I say to you, the elderly, the middle-aged, and the rising generation: you must repent and be born again."

"Thus says the Spirit: 'Repent, all you ends of the earth, for the kingdom of heaven is soon at hand. The Son of God comes in His glory, in His might, majesty, power, and dominion.' My beloved brothers and sisters, I say to you, the Spirit says: 'Behold the glory of the King of all the earth; the King of heaven shall very soon shine forth among all the children of men.'"
"And also the Spirit says to me, crying with a mighty voice: 'Go forth and say to this people—Repent, for unless you repent, you can in no way inherit the kingdom of heaven.'"

"And again I say to you, the Spirit says: 'The ax is laid at the root of the tree. Therefore every tree that does not bring forth good fruit shall be cut down and thrown into the fire—a fire that cannot be put out, an unquenchable fire.' Remember, the Holy One has spoken it."

"And now, my beloved brothers and sisters, I say to you: can you resist these words? Can you set these things aside and trample the Holy One under your feet? Can you be puffed up in the pride of your hearts? Will you continue wearing expensive clothing and setting your hearts on the empty things of the world, on your riches?"

"Will you continue thinking you are better than one another? Will you continue persecuting your brothers and sisters who humble themselves and walk after the holy order of God, by which they were brought into this church, having been sanctified by the Holy Spirit, bringing forth works worthy of repentance?"
"And will you continue turning your backs on the poor and the needy and withholding what you have from them?"
"And finally, all of you who persist in your wickedness—I say to you that these are the ones who will be cut down and thrown into the fire unless they quickly repent."

"And now I say to you, all who want to follow the voice of the good shepherd: come out from among the wicked and be separate, and do not touch their unclean things. Their names shall be blotted out so that the names of the wicked shall not be counted among the names of the righteous, that the word of God may be fulfilled, which says: 'The names of the wicked shall not be mixed with the names of my people.'"

"For the names of the righteous shall be written in the book of life, and to them I will grant an inheritance at my right hand. And now, my brothers and sisters, what do you have to say against this? I say to you, if you speak against it, it does not matter, for the word of God must be fulfilled."

"For what shepherd among you, having many sheep, does not watch over them to keep the wolves from entering and devouring his flock? If a wolf enters his flock, does he not drive it out? In the end, if he can, he will destroy it."

"And now I say to you that the good shepherd is calling you. If you will listen to His voice, He will bring you into His fold, and you will be His sheep. He commands you not to allow any dangerous wolf to enter among you, so that you will not be destroyed."
"And now I, Alma, command you in the words of Him who has commanded me, that you be careful to do the things I have spoken to you."

"I speak as a command to those of you who belong to the church, and to those who do not belong to the church, I speak as an invitation, saying: come and be baptized unto repentance, so that you also may share the fruit of the tree of life."

6.text: After Alma had finished speaking to the people of the church in the city of Zarahemla, he ordained priests and elders by laying his hands on them according to the order of God, to lead and watch over the church.
Those who did not belong to the church but repented of their sins were baptized unto repentance and received into the church.

Those who did belong to the church but did not repent of their wickedness and humble themselves before God—those who were lifted up in pride—were rejected, and their names were removed so they were no longer counted among the righteous.
In this way they began to establish the order of the church in the city of Zarahemla.
I want you to understand that the word of God was available to everyone. No one was kept from gathering together to hear the word of God.
Nevertheless, the children of God were commanded to gather together often and join in fasting and fervent prayer for the well-being of the souls of those who did not know God.

When Alma had put these things in order, he left them—the church in the city of Zarahemla—and traveled east of the river Sidon into the valley of Gideon. A city had been built there called the city of Gideon, in the valley called Gideon, named after the man who was killed by Nehor with the sword.

Alma went and began to preach the word of God to the church established in the valley of Gideon, according to the revealed truth of the word spoken by his ancestors, and according to the spirit of prophecy in him, and according to the testimony of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who would come to redeem His people from their sins, and through the holy order by which Alma was called. And so it is written. Amen.

7.text: "My beloved brothers and sisters, since I have been allowed to come to you, I will try to speak to you in my own words, by my own mouth. This is the first time I have spoken to you in person, since I have been entirely occupied with the judgment seat and have had so much business that I could not come to you."
"I could not have come even now if the judgment seat had not been given to another to serve in my place. The Lord in His great mercy has allowed me to come to you."

"I have come with great hopes and a strong desire to find that you had humbled yourselves before God, that you had continued praying for His grace, that you were blameless before Him, and that you were not in the terrible situation our brothers and sisters were in at Zarahemla."
"But blessed be the name of God, for He has let me know—and has given me the exceedingly great joy of knowing—that they have been brought back to the path of His righteousness."

"I trust, according to the Spirit of God that is in me, that I will also have joy because of you. But I do not want my joy over you to come from the same kind of suffering and sorrow I have had over the brothers and sisters in Zarahemla, for my joy over them came only after wading through much suffering and sorrow."

"But I trust that you are not in such a state of disbelief as your brothers and sisters were. I trust that you are not lifted up in pride, that you have not set your hearts on riches and the empty things of the world. I trust that you do not worship idols, but that you worship the true and living God and look forward to the forgiveness of your sins with an everlasting faith in what is to come."
"For I tell you, there are many things yet to come. But there is one thing that is more important than all the rest—for the time is not far away when the Redeemer lives and comes among His people."

"I do not say that He will come among us during the time He lives in His mortal body, for the Spirit has not told me that would happen. I do not know about that. But this much I do know: the Lord God has power to do all things that are according to His word."

"But the Spirit has told me this much, saying: 'Cry out to this people, saying: Repent, and prepare the way of the Lord, and walk in His paths, which are straight. For the kingdom of heaven is at hand, and the Son of God comes upon the face of the earth.'"

"He shall be born of Mary, at Jerusalem, which is the land of our ancestors. She will be a virgin, a precious and chosen vessel, who will be overshadowed and conceive by the power of the Holy Ghost, and bring forth a son—even the Son of God."

"And He shall go forth, suffering pains, afflictions, and temptations of every kind. This is so the word might be fulfilled which says He will take upon Himself the pains and sicknesses of His people."

"And He will take upon Himself death, so that He may break the chains of death that bind His people. He will take upon Himself their weaknesses, so that His heart may be filled with mercy, knowing in the flesh how to help His people in their weaknesses."

"Now the Spirit knows all things. Nevertheless, the Son of God suffers in the flesh so that He might take upon Himself the sins of His people, that He might erase their wrongdoings through the power of His deliverance. And this is the testimony that is in me."

"Now I say to you that you must repent and be born again, for the Spirit says if you are not born again you cannot inherit the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, come and be baptized unto repentance, so that you may be washed from your sins, that you may have faith in the Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, who is mighty to save and to cleanse from all unrighteousness."

"I say to you, come and do not be afraid. Lay aside every sin that so easily traps you and binds you down to destruction. Come and go forth, and show your God that you are willing to repent of your sins and enter into a covenant with Him to keep His commandments, and demonstrate it to Him today by going into the waters of baptism."

"Whoever does this and keeps the commandments of God from that time forward will remember that I told them—they shall have eternal life, according to the testimony of the Holy Spirit, which testifies in me."

"And now, my beloved brothers and sisters, do you believe these things? I say to you, yes, I know that you believe them. And the way I know that you believe them is by the manifestation of the Spirit that is in me. Because your faith is strong concerning these things I have spoken of, my joy is great."
"For as I said to you from the beginning, I had a great desire that you would not be in the same difficult situation as your brothers and sisters, and I have found that my desire has been fulfilled."
"For I can see that you are on the paths of righteousness. I can see that you are on the path that leads to the kingdom of God. I can see that you are making His paths straight."

"I can see that it has been made known to you through the testimony of His word that He cannot walk in crooked paths. He does not go back on what He has said. He does not shift from right to left, or from what is right to what is wrong. Therefore, His course is one eternal round."

"He does not dwell in unholy temples, and nothing filthy or unclean can be received into the kingdom of God. Therefore I say to you, the time shall come, at the last day, that whoever is filthy shall remain in their filthiness."

"And now, my beloved brothers and sisters, I have said these things to awaken you to a sense of your duty to God, so that you may walk blamelessly before Him, following the holy order of God by which you have been received."

"And now I want you to be humble, submissive, and gentle; easy to ask for help; full of patience and long-suffering; self-controlled in all things; diligent in keeping God's commandments at all times; asking for whatever you need, both spiritual and physical; always giving thanks to God for whatever you receive."
"And see that you have faith, hope, and charity, and then you will always be full of good works."

"And may the Lord bless you and keep your garments spotless, so that in the end you may be brought to sit down with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and the holy prophets who have existed since the world began, with your garments spotless like their garments are spotless, in the kingdom of heaven, to go out no more."

"And now, my beloved brothers and sisters, I have spoken these words to you according to the Spirit which testifies in me, and my soul greatly rejoices because of the outstanding diligence and attention you have given to my words."

"And now, may the peace of God rest upon you, upon your houses and lands, upon your flocks and herds, and all that you possess—your wives and your children—according to your faith and good works, from this time forth and forever. And so I have spoken. Amen."

8.text: Alma returned from the land of Gideon, after teaching the people of Gideon many things that cannot be written. He had established the order of the church, just as he had done before in the land of Zarahemla. He returned to his own house in Zarahemla to rest from his labors.
And so ended the ninth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

At the beginning of the tenth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, Alma left and traveled to the land of Melek, on the west of the river Sidon, near the borders of the wilderness.
He began to teach the people in the land of Melek according to the holy order of God by which he had been called, and he taught throughout all the land of Melek.
People came to him from all the borders of the land near the wilderness, and they were baptized throughout the whole land.
When he had finished his work in Melek, he left and traveled three days' journey to the north of the land of Melek. He came to a city called Ammonihah.

It was the custom of the Nephite people to name their lands, cities, and villages—even all their small villages—after the person who first settled them. This is how the land of Ammonihah got its name.
When Alma arrived in the city of Ammonihah, he began to preach the word of God to them.
But Satan had gained a strong hold on the hearts of the people of Ammonihah, so they would not listen to Alma's words.

Nevertheless, Alma worked hard in the spirit, wrestling with God in fervent prayer, asking that God would pour out His Spirit on the people of the city and that He would allow Alma to baptize them unto repentance.

Still, they hardened their hearts, saying to him: "We know who you are, Alma. We know you are high priest over the church that you have established in many parts of the land, according to your tradition. We are not part of your church, and we do not believe in such foolish traditions."
"And since we are not part of your church, we know you have no power over us. You have given up the judgment seat to Nephihah, so you are not the chief judge over us."
After the people said this, rejected all his words, insulted him, and spit on him, and had him thrown out of their city, he left and headed toward the city called Aaron.

As he was traveling there, weighed down with sorrow, wading through much suffering and anguish of soul because of the wickedness of the people in the city of Ammonihah, an angel of the Lord appeared to him while he was burdened with this grief, saying:

"Blessed are you, Alma. Therefore, lift up your head and rejoice, for you have great reason to be glad. You have been faithful in keeping the commandments of God from the time you received your first message from Him. I am the one who delivered that message to you."
"And I am sent to command you to return to the city of Ammonihah and preach again to the people of the city. Tell them that unless they repent, the Lord God will destroy them."
"For they are now making plans to destroy the liberty of your people (so says the Lord), which is contrary to the laws, judgments, and commandments He has given to His people."
After Alma received his message from the angel of the Lord, he quickly returned to the land of Ammonihah. He entered the city by a different way, through the south side of the city of Ammonihah.
As he entered the city he was hungry, and he said to a man: "Will you give something to eat to a humble servant of God?"

The man said to him: "I am a Nephite, and I know that you are a holy prophet of God, for you are the man an angel told me about in a vision, saying: 'You shall receive him.' So come to my house and I will share my food with you. I know you will be a blessing to me and my house."
The man took him into his house. His name was Amulek, and he brought out bread and meat and set it before Alma.
Alma ate bread and was filled, and he blessed Amulek and his house and gave thanks to God.
After he had eaten and was satisfied, he said to Amulek: "I am Alma, and I am the high priest over the church of God throughout the land."

"I have been called to preach the word of God among all this people, according to the spirit of revelation and prophecy. I was in this land and they would not receive me. They cast me out, and I was about to turn my back on this land forever."
"But I have been commanded to return and prophesy to this people, and to testify against them concerning their sins."
"And now, Amulek, because you have fed me and taken me in, you are blessed, for I was hungry, having fasted many days."
Alma stayed many days with Amulek before he began to preach to the people.
The people continued to grow worse in their sins.

The word came to Alma, saying: "Go, and also tell my servant Amulek to go forth and prophesy to this people, saying: 'Repent, for thus says the Lord: Unless you repent, I will visit this people in my anger, and I will not turn my fierce anger away.'"
Alma went forth, along with Amulek, among the people to declare the words of God to them, and they were filled with the Holy Ghost.

They were given power so that they could not be held in prisons, and no one could kill them. However, they did not use this power until they were bound and thrown into prison. This was done so the Lord could show His power through them.
They went forth and began to preach and prophesy to the people, according to the spirit and power the Lord had given them.

9.text: And again, I, Alma, having been commanded by God to take Amulek and go preach again to the people of the city of Ammonihah—as I began to preach to them, they began to argue with me, saying:
"Who are you? Do you think we are going to believe the testimony of one man, even if he preaches to us that the earth will pass away?"
They did not understand the words they spoke, for they did not know that the earth actually would pass away.
They also said: "We will not believe your words if you prophesy that this great city will be destroyed in one day."
They did not know that God could do such marvelous things, for they were a hard-hearted and stubborn people.
And they said: "Who is God, that He sends no greater authority than one man among this people to tell us the truth of such great and marvelous things?"
They came forward to grab me, but they did not. I stood with boldness to declare to them—I boldly testified to them, saying:
"O you wicked and corrupt generation, how quickly you have forgotten the traditions of your ancestors! How soon you have forgotten the commandments of God!"
"Do you not remember that our ancestor Lehi was brought out of Jerusalem by the hand of God? Do you not remember that they were all led by Him through the wilderness?"
"Have you forgotten so quickly how many times He delivered our ancestors from the hands of their enemies and preserved them from being destroyed, even by the hands of their own brothers?"

"If it had not been for His unmatched power, His mercy, and His patience toward us, we would certainly have been wiped from the face of the earth long before now, and perhaps been condemned to a state of endless misery and suffering."

"Now I say to you that He commands you to repent, and unless you repent, you can in no way inherit the kingdom of God. But that is not all—He has commanded you to repent, or He will completely destroy you from the face of the earth. He will visit you in His anger, and in His fierce anger He will not hold back."

"Do you not remember the words He spoke to Lehi: 'If you keep my commandments, you will prosper in the land'? And also: 'If you will not keep my commandments, you will be cut off from the presence of the Lord'?"

"I want you to remember that because the Lamanites have not kept God's commandments, they have been cut off from the Lord's presence. We can see that the Lord's word has proven true in this, and the Lamanites have been cut off from His presence since the beginning of their disobedience in the land."

"Nevertheless, I tell you that it will be more bearable for them on the day of judgment than for you, if you remain in your sins—and even more bearable for them in this life than for you, unless you repent."

"For many promises have been made to the Lamanites. It is because of the traditions of their ancestors that they remain in a state of ignorance, so the Lord will be merciful to them and preserve their existence in the land."

"At some point in time they will be brought to believe in His word and to know the errors of their ancestors' traditions. Many of them will be saved, for the Lord will be merciful to all who call on His name."

"But I say to you that if you persist in your wickedness, your days will not be prolonged in the land, for the Lamanites will be sent against you. If you do not repent, they will come at a time you do not expect, and you will be visited with complete destruction. It will come according to the fierce anger of the Lord."

"For He will not allow you to live in your sins and destroy His people. I say to you, no—He would rather allow the Lamanites to destroy all His people who are called the people of Nephi, if it were possible for them to fall into sin and disobedience after having received so much light and so much knowledge from the Lord their God."

"After being such a highly favored people of the Lord—after being favored above every other nation, people, language, or group—after having all things made known to them according to their desires, their faith, and their prayers, about what has been, what is, and what is to come—"

"Having been visited by the Spirit of God, having talked with angels, having heard the voice of the Lord, having the spirit of prophecy and revelation, and also many gifts—the gift of speaking in tongues, the gift of preaching, the gift of the Holy Ghost, and the gift of translation—"

"After having been delivered by God from the land of Jerusalem by the hand of the Lord, saved from famine, from sickness, and from diseases of every kind—having been made strong in battle so they would not be destroyed—having been brought out of bondage time after time—having been kept and preserved until now—having prospered until they are rich in all kinds of things—"

"And now I say to you that if this people, who have received so many blessings from the hand of the Lord, should disobey despite the light and knowledge they have, I say to you that if they fall into sin, it would be far more bearable for the Lamanites than for them."

"For the Lord's promises extend to the Lamanites, but they do not extend to you if you disobey. Has not the Lord clearly promised and firmly declared that if you rebel against Him, you will be completely destroyed from the face of the earth?"

"For this reason, so that you will not be destroyed, the Lord has sent His angel to visit many of His people, telling them they must go forth and cry mightily to this people: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near at hand.'"

"In not many days, the Son of God shall come in His glory—and His glory shall be the glory of the Only Begotten of the Father, full of grace, fairness, and truth, full of patience, mercy, and long-suffering, quick to hear the cries of His people and to answer their prayers."
"He comes to redeem those who will be baptized unto repentance through faith on His name."

"Therefore, prepare the way of the Lord, for the time is at hand when everyone shall receive a reward for their works, according to what they have been. If they have been righteous, they will receive salvation for their souls through the power and deliverance of Jesus Christ. If they have been evil, they will receive the condemnation of their souls through the power and captivity of the devil."
"This is the voice of the angel, crying out to the people."

"And now, my beloved brothers and sisters—for you are my brothers and sisters, and you should be beloved—you should bring forth works worthy of repentance, seeing that your hearts have been deeply hardened against the word of God, and seeing that you are a lost and fallen people."
When I, Alma, had spoken these words, the people were furious with me because I told them they were a hard-hearted and stubborn people.
Because I also told them they were a lost and fallen people, they were angry with me and tried to seize me so they could throw me into prison.
But the Lord did not allow them to take me and cast me into prison at that time.
Then Amulek stepped forward and began to preach to them as well. Not all of Amulek's words are recorded, but a portion of his words are written in this book.

10.text: Now these are the words that Amulek preached to the people in the land of Ammonihah, saying:

"I am Amulek. I am the son of Giddonah, who was the son of Ishmael, who was a descendant of Aminadi. It was this same Aminadi who interpreted the writing on the wall of the temple, which was written by the finger of God."
"Aminadi was a descendant of Nephi, who was the son of Lehi, who came from the land of Jerusalem and was a descendant of Manasseh, who was the son of Joseph who was sold into Egypt by his brothers."
"I am also a man of considerable reputation among all those who know me. I have many relatives and friends, and I have also acquired much wealth through my own hard work."

"Despite all this, I never really knew much about the ways of the Lord, His mysteries, and His marvelous power. I said I never knew much about these things, but I was wrong, for I have seen much of His mysteries and His marvelous power—even in preserving the lives of this people."

"Nevertheless, I hardened my heart. I was called many times and I would not listen. I knew about these things, yet I chose not to acknowledge them. I kept rebelling against God in the wickedness of my heart, even until the fourth day of this seventh month, which is in the tenth year of the reign of the judges."

"As I was traveling to visit a close relative, an angel of the Lord appeared to me and said: 'Amulek, return to your own house, for you shall feed a prophet of the Lord—a holy man, a chosen man of God. He has fasted many days because of the sins of this people, and he is hungry. You shall take him into your house and feed him, and he shall bless you and your house, and the blessing of the Lord shall rest upon you and your house.'"

"I obeyed the voice of the angel and went back toward my house. As I was going, I found the man the angel had told me about—the one I was to receive into my house—and it was this same man who has been speaking to you about the things of God."
"The angel told me he is a holy man, and so I know he is a holy man because it was said by an angel of God."

"And again, I know that the things he has testified about are true. For I say to you, as the Lord lives, He has sent His angel to make these things known to me, and He did this while this Alma has been staying at my house."
"For he has blessed my house—he has blessed me, and my wife, and my children, and my father, and my relatives. The blessing of the Lord has rested upon us according to the words he spoke."

When Amulek had spoken these words, the people began to be amazed because there was more than one witness testifying about the things they were accused of, and also about the things that were to come, according to the spirit of prophecy in them.

Nevertheless, some among them planned to question Alma and Amulek, hoping to trick them with clever tactics into saying something that could be used against them. They wanted to hand them over to their judges to be convicted under the law, and to be killed or thrown into prison, based on whatever charge they could make or get witnesses for.
The men who sought to destroy them were lawyers, hired or appointed by the people to handle the law during trials of crimes brought before the judges.
These lawyers were trained in all the clever tactics of the people, which enabled them to be skilled in their profession.
They began to question Amulek, hoping to make him contradict himself or go against what he had said.

They did not know that Amulek could detect their schemes. But as they began to question him, he perceived their thoughts and said to them: "O you wicked and corrupt generation, you lawyers and hypocrites! You are laying the foundations of the devil. You are setting traps and snares to catch the holy ones of God."
"You are making plans to twist the ways of the righteous and bring the wrath of God upon your heads, even to the complete destruction of this people."

"Mosiah, our last king, spoke well when he was about to give up the kingdom, having no one to pass it to, and arranged for this people to be governed by their own votes. He said that if the time should come when the voice of this people chose wickedness—if the time should come when this people fell into sin—they would be ripe for destruction."
"And now I say to you that the Lord judges your sins well. He calls out to this people through the voice of His angels: 'Repent, repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'"
"He calls out well through the voice of His angels: 'I will come down among my people, with fairness and justice in my hands.'"

"And I tell you that if it were not for the prayers of the righteous people now in the land, you would already be visited with complete destruction. Yet it would not be by a flood, as in the days of Noah, but by famine, disease, and the sword."

"It is the prayers of the righteous that are keeping you safe. But if you cast the righteous out from among you, the Lord will not hold back. In His fierce anger He will come out against you, and you will be struck by famine, disease, and the sword. The time is soon at hand unless you repent."
The people became even more angry with Amulek, and they cried out: "This man is speaking against our laws which are just, and against our wise lawyers whom we have chosen."

But Amulek reached out his hand and cried even louder to them: "O you wicked and corrupt generation, why has Satan gained such a great hold on your hearts? Why do you give yourselves over to him so he can have power over you, blinding your eyes so you will not understand the words that are spoken to you, words that are true?"
"Have I testified against your law? You do not understand. You say I have spoken against your law, but I have not. I have spoken in support of your law, to your condemnation."
"And now I say to you that the foundation of this people's destruction is being laid by the unrighteousness of your lawyers and your judges."

When Amulek said these things, the people cried out against him: "Now we know this man is a child of the devil, for he has lied to us. He has spoken against our law, and now he says he has not spoken against it."
"And he has also spoken against our lawyers and our judges."
The lawyers made a mental note to remember these things and use them against him.
Among them was a man named Zeezrom. He was the first to accuse Amulek and Alma. He was one of the most skilled among them and had much business among the people.
The goal of these lawyers was to make money, and they earned their pay according to their work.

11.text: Under the law of Mosiah, every judge received wages based on the amount of time they spent judging cases that were brought before them.

If a man owed someone money and refused to pay, the person he owed could report him to the judge. The judge would use his authority to send officers to bring the man before him. The judge would then decide the case based on the law and the evidence, and the man would be forced to pay what he owed, or else be stripped of his possessions, or be cast out from among the people as a thief and a robber.
A judge received a senine of gold for a day's work, or a senum of silver, which was equal in value to a senine of gold. This was established by the law.

Now these are the names of the different pieces of their gold and silver, according to their value. The names were given by the Nephites, because they did not count money the same way as the Jews in Jerusalem. They had changed their system of counting and measuring to fit the needs of the people over the generations, until the reign of the judges, which had been established by king Mosiah.
The gold coins were as follows: a senine of gold, a seon of gold, a shum of gold, and a limnah of gold.
The silver coins were: a senum of silver, an amnor of silver, an ezrom of silver, and an onti of silver.
A senum of silver was equal in value to a senine of gold, and either one could buy a measure of barley or a measure of any kind of grain.
A seon of gold was worth twice as much as a senine.
And a shum of gold was worth twice as much as a seon.
And a limnah of gold was equal in value to all of them combined.
An amnor of silver was worth as much as two senums.
An ezrom of silver was worth as much as four senums.
And an onti was worth as much as all of them combined.
Now these are the values of the smaller amounts in their system:
A shiblon is half of a senum, so a shiblon would buy half a measure of barley.
And a shiblum is half of a shiblon.
And a leah is half of a shiblum.
These were the values in their system of counting.
An antion of gold was equal to three shiblons.

Now the only reason the judges stirred up the people was to make money. Because they were paid according to how much they worked, they encouraged the people to riot and cause all kinds of trouble and wickedness so they would have more cases to judge and earn more money. This is why they stirred up the people against Alma and Amulek.

This man Zeezrom began to question Amulek, saying, "Will you answer a few questions?" Now Zeezrom was skilled in the tricks of the devil and wanted to destroy what was good. So he said to Amulek, "Will you answer the questions I ask you?"

Amulek said to him, "Yes, if it is according to the Spirit of the Lord, which is in me, because I will say nothing that goes against the Spirit of the Lord." Then Zeezrom said to him, "Look, here are six onties of silver, and I will give you all of them if you will deny the existence of a Supreme Being."
Amulek said, "You child of hell, why are you trying to tempt me? Don't you know that the righteous don't give in to such temptations?"
"Do you believe there is no God? I tell you no—you know there is a God, but you love money more than you love him."

"And now you have lied before God. You said to me, 'Look at these six onties of great worth—I will give them to you,' when in your heart you intended to keep them from me. Your only desire was for me to deny the true and living God so you could have an excuse to destroy me. And now, for this great evil, you will receive your punishment."
Zeezrom said to him, "Are you saying there is a true and living God?"
Amulek said, "Yes, there is a true and living God."
Zeezrom asked, "Is there more than one God?"
And Amulek answered, "No."
Zeezrom asked him again, "How do you know these things?"
And Amulek said, "An angel has made them known to me."
Zeezrom asked again, "Who is he that shall come? Is it the Son of God?"
And Amulek said to him, "Yes."
Zeezrom asked, "Will he save his people in their sins?" Amulek answered, "I tell you he will not, because it is impossible for him to go against his word."

Then Zeezrom said to the people, "Make sure you remember this! He said there is only one God, yet he says the Son of God will come, but he won't save his people—as if he had authority to command God."
Amulek said to him again, "You have lied, because you are twisting my words. You say I spoke as though I had authority to command God, just because I said he will not save his people in their sins."

"And I tell you again that he cannot save them in their sins, because I cannot deny his word, and he has said that no unclean thing can inherit the kingdom of heaven. So how can you be saved unless you inherit the kingdom of heaven? Therefore, you cannot be saved while remaining in your sins."
Then Zeezrom asked him, "Is the Son of God the very Eternal Father?"
Amulek said to him, "Yes, he is the very Eternal Father of heaven and earth and everything in them. He is the beginning and the end, the first and the last."

"He will come into the world to redeem his people. He will take upon himself the sins of those who believe on his name. These are the ones who will have eternal life, and salvation comes to no one else."

"Therefore the wicked will remain as though no redemption had been made, except for the breaking of the bands of death. For the day is coming when all will rise from the dead and stand before God and be judged according to their works."
"Now, there is a death called a temporal death, and the death of Christ will break the bands of this temporal death, so that all will be raised from this temporal death."

"The spirit and the body will be reunited in their perfect form. Every limb and joint will be restored to its proper shape, just as we are now. And we will be brought to stand before God, knowing just as we know now, and having a clear memory of all our guilt."

"This restoration will come to everyone—old and young, slave and free, male and female, the wicked and the righteous. Not even a hair of their heads will be lost. Everything will be restored to its perfect form, as it is now, and they will be brought before the judgment bar of Christ the Son, God the Father, and the Holy Spirit, who is one Eternal God, to be judged according to their works, whether good or evil."

"Now I have spoken to you about the death of the mortal body and the resurrection of the mortal body. I tell you that this mortal body is raised to an immortal body—from death, from the first death, to life, so that they can never die again. Their spirits will be united with their bodies, never to be separated. The whole person becomes spiritual and immortal, never again to experience decay."
When Amulek had finished these words, the people began to be amazed again, and Zeezrom began to tremble. And so ended the words of Amulek, or at least this is all that has been written.

12.text: Now Alma, seeing that Amulek's words had silenced Zeezrom—because he could see that Amulek had caught Zeezrom in his lying and deception meant to destroy him, and seeing that Zeezrom began to tremble under a awareness of his guilt—opened his mouth and began to speak to him, building on Amulek's words and explaining the scriptures even further than Amulek had done.
The words that Alma spoke to Zeezrom were heard by the people all around, because the crowd was large. He spoke in this way:

"Now Zeezrom, since you have been caught in your lying and craftiness—for you have not only lied to men, but you have lied to God, because he knows all your thoughts, and you can see that your thoughts have been made known to us by his Spirit—"

"And you can see that we know your plan was a very cunning plan, following the craftiness of the devil, designed to lie to and deceive this people so that you could turn them against us, to insult us and cast us out—"
"Now this was a plan of your enemy, and he has used his power through you. I want you to remember that what I say to you, I say to everyone."

"I say to all of you that this was a trap set by the adversary to catch this people, so he could bring you under his control and wrap you in his chains and drag you down to everlasting destruction, according to his power to hold you captive."

When Alma had spoken these words, Zeezrom began to tremble even more, because he was becoming more and more convinced of the power of God. He was also convinced that Alma and Amulek knew about him, because he realized they knew the thoughts and intentions of his heart. They had been given power to know these things through the spirit of prophecy.

Zeezrom began to sincerely ask them questions so he could learn more about the kingdom of God. He said to Alma, "What does Amulek mean about the resurrection of the dead—that all will rise from the dead, both the just and the unjust, and be brought to stand before God to be judged according to their works?"

Alma began to explain these things to him, saying, "Many people are allowed to know the mysteries of God, but they are under strict command to share only the portion of his word that he allows them to give to the children of men, according to how much attention and effort people give to him."

"Therefore, whoever hardens his heart receives a lesser portion of the word. And whoever does not harden his heart receives a greater portion of the word, until he is given to know the mysteries of God in full."

"And those who harden their hearts are given less and less of the word until they know nothing about his mysteries. Then they are taken captive by the devil and led by his will down to destruction. This is what is meant by the chains of hell."

"Amulek has spoken clearly about death and about being raised from this mortal state to a state of immortality, and about being brought before the judgment bar of God to be judged according to our works."
"If our hearts have been hardened—if we have hardened our hearts against the word so that it has not been found in us—then our condition will be terrible, because we will be condemned."

"Our words will condemn us, and all our works will condemn us. We will not be found spotless. Our thoughts will also condemn us. In this awful state, we will not dare to look up at our God, and we would be glad if we could command the rocks and mountains to fall on us and hide us from his presence."

"But this cannot happen. We must come forward and stand before him in his glory, power, might, majesty, and dominion, and acknowledge to our everlasting shame that all his judgments are just—that he is just in all his works, that he is merciful to the children of men, and that he has all power to save everyone who believes on his name and brings forth the fruits of repentance."

"And I tell you that then comes a death, a second death, which is a spiritual death. At that time, whoever dies in his sins as to a temporal death will also die a spiritual death—he will die as to things pertaining to righteousness."

"Then their torments will be like a lake of fire and brimstone, whose flame rises up forever and ever. Then they will be chained down to everlasting destruction, according to the power and captivity of Satan, because he has brought them under his control according to his will."
"Then, I tell you, they will be as though no redemption had been made, because they cannot be redeemed according to God's justice, and they cannot die, since there is no more decay."
When Alma had finished speaking these words, the people began to be even more astonished.

But there was one man named Antionah, who was a chief ruler among them. He came forward and said, "What do you mean when you say that man will rise from the dead and be changed from this mortal to an immortal state so that the soul can never die?"

"What does the scripture mean where it says that God placed cherubim and a flaming sword on the east side of the garden of Eden so that our first parents could not enter and eat the fruit of the tree of life and live forever? This shows that there was no way for them to live forever."

Alma said to him, "This is exactly what I was about to explain. We see that Adam fell by eating the forbidden fruit, according to the word of God, and by his fall, all mankind became a lost and fallen people."

"Now if Adam had been able to eat the fruit of the tree of life at that time, there would have been no death, and God's word would have been meaningless, making God a liar, because he had said, 'If you eat, you will surely die.'"

"We see that death does come upon mankind—the death that Amulek spoke about, which is temporal death. Nevertheless, a period of time was given to mankind in which to repent. Therefore, this life became a probationary state—a time to prepare to meet God, a time to prepare for that endless state that we have spoken about, which comes after the resurrection of the dead."

"If there had been no plan of redemption prepared from the foundation of the world, there could have been no resurrection of the dead. But a plan of redemption was prepared, which brings about the resurrection of the dead that we have spoken about."

"And if our first parents could have gone and eaten from the tree of life, they would have been forever miserable, having no preparatory state. The plan of redemption would have been ruined, and the word of God would have meant nothing."
"But it was not that way. Instead, it was appointed for men to die, and after death they must come to judgment—that same judgment we have spoken about, which is the end."
"After God had appointed that these things should happen, he saw that it was important for people to know about the things he had planned for them."
"Therefore he sent angels to talk with them, and the angels caused people to see his glory."

"From that time forward, people began to call on his name. God talked with men and made known to them the plan of redemption, which had been prepared from the foundation of the world. He made it known to them according to their faith, their repentance, and their holy works."

"So he gave commandments to men, who had first broken the original commandments regarding temporal things and had become like gods, knowing good from evil. They were placed in a position to act according to their own wills and desires, whether to do evil or to do good."

"Therefore God gave them commandments, after making the plan of redemption known to them, that they should not do evil. The penalty for evil was a second death—an everlasting death as to things pertaining to righteousness. The plan of redemption could have no effect on such people, because the demands of justice could not be set aside, according to the supreme goodness of God."

"But God called on men in the name of his Son (this being the plan of redemption that was established), saying, 'If you will repent and not harden your hearts, then I will have mercy on you through my Only Begotten Son.'"
"Therefore, whoever repents and does not harden his heart will have a claim on mercy through my Only Begotten Son, leading to a forgiveness of his sins. These will enter into my rest."
"And whoever hardens his heart and does evil—I swear in my wrath that he will not enter into my rest."

"And now, my brothers, I tell you that if you harden your hearts, you will not enter into the rest of the Lord. Your wickedness provokes him to send his wrath upon you, just as he did in the first provocation—and according to his word, in the last provocation as well as the first—leading to the everlasting destruction of your souls. According to his word, this leads to the last death as well as the first."

"And now, my brothers, since we know these things are true, let us repent and not harden our hearts, so that we do not provoke the Lord our God to bring his wrath upon us because of these second commandments he has given us. Instead, let us enter into the rest of God, which is prepared according to his word."

13.text: "And again, my brothers, I would direct your minds to the time when the Lord God gave these commandments to his children. I want you to remember that the Lord God ordained priests after his holy order, which was after the order of his Son, to teach these things to the people."
"These priests were ordained after the order of his Son in such a way that the people could know how to look forward to his Son for redemption."

"This is the way they were ordained: They were called and prepared from the foundation of the world, according to God's foreknowledge, because of their great faith and good works. In the beginning, they were free to choose good or evil. Because they chose good and exercised great faith, they were called with a holy calling—a calling prepared through a preparatory redemption for people like them."
