"They were called to this holy calling because of their faith, while others rejected the Spirit of God because of the hardness of their hearts and the blindness of their minds. If not for this, they could have had the same opportunity as their brethren."

"In short, they all started on the same level. This holy calling was prepared from the foundation of the world for those who would not harden their hearts, through the atonement of the Only Begotten Son, who was prepared."
"Being called by this holy calling and ordained to the high priesthood of the holy order of God, they were to teach his commandments to the children of men, so they could also enter into his rest."

"This high priesthood was after the order of his Son, which was established from the foundation of the world. In other words, it is without beginning of days or end of years, prepared from eternity to all eternity, according to God's foreknowledge of all things."

"They were ordained in this way—called with a holy calling, ordained with a holy ordinance, and given the high priesthood of the holy order. This calling, ordinance, and high priesthood is without beginning or end."

"In this way they become high priests forever, after the order of the Son, the Only Begotten of the Father, who is without beginning of days or end of years, who is full of grace, fairness, and truth. And so it is. Amen."

"Now as I said about this holy order, or high priesthood, many people were ordained and became high priests of God. This was because of their great faith and repentance and their righteousness before God—they chose to repent and live righteously rather than to perish."
"Therefore they were called after this holy order and were sanctified, and their garments were washed white through the blood of the Lamb."

"After being sanctified by the Holy Ghost and having their garments made white—being pure and spotless before God—they could not look upon sin without feeling deep horror. There were many, a very great number, who were made pure and entered into the rest of the Lord their God."
"And now, my brothers, I want you to humble yourselves before God and bring forth the fruits of repentance, so that you also may enter into that rest."
"Yes, humble yourselves just like the people in the days of Melchizedek, who was also a high priest after this same order I have spoken about and who held the high priesthood forever."
"It was this same Melchizedek to whom Abraham paid tithes. Yes, even our father Abraham paid tithes of one-tenth of everything he owned."

"These ordinances were given in this way so that the people could look forward to the Son of God. They were a symbol of his order—his order itself—so that people could look forward to him for a forgiveness of their sins and enter into the rest of the Lord."
"Now this Melchizedek was a king over the land of Salem. His people had grown very wicked and full of sin. They had all gone astray and were full of all kinds of wickedness."

"But Melchizedek exercised mighty faith and received the office of the high priesthood according to the holy order of God. He preached repentance to his people, and they did repent. Melchizedek established peace in the land during his time. Because of this, he was called the prince of peace, for he was the king of Salem, and he ruled under his father."
"There were many before him and many after him, but none were greater. Because of this, people have made special mention of him."
"I don't need to go over all the details; what I have said should be enough. The scriptures are before you—if you twist them, it will lead to your own destruction."
When Alma had said these words, he stretched out his hand and cried with a powerful voice, saying, "Now is the time to repent, for the day of salvation is drawing near!"

"Yes, the voice of the Lord, through the mouths of angels, proclaims it to all nations. He declares it so that people may have glad tidings of great joy. He spreads these glad tidings among all his people, even to those who are scattered across the face of the earth. And so they have come to us."

"They are made known to us in plain words so that we can understand and cannot make mistakes. Because we are wanderers in a strange land, we are greatly blessed, for these glad tidings are declared to us in all parts of our land."
"For angels are declaring these things to many people in our land right now, and this is to prepare the hearts of the people to receive God's word at the time of his coming in glory."

"And now we only wait to hear the joyful news declared to us by angels about his coming. The time is coming, though we don't know how soon. I wish to God it might be in my lifetime, but whether it is sooner or later, I will rejoice in it."

"It will be made known to righteous and holy men through the mouths of angels at the time of his coming, so that the words of our fathers may be fulfilled, according to what they spoke about him through the spirit of prophecy that was in them."

"And now, my brothers, from the very depths of my heart, with great anxiety, even to the point of pain, I urge you to listen to my words and cast off your sins. Do not put off the day of your repentance."

"Instead, humble yourselves before the Lord and call on his holy name. Watch and pray continually so that you will not be tempted beyond what you can bear, and in this way be led by the Holy Spirit, becoming humble, gentle, submissive, patient, full of love and endurance."
"Have faith in the Lord. Have a hope that you will receive eternal life. Have the love of God always in your hearts, so that you may be lifted up on the last day and enter into his rest."
"And may the Lord grant you repentance, so that you do not bring his wrath upon yourselves, and so that you are not bound by the chains of hell, and so that you do not suffer the second death."
Alma spoke many more words to the people, which are not written in this book.

14.text: After Alma had finished speaking, many of the people believed his words and began to repent and search the scriptures.

But the majority of them wanted to destroy Alma and Amulek. They were angry with Alma because of how plainly he had spoken to Zeezrom. They also said that Amulek had lied to them and had spoken against their law and their lawyers and judges.
They were also angry with Alma and Amulek because they had testified so plainly against their wickedness, and they tried to get rid of them secretly.
But they did not succeed. Instead, they seized them, bound them with strong ropes, and brought them before the chief judge of the land.

The people came forward and accused them, testifying that they had spoken against the law and their lawyers and judges and all the people of the land. They also said that Alma and Amulek had testified that there was only one God, and that he would send his Son among the people, but that he would not save them. The people made many such accusations against Alma and Amulek before the chief judge of the land.

Zeezrom was astonished at the words that had been spoken, and he recognized the blindness he had caused among the people through his lies. His soul began to be tormented by the awareness of his own guilt. He began to feel the pains of hell closing in around him.

He began to cry out to the people, saying, "I am the one who is guilty, and these men are blameless before God." He began to plead for them from that time forward, but they attacked him, saying, "Are you possessed by the devil too?" They spit on him and threw him out, along with everyone who believed in the words spoken by Alma and Amulek. They cast them out and sent men to throw stones at them.

They gathered the wives and children of those who believed, and whoever had believed or been taught to believe in the word of God, they had thrown into the fire. They also brought out their records that contained the holy scriptures and threw them into the fire too, to be burned and destroyed.
They took Alma and Amulek and brought them to the place of martyrdom, so that they could witness the destruction of those being consumed by fire.

When Amulek saw the suffering of the women and children burning in the fire, he was deeply pained. He said to Alma, "How can we watch this terrible scene? Let us stretch out our hands and use the power of God that is in us to save them from the flames."

But Alma said to him, "The Spirit holds me back from stretching out my hand, because the Lord is receiving these people up to himself in glory. He allows this to happen so that the people who do this may act according to the hardness of their hearts, and so that the judgments he will bring upon them in his wrath will be just. The blood of these innocent people will stand as a witness against the wicked and cry out against them on the last day."
Amulek said to Alma, "Maybe they will burn us too."
Alma said, "Let it be according to the will of the Lord. But our work is not yet finished, so they will not burn us."

When the bodies of those who had been thrown into the fire were consumed, along with the records that were thrown in with them, the chief judge of the land came and stood before Alma and Amulek as they were bound. He slapped them on their cheeks and said, "After what you have seen, will you preach to this people again that they will be cast into a lake of fire and brimstone?"

"You see that you did not have power to save those who were thrown into the fire. God didn't save them either, because they believed as you do." The judge slapped them again on their cheeks and asked, "What do you have to say for yourselves?"
Now this judge followed the beliefs and practices of Nehor, who had killed Gideon.
Alma and Amulek did not answer him. He hit them again and handed them over to officers to be thrown into prison.

After they had been in prison for three days, many lawyers, judges, priests, and teachers who followed Nehor came to the prison to see them and question them with many words. But Alma and Amulek answered them nothing.
The judge stood before them and said, "Why won't you answer these people? Don't you know that I have the power to hand you over to the flames?" He commanded them to speak, but they answered nothing.

They left but came again the next day, and the judge again slapped them on their cheeks. Many others also came and struck them, saying, "Will you stand and judge this people again and condemn our law? If you have such great power, why don't you free yourselves?"
They said many things like this, grinding their teeth at them and spitting on them, and saying, "How will we look when we are damned?"

They said many more things like this, and they mocked them for many days. They kept food from them so they would starve, and water so they would thirst. They also took away their clothes so that they were naked. They were bound with strong ropes and kept in prison.

After they had suffered for many days (it was the twelfth day of the tenth month in the tenth year of the reign of the judges), the chief judge over the land of Ammonihah and many of their teachers and lawyers came to the prison where Alma and Amulek were bound with ropes.

The chief judge stood before them, struck them again, and said, "If you have the power of God, free yourselves from these ropes, and then we will believe that the Lord will destroy this people as you have said."

They all came forward and struck them, repeating the same words, one after another, until the last one had spoken. When the last one had spoken, the power of God came upon Alma and Amulek, and they rose and stood on their feet.

Alma cried out, "How long must we endure these great afflictions, O Lord? O Lord, give us strength according to our faith in Christ, even to the point of deliverance." Then they broke the ropes that bound them. When the people saw this, they began to flee, because the fear of destruction had come upon them.

Their fear was so great that they fell to the ground and could not reach the outer door of the prison. The earth shook powerfully, and the walls of the prison split in two and fell to the ground. The chief judge, the lawyers, the priests, and the teachers who had struck Alma and Amulek were killed when the walls fell on them.

Alma and Amulek came out of the prison unharmed, for the Lord had given them power according to their faith in Christ. They came straight out of the prison, free from their bonds. The prison had fallen to the earth, and every person inside it was killed—except for Alma and Amulek. They immediately went into the city.

When the people heard the great noise, they came running together in crowds to find out what had caused it. When they saw Alma and Amulek coming out of the prison and the walls lying on the ground, they were struck with great fear and fled from Alma and Amulek like a mother goat running with her young from two lions. And so they fled from the presence of Alma and Amulek.

15.text: Alma and Amulek were commanded to leave that city. They departed and came to the land of Sidom, where they found all the people who had left Ammonihah—those who had been thrown out and stoned because they believed in Alma's words.
These people told Alma and Amulek everything that had happened to their wives and children, and also about themselves and about the power that had delivered them.

Zeezrom was also in Sidom, lying sick with a burning fever. This fever was caused by the terrible anguish of his mind over his wickedness. He thought Alma and Amulek were dead and believed they had been killed because of his wrongdoing. This great sin and his many other sins tormented his mind until it became unbearably painful, with no relief. He was burning with an intense heat.
When Zeezrom heard that Alma and Amulek were in the land of Sidom, his heart began to take courage. He immediately sent a message to them, asking them to come to him.

They went immediately, obeying his request. They went into the house where Zeezrom was and found him on his bed, very sick with a burning fever. His mind was also in great pain because of his sins. When he saw them, he stretched out his hand and begged them to heal him.
Alma took him by the hand and said, "Do you believe in the power of Christ to save?"
Zeezrom answered, "Yes, I believe everything you have taught."
Alma said, "If you believe in the redemption of Christ, you can be healed."
Zeezrom said, "Yes, I believe according to your words."
Then Alma cried out to the Lord, saying, "O Lord our God, have mercy on this man and heal him according to his faith in Christ."
When Alma had said these words, Zeezrom leaped to his feet and began to walk. This was done to the great amazement of all the people, and news of it spread throughout the entire land of Sidom.
Alma baptized Zeezrom, and from that time on, Zeezrom began to preach to the people.
Alma established a church in the land of Sidom and set apart priests and teachers to baptize everyone who wanted to be baptized.
There were many who came, because people flocked in from all the surrounding area of Sidom and were baptized.

But the people in the land of Ammonihah remained hard-hearted and stubborn. They did not repent of their sins and attributed all the power of Alma and Amulek to the devil. They followed the teachings of Nehor and did not believe in repentance from their sins.

Alma and Amulek—Amulek having given up all his gold, silver, and precious things in the land of Ammonihah for the word of God, and having been rejected by those who were once his friends and also by his father and his relatives—

After Alma had established the church at Sidom, he saw a great change—the people were humbled in the pride of their hearts and began to humble themselves before God. They began to gather at their places of worship to pray before the altar, watching and praying continually so they could be delivered from Satan, death, and destruction.
After seeing all these things, Alma took Amulek to the land of Zarahemla and brought him to his own house. There he cared for Amulek in his hardships and strengthened him in the Lord.
And so ended the tenth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

16.text: In the eleventh year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, on the fifth day of the second month—there having been much peace in the land of Zarahemla with no wars or conflicts for a number of years—a cry of war was heard throughout the land.
The armies of the Lamanites had come through the wilderness and into the borders of the land, even into the city of Ammonihah, and began to kill the people and destroy the city.

Before the Nephites could raise a large enough army to drive them out, the Lamanites had destroyed the people in the city of Ammonihah and some of those near the borders of Noah, and had taken others captive into the wilderness.
The Nephites wanted to rescue those who had been taken captive into the wilderness.

The chief captain over the Nephite armies was a man named Zoram, and he had two sons, Lehi and Aha. Zoram and his two sons knew that Alma was the high priest over the church, and they had heard that he had the spirit of prophecy. So they went to him and asked where the Lord wanted them to go in the wilderness to search for their people who had been taken captive by the Lamanites.

Alma asked the Lord about the matter, then returned and told them, "The Lamanites will cross the river Sidon in the south wilderness, far beyond the borders of the land of Manti. You will meet them on the east side of the river Sidon, and there the Lord will deliver your people who have been taken captive by the Lamanites."
Zoram and his sons crossed the river Sidon with their armies and marched beyond the borders of Manti into the south wilderness on the east side of the river Sidon.

They attacked the Lamanite armies, and the Lamanites were scattered and driven into the wilderness. They rescued their people who had been taken captive—not a single one had been lost. They brought them back to their own lands.

And so ended the eleventh year of the judges. The Lamanites had been driven out of the land, and the people of Ammonihah were destroyed. Every living person in Ammonihah was killed, along with their great city, which they had said God could not destroy because of its greatness.
But in a single day it was left desolate, and the dead bodies were torn apart by dogs and wild animals of the wilderness.

After many days, their dead bodies were heaped up on the ground and covered with only a thin layer of earth. The smell was so terrible that people would not go to settle in the land of Ammonihah for many years. It was called the Desolation of Nehors, because the people who had been killed followed the teachings of Nehor. Their lands remained empty and abandoned.
The Lamanites did not come to war against the Nephites again until the fourteenth year of the reign of the judges. So for three years the Nephites had continual peace throughout the land.
Alma and Amulek went throughout the land preaching repentance to the people in their temples, sanctuaries, and synagogues, which were built in the style of the Jews.
They shared the word of God with everyone who would listen, without showing favoritism to anyone.

Alma and Amulek went out, along with many others who had been chosen for the work, to preach the word throughout the whole land. The church became established throughout the entire land among all the Nephite people.

There was no inequality among them. The Lord poured out his Spirit on all the land to prepare the minds and hearts of the people to receive the word that would be taught among them at the time of Christ's coming.

This was so they would not be hardened against the word, and so they would not be unbelieving and head toward destruction, but instead would receive the word with joy, and like a branch be grafted into the true vine, so they could enter into the rest of the Lord their God.

The priests who went out among the people preached against all lying, deception, envy, conflict, hatred, insults, theft, robbery, plundering, murder, adultery, and all forms of immoral behavior, declaring that these things should not exist.
They spoke of things that would soon happen—the coming of the Son of God, his suffering and death, and the resurrection of the dead.
Many of the people asked about the place where the Son of God would come. They were taught that he would appear to them after his resurrection, and the people heard this with great joy and gladness.

After the church had been established throughout all the land—having gained victory over the devil, the word of God being preached in its purity throughout the land, and the Lord pouring out his blessings on the people—the fourteenth year of the reign of the judges came to an end.

17.text: As Alma was traveling from the land of Gideon southward toward the land of Manti, to his astonishment he met the sons of Mosiah, who were traveling toward the land of Zarahemla.

These sons of Mosiah had been with Alma when the angel first appeared to him, so Alma was overjoyed to see his brothers. What added even more to his joy was that they were still his brothers in the Lord. They had grown strong in the knowledge of the truth, because they were men of good understanding who had studied the scriptures carefully to learn the word of God.

But that was not all. They had devoted themselves to much prayer and fasting, and because of this they had the spirit of prophecy and the spirit of revelation. When they taught, they taught with the power and authority of God.

They had been teaching the word of God among the Lamanites for fourteen years and had great success in bringing many people to a knowledge of the truth. Through the power of their words, many were brought before the altar of God to call on his name and confess their sins.

Now these are the experiences they had during their travels, for they went through many hardships. They suffered greatly, both physically and mentally, including hunger, thirst, and exhaustion, as well as much spiritual effort.

This is the account of their journeys: They left their father, Mosiah, in the first year of the judges. They had turned down the kingdom their father wanted to give them, which the people also wanted them to have.
Nevertheless, they left the land of Zarahemla and took their swords, spears, bows, arrows, and slings so they could provide food for themselves while traveling through the wilderness.
With the group they had chosen, they departed into the wilderness to go up to the land of Nephi to preach the word of God to the Lamanites.

They traveled many days in the wilderness and fasted and prayed often, asking the Lord to give them a portion of his Spirit to go with them and stay with them, so they could be an instrument in God's hands to bring their brethren, the Lamanites, if possible, to a knowledge of the truth—to show them that the traditions of their fathers were not correct.
The Lord visited them with his Spirit and said to them, "Be comforted." And they were comforted.

The Lord also said to them, "Go out among the Lamanites, your brethren, and establish my word. Be patient through long-suffering and hardship, so you can set a good example for them in me, and I will make you an instrument in my hands for the salvation of many souls."
The hearts of the sons of Mosiah and those who were with them took courage to go to the Lamanites and declare the word of God to them.

When they arrived at the borders of the Lamanite lands, they separated from one another and went their own ways, trusting in the Lord that they would meet again at the end of their harvest season. They knew the work they had taken on was great.

And it truly was great, because they had undertaken to preach the word of God to a wild, hardened, and fierce people—a people who loved to murder the Nephites and to rob and plunder them. Their hearts were set on riches—gold, silver, and precious stones—but they tried to get these things by murdering and plundering so they wouldn't have to work for them.

They were a very lazy people. Many of them worshipped idols, and God's curse had fallen on them because of their fathers' false traditions. Even so, the Lord's promises were extended to them on the condition that they repent.
This is why the sons of Mosiah had undertaken this work—hoping they might bring the Lamanites to repentance and help them learn about the plan of redemption.
So they separated from one another and went among the Lamanites, each man alone, according to the word and power of God given to him.

Ammon was the leader among them. He blessed them according to their various assignments, shared the word of God with them, and ministered to them before they parted ways. Then they each went on their separate journeys throughout the land.
Ammon went to the land of Ishmael, which was named after the sons of Ishmael, who had also become Lamanites.

When Ammon entered the land of Ishmael, the Lamanites captured him and tied him up, as was their custom with all Nephites who fell into their hands. They brought their captives before the king, who could choose to kill them, keep them as prisoners, throw them in jail, or banish them from his land—whatever he wanted.
So Ammon was brought before the king of the land of Ishmael, whose name was Lamoni. He was a descendant of Ishmael.
The king asked Ammon if he wanted to live in the land among the Lamanites, among his people.
Ammon said to him, "Yes, I want to live among this people for a while—perhaps even until the day I die."
King Lamoni was very pleased with Ammon and had his ropes removed. He wanted Ammon to marry one of his daughters.
But Ammon said, "No, but I will be your servant." So Ammon became a servant to king Lamoni and was assigned to work with other servants watching Lamoni's flocks, as was the Lamanite custom.

After he had been serving the king for three days, he went with the other servants to take their flocks to the watering place called the water of Sebus, where all the Lamanites brought their flocks to drink.

As Ammon and the king's servants were driving their flocks to this watering place, a group of Lamanites who had already been there with their flocks came and scattered the flocks of Ammon and the king's servants. They scattered them so badly that the animals fled in every direction.

The king's servants began to complain, saying, "Now the king will kill us just like he killed our brothers, because their flocks were scattered by these wicked men." They began to cry bitterly, saying, "Our flocks are already scattered."

They wept because they were afraid of being killed. When Ammon saw this, his heart swelled with joy, because he thought, "I will show my power to these fellow servants—the power that is in me—by gathering these flocks back for the king, so that I may win their hearts and lead them to believe in my words."
These were Ammon's thoughts when he saw the distress of those he considered to be his brothers.

He encouraged them with his words, saying, "My brothers, cheer up! Let's go find the flocks. We will gather them together and bring them back to the watering place. That way we will save the flocks for the king, and he will not kill us."
They went to search for the flocks and followed Ammon. They rushed forward quickly and headed off the king's flocks, gathering them back together at the watering place.
The same men came again to scatter the flocks, but Ammon said to his brothers, "Surround the flocks so they don't run away. I will go and deal with these men who keep scattering our flocks."
They did as Ammon told them. He went and stood to face the men who stood by the waters of Sebus, and there were quite a few of them.

They were not afraid of Ammon because they thought any one of them could easily kill him. They didn't know that the Lord had promised Mosiah he would protect his sons, and they knew nothing about the Lord. They took pleasure in destroying their Nephite brothers, which is why they stood there to scatter the king's flocks.

Ammon stepped forward and began slinging stones at them with mighty power. He killed several of them, which astonished them. But they were angry because of their fallen brothers and were determined to take him down. Since they couldn't hit him with their stones, they came at him with clubs to kill him.

But every man who raised his club to strike Ammon had his arm cut off by Ammon's sword. Ammon blocked their blows by striking their arms with the edge of his sword. They became so astonished that they began to flee before him. There were many of them, but he drove them back by the strength of his arm.
Six of them had fallen from his sling, but he killed only their leader with his sword. He cut off as many arms as were raised against him, and there were quite a few.

After he had driven them far away, he returned, and they watered their flocks and brought them back to the king's pasture. Then the servants went to the king, carrying the arms that had been cut off by Ammon's sword—the arms of those who had tried to kill him. These were brought to the king as proof of what had happened.

18.text: King Lamoni had his servants come forward and testify about everything they had seen.

After they had all testified about what they had seen—and the king had learned about Ammon's faithfulness in protecting his flocks and his great power in fighting those who tried to kill him—he was extremely astonished. He said, "Surely this is more than a man. Isn't this the Great Spirit who sends such great punishments upon this people because of their murders?"

The servants answered the king, "Whether he is the Great Spirit or just a man, we don't know. But we do know that he cannot be killed by the king's enemies, and they cannot scatter the king's flocks when he is with us, because of his skill and great strength. We know he is a friend to the king. O king, we don't believe that any man has such great power, because we know he cannot be killed."

When the king heard these words, he said, "Now I know it is the Great Spirit. He has come down at this time to protect your lives so that I wouldn't kill you like I killed your brothers before. This is the Great Spirit our fathers have spoken about."

This was the tradition Lamoni had received from his father—that there was a Great Spirit. Even though they believed in a Great Spirit, they thought that whatever they did was right. Nevertheless, Lamoni began to fear greatly that he had done wrong by killing his servants.
He had killed many of them because their brothers had scattered the flocks at the watering place, and because the flocks had been scattered, they were put to death.

It was the practice of these Lamanites to stand by the waters of Sebus to scatter other people's flocks so they could drive the scattered animals to their own land. This was their custom of plundering.
King Lamoni asked his servants, "Where is this man who has such great power?"

They told him, "He is feeding your horses." Before the trip to water the flocks, the king had commanded his servants to prepare his horses and chariots to take him to the land of Nephi, where his father, who was king over all the land, had arranged a great feast.

When king Lamoni heard that Ammon was preparing his horses and chariots, he was even more amazed at Ammon's faithfulness. He said, "There has never been a servant among all my servants who has been as faithful as this man. He even remembers all my commands and carries them out."
"Now I am sure this is the Great Spirit. I would like him to come in and speak with me, but I don't dare ask him."
When Ammon had finished preparing the horses and chariots for the king and his servants, he went in to the king. He noticed that the king's expression had changed, so he was about to leave.
One of the king's servants said to Ammon, "Rabbanah"—which means "powerful" or "great king," because they considered their kings to be powerful—and said, "Rabbanah, the king wants you to stay."
So Ammon turned to the king and said, "What would you like me to do for you, O king?" But the king did not answer him for about an hour, because he didn't know what to say.
Ammon asked him again, "What do you want from me?" But the king still did not answer.

Ammon, being filled with the Spirit of God, was able to perceive the king's thoughts. He said to the king, "Is it because you heard that I defended your servants and your flocks—that I killed seven of the attackers with my sling and sword and cut off the arms of others to protect your flocks and servants? Is this what amazes you so much?"
"Tell me, why are you so amazed? I am just a man, and I am your servant. Whatever you want that is right, I will do it."

When the king heard these words, he was amazed again, because he realized that Ammon could read his thoughts. Even so, king Lamoni opened his mouth and said, "Who are you? Are you that Great Spirit who knows all things?"
Ammon answered, "I am not."
The king said, "How do you know the thoughts of my heart? Speak freely and tell me about these things. Also tell me by what power you killed and cut off the arms of the men who scattered my flocks."

"If you will tell me about these things, I will give you whatever you ask for. If you needed it, I would even have my armies protect you. But I know you are more powerful than all of them. Whatever you want from me, I will give it to you."
Ammon, being wise yet harmless, said to Lamoni, "Will you listen to my words if I tell you by what power I do these things? This is all I ask of you."
The king answered, "Yes, I will believe everything you say." And in this way he was won over.
Ammon began to speak to him boldly and said, "Do you believe there is a God?"
The king answered, "I don't know what that means."
Then Ammon asked, "Do you believe there is a Great Spirit?"
The king said, "Yes."
Ammon said, "This is God." Then Ammon asked again, "Do you believe that this Great Spirit, who is God, created all things in heaven and earth?"
The king said, "Yes, I believe he created all things on the earth, but I don't know about the heavens."
Ammon said to him, "The heavens are the place where God lives with all his holy angels."
King Lamoni asked, "Is it above the earth?"
Ammon said, "Yes, and he looks down upon all the children of men. He knows all the thoughts and intentions of the heart, because by his hand they were all created from the beginning."
King Lamoni said, "I believe all these things you have told me. Are you sent from God?"

Ammon said, "I am a man. In the beginning, man was created in the image of God. I have been called by his Holy Spirit to teach these things to this people, so they may come to know what is right and true."
"A portion of that Spirit lives in me and gives me knowledge and power according to my faith and desires in God."

When Ammon had said these words, he started from the creation of the world, including the creation of Adam, and told the king all about the fall of man. He went over the records and holy scriptures of the people that had been spoken by the prophets, all the way down to the time when their father Lehi left Jerusalem.
He also told them (for it was the king and his servants) about all the journeys of their fathers in the wilderness and all their suffering from hunger, thirst, and hardship.

He also told them about the rebellions of Laman and Lemuel and the sons of Ishmael—all their rebellions. He explained all the records and scriptures from the time Lehi left Jerusalem down to the present time.

But that was not all. He taught them about the plan of redemption, which was prepared from the foundation of the world. He also told them about the coming of Christ and made known all the works of the Lord.
After Ammon had said all these things and explained them to the king, the king believed everything he said.
The king began to cry out to the Lord, saying, "O Lord, have mercy! Just as you have shown your great mercy to the people of Nephi, please show mercy to me and my people."
When he had said this, he fell to the ground as if he were dead.

His servants took him and carried him to his wife and laid him on a bed. He lay there as if he were dead for two days and two nights. His wife, sons, and daughters mourned over him in the Lamanite custom, greatly grieving his loss.

19.text: After two days and two nights, they were about to take his body and place it in a tomb they had prepared for burying their dead.
The queen had heard about Ammon's reputation, so she sent for him and asked him to come to her.
Ammon did as he was asked and went to the queen to find out what she wanted him to do.
She said to him, "My husband's servants have told me that you are a prophet of a holy God and that you have the power to do many mighty works in his name."

"If this is the case, I want you to go in and see my husband, because he has been lying on his bed for two days and two nights. Some say he is not dead, but others say he is dead and that his body smells and should be placed in the tomb. But to me, he does not smell."

Now Ammon knew that king Lamoni was under the power of God. He knew that the dark veil of unbelief was being removed from the king's mind, and the light that was filling his mind was the light of the glory of God—a marvelous light of his goodness. This light had filled his soul with such joy that the cloud of darkness had been chased away, and the light of everlasting life had been lit in his soul. Ammon knew this had overwhelmed the king's physical body, and that he had been carried away in God.
So what the queen wanted was exactly what Ammon wanted too. He went in to see the king as the queen had asked, and he saw the king and knew that he was not dead.
He said to the queen, "He is not dead. He is sleeping in God, and tomorrow he will rise again. Do not bury him."
Ammon asked her, "Do you believe this?" She said, "I have no proof other than your word and the word of our servants, but I believe it will happen just as you have said."
Ammon said to her, "Blessed are you because of your great faith. I tell you, woman, there has never been such great faith among all the people of the Nephites."
She watched over her husband's bed from that time until the next day at the time Ammon had said the king would rise.
The king arose, just as Ammon had said. As he got up, he stretched out his hand to the woman and said, "Blessed be the name of God, and blessed are you."

"As surely as you live, I have seen my Redeemer. He will come forth and be born of a woman, and he will redeem all mankind who believe on his name." When he had said these words, his heart overflowed with joy and he sank down again. The queen was also overcome by the Spirit.

When Ammon saw the Spirit of the Lord being poured out according to his prayers upon the Lamanites—his brethren, who had been the cause of so much sorrow among the Nephites and all the people of God because of their wickedness and their traditions—he fell to his knees and began to pour out his soul in prayer and thanksgiving to God for what he had done for his brethren. He too was overcome with joy. And so all three of them had sunk to the ground.

When the king's servants saw that they had all fallen, they also began to cry out to God, because the fear of the Lord had come upon them too. These were the same servants who had stood before the king and testified about Ammon's great power.

They called on the name of the Lord with all their strength until they had all fallen to the ground—all except one Lamanite woman named Abish, who had been converted to the Lord for many years because of a remarkable vision her father had.

Abish had been converted to the Lord but had never told anyone. When she saw that all the servants of Lamoni, the queen, the king, and Ammon had all fallen to the ground, she knew it was the power of God. Thinking this was her chance to make it known to the people, so that by seeing this they would believe in the power of God, she ran from house to house, telling everyone what had happened.

The people began to gather at the house of the king. A large crowd came, and to their astonishment, they saw the king, the queen, and their servants lying on the ground as if they were dead. They also saw Ammon and recognized that he was a Nephite.
The people began to argue among themselves. Some said this was a great evil that had come upon the king and his household because he had allowed the Nephite to stay in the land.
But others said the king had brought this upon his own house because he had killed his servants whose flocks had been scattered at the waters of Sebus.

They were also challenged by the men who had stood at the waters of Sebus and scattered the king's flocks. Those men were angry with Ammon because he had killed several of their brothers at the waters of Sebus while defending the king's flocks.
One of them, whose brother had been killed by Ammon's sword, was extremely angry. He drew his sword and went to kill Ammon, but as he raised the sword to strike him, he fell dead.
Now we see that Ammon could not be killed, because the Lord had said to his father Mosiah, "I will spare him, and it will be according to your faith." Therefore Mosiah trusted him to the Lord.

When the crowd saw that the man who had raised his sword to kill Ammon had fallen dead, fear came upon all of them. They didn't dare touch Ammon or any of those who had fallen, and they began to wonder among themselves what could be the cause of this great power and what it all meant.
Many of them said that Ammon was the Great Spirit, while others said he had been sent by the Great Spirit.
But others argued against all of these ideas, saying he was a monster sent by the Nephites to torment them.

Some said Ammon had been sent by the Great Spirit to punish them for their sins. They said the Great Spirit had always been with the Nephites and had always delivered them. They said it was this Great Spirit who had destroyed so many of their Lamanite brothers.

The argument became very heated among them. While they were arguing, the woman servant who had gathered the crowd came. When she saw the fighting among the people, she was deeply saddened, even to the point of tears.

She went and took the queen by the hand, hoping she might raise her from the ground. As soon as she touched the queen's hand, the queen arose and stood on her feet and cried out with a loud voice, "O blessed Jesus, who has saved me from an awful hell! O blessed God, have mercy on this people!"
When she had said this, she clasped her hands together, filled with joy, and spoke many words that were not understood. Then she took king Lamoni by the hand, and he arose and stood on his feet.

Immediately seeing the arguing among his people, the king went out and began to correct them and teach them the words he had heard from Ammon. Everyone who heard his words believed and was converted to the Lord.
But there were many who would not listen to his words, so they went their way.

When Ammon got up, he also ministered to them, as did all the servants of Lamoni. They all told the people the same thing—that their hearts had been changed and they no longer had any desire to do evil.
Many of them told the people that they had seen angels and had talked with them. The angels had told them things about God and his righteousness.
Many believed their words, and those who believed were baptized. They became a righteous people and established a church among them.
And so the work of the Lord began among the Lamanites. The Lord began to pour out his Spirit upon them, and we see that his arm reaches out to all people who will repent and believe on his name.

20.text: After they had established a church in that land, king Lamoni wanted Ammon to go with him to the land of Nephi so he could introduce Ammon to his father.

But the voice of the Lord came to Ammon, saying, "Do not go to the land of Nephi, because the king there will try to kill you. Instead, go to the land of Middoni, because your brother Aaron, along with Muloki and Ammah, are in prison there."
When Ammon heard this, he said to Lamoni, "My brother and brethren are in prison at Middoni, and I must go to free them."

Lamoni said to Ammon, "I know that with the Lord's strength, you can do anything. But I will go with you to the land of Middoni, because the king of that land, whose name is Antiomno, is my friend. I will go and persuade the king of that land to release your brothers from prison." Then Lamoni asked, "Who told you that your brothers were in prison?"
Ammon said, "No one told me except God. He said to me, 'Go and rescue your brothers, because they are in prison in the land of Middoni.'"
When Lamoni heard this, he had his servants prepare his horses and chariots.
He said to Ammon, "Come, I will go with you to the land of Middoni, and I will ask the king to release your brothers from prison."
As Ammon and Lamoni were traveling there, they met the father of Lamoni, who was king over all the land.
Lamoni's father said to him, "Why didn't you come to the feast on that great day when I held a feast for my sons and my people?"
He also said, "Where are you going with this Nephite, who is a child of a liar?"
Lamoni explained where he was going, because he was afraid of offending his father.
He also told his father the reason he had stayed in his own kingdom and had not come to the feast.

After Lamoni had told him all these things, his father became angry with him to Lamoni's surprise. His father said, "Lamoni, you are going to free these Nephites, who are sons of a liar. Their ancestor robbed our fathers, and now his children have come among us to deceive us with their cunning and lies so they can rob us of our property again."
Lamoni's father commanded him to kill Ammon with the sword. He also ordered Lamoni not to go to Middoni but to return with him to the land of Ishmael.

But Lamoni said, "I will not kill Ammon, and I will not go back to the land of Ishmael. I am going to the land of Middoni to free Ammon's brothers, because I know they are righteous men and holy prophets of the true God."
When his father heard these words, he was angry with Lamoni and drew his sword to strike him down.

But Ammon stepped forward and said, "You shall not kill your son. Nevertheless, it would be better for him to fall than for you to fall, because he has repented of his sins. But if you were to fall now in your anger, your soul could not be saved."

"And also, you should hold back, because if you kill your son, since he is an innocent man, his blood would cry from the ground to the Lord his God for vengeance against you, and you might lose your soul."
When Ammon had said these words, the king answered, "I know that if I kill my son, I would be shedding innocent blood—because it is you who have tried to destroy him."
The king reached out to kill Ammon, but Ammon blocked his blows and struck his arm so that the king could not use it.
When the king saw that Ammon could kill him, he began to beg Ammon to spare his life.
But Ammon raised his sword and said, "I will strike you unless you agree to let my brothers be released from prison."
The king, fearing for his life, said, "If you will spare me, I will give you whatever you ask, even up to half of my kingdom."

When Ammon saw that he had influenced the old king as he wanted, he said, "If you will release my brothers from prison and allow Lamoni to keep his kingdom without being angry with him—and let him do whatever he thinks is right—then I will spare you. Otherwise, I will strike you to the ground."
When Ammon said these words, the king began to rejoice because his life would be spared.

When the king saw that Ammon had no desire to destroy him, and when he also saw the great love Ammon had for his son Lamoni, he was extremely amazed. He said, "Because all you have asked for is the release of your brothers and that my son Lamoni may keep his kingdom, I will grant that my son may keep his kingdom from now on and forever, and I will no longer rule over him."

"I will also grant that your brothers be released from prison, and you and your brothers may come to me in my kingdom, because I very much want to see you again." The king was deeply astonished by Ammon's words and by the words his son Lamoni had spoken, and he wanted to learn more from them.
Ammon and Lamoni continued on their journey toward the land of Middoni. Lamoni found favor with the king of that land, and Ammon's brothers were brought out of prison.

When Ammon met his brothers, he was deeply sorrowful, because they were naked and their skin was badly worn from being tied with strong ropes. They had also suffered hunger, thirst, and all kinds of hardships. But they had been patient through all their suffering.

As it happened, they had ended up among a harder and more stubborn people who would not listen to their words. They had thrown them out, beaten them, and chased them from house to house and from place to place until they reached the land of Middoni. There they were captured, thrown into prison, tied with strong ropes, and kept in prison for many days until they were freed by Lamoni and Ammon.

21.text: Now when Ammon and his brothers separated and went to the borders of the land of the Lamanites, Aaron traveled toward the land that the Lamanites called Jerusalem, named after the land where their ancestors were born. It was near the borders of the land of Mormon.
The Lamanites, the Amalekites, and the people of Amulon had built a great city called Jerusalem.

The Lamanites themselves were already hard-hearted, but the Amalekites and the Amulonites were even harder. They influenced the Lamanites to harden their hearts and grow stronger in wickedness and sinful practices.

Aaron came to the city of Jerusalem and first began to preach to the Amalekites. He preached to them in their synagogues, which they had built following the teachings of Nehor, since many of the Amalekites and the Amulonites followed the order of the Nehors.

As Aaron entered one of their synagogues to preach to the people and was speaking to them, an Amalekite stood up and began to argue with him, saying, "What have you been testifying about? Have you seen an angel? Why don't angels appear to us? Aren't our people just as good as your people?"

"You also say that unless we repent, we will perish. How do you know what we think and intend in our hearts? How do you know we need to repent? How do you know we aren't a righteous people? We have built places of worship, and we gather together to worship God. We believe that God will save everyone."
Aaron said to him, "Do you believe that the Son of God will come to save people from their sins?"

The man said to him, "We don't believe you know anything like that. We don't believe in these foolish traditions. We don't believe you can know things about the future, and we don't believe that your ancestors or our ancestors knew anything about what they said was coming."

Then Aaron began to teach them from the scriptures about the coming of Christ and about the resurrection of the dead, and that there could be no redemption for mankind except through the death, sufferings, and atoning blood of Christ.
But as he began to explain these things to them, they became angry with him and began to mock him, and they refused to listen to his words.

So when Aaron saw that they would not listen to him, he left their synagogue and went to a village called Ani-Anti. There he found Muloki preaching the word to the people, along with Ammah and his brothers. They were debating with many people about the word of God.
When they saw that the people were hardening their hearts, they left and went to the land of Middoni. They preached the word to many, but only a few believed what they taught.
Nevertheless, Aaron and some of his brothers were captured and thrown into prison, and the rest of them escaped from the land of Middoni to the surrounding regions.
Those who were thrown into prison suffered greatly, but they were set free by Lamoni and Ammon, and were given food and clothing.
They went out again to preach the word. This was the first time they were delivered from prison, and this is what they had suffered.
They went wherever the Spirit of the Lord led them, preaching the word of God in every synagogue of the Amalekites and in every gathering of the Lamanites where they were allowed to enter.
The Lord began to bless them so much that they brought many people to the knowledge of the truth. They convinced many of their sins and of the incorrect traditions of their fathers.
Ammon and Lamoni returned from the land of Middoni to the land of Ishmael, which was the land of their inheritance.
King Lamoni would not allow Ammon to serve him or be his servant.
Instead, he had synagogues built in the land of Ishmael, and he had his people—those under his rule—gather together.

He rejoiced over them and taught them many things. He declared to them that they were his people and that they were a free people, free from the control of his father, the king. His father had given him the right to rule over the people in the land of Ishmael and in all the surrounding land.
He also declared that they could worship the Lord their God however they desired, in whatever place they were, as long as it was within the land under King Lamoni's rule.

And Ammon preached to the people of King Lamoni. He taught them all things concerning righteousness and encouraged them daily with great effort. They listened carefully to his words and were eager to keep the commandments of God.

22.text: Now, while Ammon was teaching the people of Lamoni continually, we will return to the story of Aaron and his brothers. After Aaron left the land of Middoni, the Spirit led him to the land of Nephi, to the house of the king who ruled over all the land except the land of Ishmael. This king was the father of Lamoni.
Aaron went into the king's palace with his brothers, bowed before the king, and said, "O king, we are the brothers of Ammon, whom you set free from prison."

"Now, O king, if you will spare our lives, we will be your servants." The king said to them, "Stand up, for I will let you live, and I will not make you my servants. But I insist that you teach me, because I have been troubled in my mind by the generosity and the powerful words of your brother Ammon. I want to know why he did not come up from Middoni with you."
Aaron said to the king, "The Spirit of the Lord has called him to go a different way. He has gone to the land of Ishmael to teach the people of Lamoni."
The king said to them, "What do you mean by the Spirit of the Lord? This is the thing that troubles me."
"And also, what did Ammon mean when he said, 'If you will repent you will be saved, and if you will not repent, you will be cast off at the last day'?"

Aaron answered him, "Do you believe there is a God?" The king said, "I know that the Amalekites say there is a God, and I have allowed them to build places of worship so they can gather to worship him. If you say there is a God, I will believe."
When Aaron heard this, his heart began to rejoice, and he said, "As surely as you live, O king, there is a God."
The king asked, "Is God that Great Spirit who brought our ancestors out of the land of Jerusalem?"
Aaron said to him, "Yes, he is that Great Spirit, and he created all things both in heaven and on earth. Do you believe this?"
The king said, "Yes, I believe that the Great Spirit created all things, and I want you to tell me about all these things, and I will believe your words."

When Aaron saw that the king was willing to believe his words, he began teaching from the creation of Adam, reading the scriptures to the king—how God created man in his own image, how God gave him commandments, and how because of disobedience, man had fallen.

Aaron explained the scriptures to him from the creation of Adam, laying out the fall of man and their sinful nature, and also the plan of redemption, which was prepared from the foundation of the world through Christ for all who would believe on his name.

Since man had fallen, he could not earn anything by himself. But the sufferings and death of Christ atone for sins through faith and repentance. Christ breaks the chains of death so that the grave has no victory, and the sting of death is swallowed up in the hope of glory. Aaron explained all these things to the king.

After Aaron had explained these things, the king said, "What must I do to have the eternal life you have spoken of? What must I do to be born of God, to have this wicked spirit torn from my heart and receive his Spirit, so I can be filled with joy and not be cast off at the last day? I will give up everything I own—I will even give up my kingdom to receive this great joy."

But Aaron said to him, "If you desire this, if you will bow down before God, if you will repent of all your sins and bow down before God and call on his name in faith, believing that you will receive, then you will receive the hope you desire."
When Aaron had said these words, the king bowed down before the Lord on his knees. He even lay face down on the ground and cried out with great intensity, saying:

"O God, Aaron has told me that there is a God. If there is a God, and if you are God, will you make yourself known to me? I will give away all my sins to know you, so that I may be raised from the dead and be saved at the last day." When the king had said these words, he fell down as if he were dead.

His servants ran and told the queen everything that had happened to the king. She came to him, and when she saw him lying as if he were dead, and Aaron and his brothers standing there as though they had caused his collapse, she was furious with them. She commanded her servants—the servants of the king—to seize them and kill them.

But the servants had seen what caused the king to fall, so they did not dare to touch Aaron and his brothers. They pleaded with the queen, saying, "Why do you command us to kill these men? One of them is more powerful than all of us! We would fall before them."

When the queen saw how afraid the servants were, she also became very frightened that something terrible might happen to her. She commanded her servants to go call the people so they could come and kill Aaron and his brothers.

When Aaron saw the queen's determination, and knowing how hard-hearted the people were, he feared that a large crowd would gather and a violent conflict would break out. So he reached out his hand and raised the king from the ground, saying, "Stand up." The king stood on his feet and regained his strength.

This happened in the presence of the queen and many servants. When they saw it, they were amazed and began to be afraid. The king stood up and began to teach them. He taught them so effectively that his entire household was converted to the Lord.
Now a crowd had gathered because of the queen's command, and there was a great deal of complaining among them because of Aaron and his brothers.
But the king stood among them and spoke to them, and they were calmed toward Aaron and those who were with him.
When the king saw that the people were calmed, he had Aaron and his brothers stand in the middle of the crowd and preach the word to them.

The king sent a proclamation throughout all his land, to all his people in every region. The land stretched to the sea on the east and on the west, and was separated from the land of Zarahemla by a narrow strip of wilderness that ran from the east sea to the west sea. It extended around the borders of the seashore and the wilderness on the north by the land of Zarahemla, through the borders of Manti, along the head of the river Sidon, running from the east toward the west. This is how the Lamanites and the Nephites were divided.

The less industrious Lamanites lived in the wilderness and dwelt in tents. They were scattered throughout the wilderness on the west, in the land of Nephi, and also on the west of the land of Zarahemla along the seashore, and on the west in the land of Nephi where their ancestors first settled, extending along the seashore.

There were also many Lamanites on the east by the seashore, where the Nephites had pushed them. The Nephites were nearly surrounded by the Lamanites. Nevertheless, the Nephites had taken possession of all the northern parts of the land bordering on the wilderness at the head of the river Sidon, from east to west, around the wilderness side to the north, all the way to the land they called Bountiful.

Bountiful bordered the land they called Desolation, which was so far north that it reached the land that had once been populated and then destroyed—the people whose bones we have mentioned—which was discovered by the people of Zarahemla and was the place where they first landed.

From there they had come up into the south wilderness. The land to the north was called Desolation, and the land to the south was called Bountiful, because it was a wilderness filled with all kinds of wild animals, some of which had come from the land to the north for food.

It was only about a day and a half's journey for a Nephite across the line between Bountiful and Desolation, from the east to the west sea. The land of Nephi and the land of Zarahemla were nearly surrounded by water, with only a small neck of land between the land to the north and the land to the south.

The Nephites had settled the land of Bountiful from the east all the way to the west sea. In their wisdom, using their guards and their armies, the Nephites had hemmed in the Lamanites to the south so they could have no possessions to the north and could not overrun the land to the north.

So the Lamanites could only have possessions in the land of Nephi and the surrounding wilderness. This was wise on the part of the Nephites—since the Lamanites were their enemies, they did not want to be attacked from every direction and wanted to have a place where they could flee if needed.
And now, after saying this, I return again to the account of Ammon, Aaron, Omner, Himni, and their brothers.

23.text: The king of the Lamanites sent a proclamation among all his people that they should not harm Ammon, Aaron, Omner, Himni, or any of their brothers who went out preaching the word of God, in whatever part of the land they might be.

He sent a decree that the people should not capture them, throw them in prison, spit on them, hit them, throw them out of their synagogues, whip them, or throw stones at them. Instead, they should have free access to their houses, temples, and places of worship.

This way they could go and preach the word as they desired. The king had been converted to the Lord, along with all his household. So he sent this proclamation throughout the land so the word of God could spread freely, that his people might learn the truth about the wicked traditions of their fathers, realize they were all brothers, and understand that they should not murder, plunder, steal, commit adultery, or commit any kind of wickedness.

When the king sent this proclamation, Aaron and his brothers went from city to city, from one house of worship to another, establishing churches and setting apart priests and teachers throughout the land among the Lamanites to preach and teach the word of God. They began to have great success.

Thousands were brought to the knowledge of the Lord. Thousands came to believe in the traditions of the Nephites, and they were taught the records and prophecies that had been passed down to the present time.

As surely as the Lord lives, every one of the Lamanites who believed in the preaching of Ammon and his brothers and was converted to the Lord—through the spirit of revelation and prophecy and the power of God working miracles among them—never fell away.
They became a righteous people. They laid down the weapons of their rebellion and no longer fought against God or against any of their fellow people.
Now these are the people who were converted to the Lord:
The Lamanites who were in the land of Ishmael;
And also the Lamanites who were in the land of Middoni;
And also the Lamanites who were in the city of Nephi;
And also the Lamanites who were in the land of Shilom, in the land of Shemlon, in the city of Lemuel, and in the city of Shimnilom.

These are the names of the Lamanite cities whose people were converted to the Lord. These are the people who laid down the weapons of their rebellion—all their weapons of war. They were all Lamanites.

The Amalekites were not converted, except for one person. None of the Amulonites were converted either. Instead, they hardened their hearts and also hardened the hearts of the Lamanites in the parts of the land where they lived, including all their villages and all their cities.
Therefore, we have listed all the Lamanite cities where the people repented, came to the knowledge of the truth, and were converted.

The king and those who were converted wanted a special name so they could be distinguished from the rest of their people. So the king consulted with Aaron and many of their priests about what name they should take.
They chose to call themselves Anti-Nephi-Lehies. They were known by this name and were no longer called Lamanites.
They became a very hardworking people. They were friendly with the Nephites and began communicating with them, and the curse of God no longer followed them.

24.text: The Amalekites, the Amulonites, and the Lamanites who were in the land of Amulon, the land of Helam, the land of Jerusalem, and all the surrounding areas—those who had not been converted and had not taken the name of Anti-Nephi-Lehi—were stirred up by the Amalekites and Amulonites to anger against their brothers.

Their hatred became extremely intense against them, so much that they began to rebel against their king and refused to have him rule over them. They took up weapons against the people of Anti-Nephi-Lehi.
The king gave the kingdom to his son and named him Anti-Nephi-Lehi.
The king died in that same year that the Lamanites began to prepare for war against the people of God.

When Ammon and his brothers and all who had come with him saw the Lamanites preparing to destroy their fellow believers, they went to the land of Midian. There Ammon met all his brothers, and from there they traveled to the land of Ishmael to hold a council with Lamoni and his brother Anti-Nephi-Lehi about what they should do to defend themselves against the Lamanites.

Not a single person among all those who had been converted to the Lord would take up weapons against their brothers. They would not even make any preparations for war, and their king commanded them not to.

These are the words the king said to the people about this matter: "I thank my God, my beloved people, that our great God in his goodness has sent these our brothers, the Nephites, to preach to us and convince us of the wicked traditions of our fathers."
"And I thank my great God that he has given us a portion of his Spirit to soften our hearts, so that we have opened communication with these brothers, the Nephites."
"And I also thank my God that by opening this communication, we have been made aware of our sins and of the many murders we have committed."

"I also thank my God—my great God—that he has allowed us to repent of these things and has forgiven us of our many sins and murders, and has removed the guilt from our hearts through the merits of his Son."

"And now, my brothers, since it has been all that we could do—being the most lost of all mankind—to repent of all our sins and the many murders we have committed, and to get God to remove them from our hearts (for it was all we could do to repent enough before God that he would take away our stain)—"
"Now, my dearest brothers, since God has taken away our stains, and our swords have become bright and clean, let us not stain our swords again with the blood of our brothers."

"I say to you, no—let us keep our swords unstained with the blood of our brothers. For perhaps if we should stain our swords again, they could no longer be washed clean through the blood of the Son of our great God, which will be shed for the atonement of our sins."

"The great God has had mercy on us and made these things known to us so we would not perish. He has made these things known to us ahead of time because he loves our souls as much as he loves our children. In his mercy he visits us through his angels so the plan of salvation can be made known to us as well as to future generations."

"Oh, how merciful is our God! And now, since it has been as much as we could do to get our stains removed and our swords made bright, let us hide them away so they may be kept bright as a testimony to our God at the last day—the day when we will stand before him to be judged—that we have not stained our swords with the blood of our brothers since he taught us his word and made us clean."

"And now, my brothers, if our brothers come to destroy us, we will hide our swords—we will bury them deep in the earth so they may be kept bright as a testimony that we never used them. At the last day, if our brothers destroy us, we will go to our God and be saved."
When the king finished speaking and all the people were gathered together, they took their swords and all their weapons that had been used for shedding blood and buried them deep in the earth.

They did this as a testimony to God and to men that they would never again use weapons to shed blood. They made a promise and covenant with God that rather than shed the blood of their brothers, they would give up their own lives; rather than take from a brother, they would give to him; and rather than spend their days in laziness, they would work hard with their hands.

And so we see that when these Lamanites came to believe and know the truth, they were firm and would suffer death rather than commit sin. We see that they buried their weapons of war for the sake of peace.
Their unconverted brothers, the Lamanites, prepared for war and came to the land of Nephi to destroy the king, replace him with someone else, and wipe out the people of Anti-Nephi-Lehi from the land.

When the people saw the Lamanites coming against them, they went out to meet them, lay face down on the ground, and began to call on the name of the Lord. They were in this position of prayer when the Lamanites began to attack them and kill them with the sword.
Without meeting any resistance, they killed one thousand and five of them. And we know these people are blessed, for they have gone to dwell with their God.
When the Lamanites saw that their brothers would not run from the sword, would not turn aside to the right or the left, but would lie down and die while praising God even as they were being killed—
When the Lamanites saw this, they stopped killing them. Many of their hearts swelled with sorrow for those of their brothers who had fallen under the sword, and they repented of what they had done.

They threw down their own weapons of war and refused to pick them up again, because they were stricken with guilt for the murders they had committed. They lay down just as their brothers had, relying on the mercy of those whose arms were raised to kill them.
That day, more people joined the people of God than the number who had been killed. Those who had been killed were righteous people, so we have no reason to doubt that they were saved.

Not a single wicked person was killed among them. But more than a thousand were brought to the knowledge of the truth. So we see that the Lord works in many ways to bring about the salvation of his people.
The largest number of those Lamanites who killed so many of their brothers were Amalekites and Amulonites, most of whom followed the order of the Nehors.
Among those who joined the people of the Lord, none were Amalekites or Amulonites or followers of the order of Nehor. They were all actual descendants of Laman and Lemuel.

And so we can clearly see that after a people have been enlightened by the Spirit of God and have had great knowledge of things pertaining to righteousness, and then have fallen away into sin and transgression, they become more hardened than before. Their condition becomes worse than if they had never known these things.

25.text: Now those Lamanites were even more angry because they had killed their own brothers. So they swore revenge against the Nephites, and they did not try to kill the people of Anti-Nephi-Lehi again at that time.
Instead, they took their armies and crossed into the borders of the land of Zarahemla. They attacked the people in the land of Ammonihah and destroyed them.
After that, they had many battles with the Nephites, in which they were driven back and killed.
Among the Lamanites who were killed were almost all the descendants of Amulon and his brothers, who had been the priests of Noah. They were killed by the Nephites.

The remaining descendants of Amulon fled into the eastern wilderness. Having taken power and authority over the Lamanites there, they caused many of the Lamanites to be put to death by fire because of their beliefs.

Many of those Lamanites, after suffering much loss and many hardships, began to remember the words that Aaron and his brothers had preached to them in their land. They began to question the traditions of their fathers and to believe in the Lord, and they saw that he gave great power to the Nephites. Many of them were converted in the wilderness.
The rulers who were the descendants of the children of Amulon had those believers put to death—all who believed in these things.

This persecution stirred up many of their brothers to anger, and there began to be conflict in the wilderness. The Lamanites began to hunt down the descendants of Amulon and his brothers and began to kill them, and those descendants fled into the eastern wilderness.
They are still being hunted by the Lamanites to this day. In this way, the words of Abinadi came true—what he said about the descendants of the priests who caused him to suffer death by fire.
For Abinadi had said to them, "What you do to me will be an example of things to come."
Abinadi was the first to suffer death by fire because of his belief in God. What he meant was that many would suffer death by fire, just as he had suffered.

He said to the priests of Noah that their descendants would cause many to be put to death in the same way he was, and that they would be scattered and killed, just as a sheep without a shepherd is chased and killed by wild animals. And these words came true, for they were chased by the Lamanites, hunted down, and killed.

When the Lamanites saw that they could not overpower the Nephites, they returned to their own land. Many of them came to live in the land of Ishmael and the land of Nephi and joined the people of God, who were the people of Anti-Nephi-Lehi.

They also buried their weapons of war, just as their converted brothers had done, and they began to be a righteous people. They walked in the ways of the Lord and were careful to keep his commandments and his statutes.

They kept the law of Moses, because it was important for them to keep it since it had not yet been completely fulfilled. But even though they kept the law of Moses, they looked forward to the coming of Christ, believing that the law of Moses was an example pointing to his coming. They believed they must keep those outward practices until the time that Christ would be revealed to them.

They did not think that salvation came through the law of Moses. Rather, the law of Moses strengthened their faith in Christ. In this way they held on to hope through faith, looking toward eternal salvation, relying on the spirit of prophecy which spoke of things to come.

And Ammon, Aaron, Omner, Himni, and their brothers rejoiced greatly because of the success they had among the Lamanites. They saw that the Lord had answered their prayers and had fulfilled his word to them in every detail.

26.text: Now these are the words of Ammon to his brothers: "My brothers, I say to you, how great a reason we have to rejoice! Could we have imagined when we left the land of Zarahemla that God would grant us such great blessings?"
"And now I ask, what great blessings has he given us? Can you tell?"

"I will answer for you: our brothers, the Lamanites, were in darkness—in the deepest darkness—but look at how many of them have been brought to see the marvelous light of God! This is the blessing given to us—that we have been instruments in the hands of God to bring about this great work."
"Thousands of them are rejoicing and have been brought into the fold of God."

"The field was ripe for harvest, and you are blessed because you thrust in the sickle and reaped with all your might. All day long you worked, and look at the number of your sheaves! They will be gathered into the barns so they are not wasted."

"They will not be beaten down by the storm at the last day. They will not be torn apart by the whirlwinds. When the storm comes, they will be gathered together in their place, where the storm cannot reach them. They will not be driven by fierce winds wherever the enemy wants to carry them."
"They are in the hands of the Lord of the harvest, and they are his. He will raise them up at the last day."
"Blessed be the name of our God! Let us sing his praise. Let us give thanks to his holy name, for he does righteous works forever."

"For if we had not come from the land of Zarahemla, these dearly beloved brothers of ours, who have loved us so deeply, would still have been filled with hatred against us, and they would have been strangers to God."
When Ammon had said these words, his brother Aaron corrected him, saying, "Ammon, I'm afraid your joy is leading you into boasting."
But Ammon said, "I am not boasting in my own strength or in my own wisdom. My joy is full—my heart is overflowing with joy—and I will rejoice in my God."

"I know that I am nothing. In my own strength I am weak. So I will not boast about myself, but I will boast about my God. In his strength I can do all things. We have done many mighty miracles in this land, for which we will praise his name forever."

"How many thousands of our brothers has he freed from the pains of hell! They have been brought to sing of redeeming love, and this is because of the power of his word which is in us. Do we not have great reason to rejoice?"
"Yes, we have reason to praise him forever, for he is the Most High God and has freed our brothers from the chains of hell."

"They were surrounded by everlasting darkness and destruction, but he has brought them into his everlasting light—into everlasting salvation. They are surrounded by the matchless abundance of his love. And we have been instruments in his hands in doing this great and marvelous work."

"So let us glory—let us glory in the Lord! Let us rejoice, for our joy is full! Let us praise our God forever. Who can glory too much in the Lord? Who can say too much about his great power, his mercy, and his patience toward the children of men? I cannot express even the smallest part of what I feel."
"Who would have thought that our God would be so merciful as to rescue us from our terrible, sinful, and polluted state?"
"We went out in anger, with fierce threats to destroy his church."
"Then why didn't he sentence us to an awful destruction? Why didn't he let the sword of his justice fall on us and doom us to eternal despair?"

"Oh, my soul nearly flees at the thought! He did not carry out his justice upon us, but in his great mercy has brought us across that everlasting gulf of death and misery, to the salvation of our souls."
