Now my son, I want you to repent and abandon your sins. Stop chasing after the desires of your eyes, and exercise self-control in all these things. For unless you do this, you cannot inherit the kingdom of God. Oh, remember this, take it seriously, and exercise self-control in these things.
And I command you to seek counsel from your older brothers in your decisions. You are young, and you need to be guided by your brothers. Pay attention to their advice.

Do not allow yourself to be led astray by any empty or foolish thing. Do not let the devil lead your heart after those wicked harlots again. Look, my son, at how much damage you brought upon the Zoramites, for when they saw your behavior, they would not believe my words.

And now the Spirit of the Lord says to me: Command your children to do good, so they do not lead the hearts of many people to destruction. Therefore I command you, my son, in the fear of God, to turn away from your sins.
Turn to the Lord with all your mind, might, and strength. Do not lead anyone else's heart to do wickedly. Instead, go back to them, acknowledge your faults, and make right the wrongs you have done.
Do not seek after riches or the worthless things of this world, for you cannot take them with you.

And now, my son, I want to tell you something about the coming of Christ. He is the one who will surely come to take away the sins of the world. He comes to bring the good news of salvation to his people.

And now, my son, this was the ministry you were called to—to declare this good news to this people, to prepare their minds. Or rather, so that salvation might come to them and they might prepare the minds of their children to hear the word when Christ comes.

And now I will put your mind at ease on this subject. You wonder why these things should be made known so long beforehand. I ask you: is not a soul at this time just as precious to God as a soul will be at the time of Christ's coming?
Is it not just as necessary that the plan of redemption be made known to this people as it will be to their children?
Is it not just as easy right now for the Lord to send his angel to declare this good news to us as it will be to our children, or as it will be after the time of his coming?

40.text: Now my son, there is something more I want to say to you, because I can see that your mind is troubled about the resurrection of the dead.
Listen, I tell you that there is no resurrection—or in other words, this mortal body does not become immortal, and this decaying body does not become incorruptible—until after the coming of Christ.

He is the one who brings about the resurrection of the dead. But my son, the resurrection has not yet happened. Now, I will share with you a mystery. There are many mysteries that no one knows except God himself. But I will show you one thing that I have asked God about earnestly so that I could understand it—and that is about the resurrection.
There is an appointed time when all people will come back from the dead. No one knows when this time will come, but God knows the time that has been set.

Now, whether there will be one time, a second time, or a third time that people will come back from the dead doesn't really matter, because God knows all these things. It is enough for me to know that there is an appointed time when all will rise from the dead.
There must be a period of time between the moment of death and the time of the resurrection.
And now I want to ask: what happens to the souls of people from the time of death to the appointed time for the resurrection?

Whether there is more than one appointed time for people to rise doesn't matter, because not everyone dies at the same time, and that doesn't matter either. Everything is like one day to God, and time is only measured by people.

So there is an appointed time for people to rise from the dead, and there is a period between the time of death and the resurrection. As for what happens to the souls of people during this time—that is the very thing I have asked the Lord earnestly to understand, and this is what I know.
And when the time comes for all to rise, then they will know that God knows every appointed time set for each person.

Now, about the state of the soul between death and the resurrection—an angel has told me that the spirits of all people, as soon as they leave this mortal body—yes, the spirits of all people, whether they are good or evil—are taken home to that God who gave them life.

And then the spirits of those who are righteous are received into a state of happiness called paradise—a state of rest, a state of peace, where they will rest from all their troubles, from all worry and sorrow.

And then the spirits of the wicked—yes, those who are evil—because they have no part of the Spirit of the Lord, since they chose evil deeds rather than good, and so the spirit of the devil entered into them and took control of them—these will be cast out into outer darkness. There will be weeping, wailing, and grinding of teeth, and this is because of their own wickedness, having been led captive by the will of the devil.

Now this is the state of the souls of the wicked—in darkness, in an awful, fearful state of waiting for the burning anger of God's wrath to come upon them. They remain in this state, just as the righteous remain in paradise, until the time of their resurrection.

Now, some people have understood that this state of happiness and this state of misery of the soul, before the resurrection, was a first resurrection. Yes, I admit it could be called a resurrection—the raising of the spirit or the soul and their being assigned to happiness or misery, according to what has been taught.
And it has also been taught that there is a first resurrection—a resurrection of all those who have lived, who are living, or who will live, up to the resurrection of Christ from the dead.
Now, we should not think that this first resurrection, spoken of in this way, refers to the raising of souls and their assignment to happiness or misery. You should not think that is what it means.
I tell you, no—it means the reuniting of the soul with the body, for those from the days of Adam down to the resurrection of Christ.

Now, whether the souls and the bodies of those who have been spoken of will all be reunited at the same time—the wicked as well as the righteous—I do not say. Let it be enough that I say they all come forth; or in other words, their resurrection happens before the resurrection of those who die after the resurrection of Christ.

Now, my son, I am not saying that their resurrection happens at the exact moment of Christ's resurrection. But it is my belief that the souls and bodies of the righteous are reunited at the resurrection of Christ and his ascension into heaven.

But whether it happens at his resurrection or after, I do not say. But this much I do say: there is a period between death and the resurrection of the body, and the soul is in a state of happiness or misery until the time God has appointed for the dead to come forth and be reunited—both soul and body—and be brought to stand before God and be judged according to their works.
Yes, this brings about the restoration that the prophets have spoken of.

The soul will be restored to the body, and the body to the soul. Yes, every limb and joint will be restored to its body. Even a hair of the head will not be lost, but all things will be restored to their proper and perfect form.
And now, my son, this is the restoration that the prophets have spoken about—
And then the righteous will shine brightly in the kingdom of God.

But an awful death comes upon the wicked, for they die to all things of righteousness. Because they are unclean, and nothing unclean can inherit the kingdom of God, they are cast out and left to receive the results of their labors and their works, which have been evil. They drink the very last drops of a bitter cup.

41.text: And now, my son, I have something to say about the restoration that has been talked about, because some people have twisted the scriptures and have gone far off course because of this teaching. And I can see that your mind has been troubled about this too. But I will explain it to you.

I tell you, my son, that the plan of restoration is required by the justice of God, because it is necessary that all things be restored to their proper order. It is required and just, by the power and resurrection of Christ, that the soul of every person be restored to its body, and that every part of the body be restored to itself.

And the justice of God requires that people be judged according to their works. If their works were good in this life, and the desires of their hearts were good, then at the last day they will also be restored to that which is good.

And if their works are evil, they will have evil restored to them. Therefore, all things will be restored to their proper order, everything to its natural state—mortality raised to immortality, corruption to incorruption—raised to endless happiness to inherit the kingdom of God, or to endless misery to inherit the kingdom of the devil, one on one side and the other on the other—

One person raised to happiness because of their desire for happiness, or to good because of their desire for good; and the other raised to evil because of their desire for evil. For just as a person has desired to do evil all day long, so will they receive their reward of evil when the night comes.
And it works the same way on the other side. If a person has repented of their sins and desired righteousness until the end of their days, they will be rewarded with righteousness.

These are the ones who are redeemed by the Lord. Yes, these are the ones who are rescued and delivered from that endless night of darkness. And so they stand or fall, because they are their own judges—whether to do good or do evil.
Now, the decrees of God cannot be changed. Therefore, the way has been prepared so that whoever chooses to may walk in it and be saved.
And now, my son, do not risk committing even one more offense against your God on those points of doctrine that you have already used as an excuse to sin.
Do not suppose, because restoration has been taught, that you will be restored from sin to happiness. I tell you, wickedness never was happiness.

And now, my son, all people who are in their natural state—or I should say, in a carnal state—are trapped in bitterness and bound by sin. They are without God in the world, and they have gone against the nature of God. Therefore, they are in a state that is opposite to the nature of happiness.
And now, does the word restoration mean to take something from its natural state and place it in an unnatural state, or to place it in a state opposite to its nature?

Oh, my son, that is not what it means at all. The meaning of restoration is to bring back evil for evil, or carnal for carnal, or devilish for devilish—and good for that which is good, righteous for that which is righteous, just for that which is just, and merciful for that which is merciful.

Therefore, my son, make sure you are merciful to others. Deal justly, judge righteously, and do good continually. If you do all these things, then you will receive your reward—yes, you will have mercy restored to you again, justice restored to you again, a righteous judgment restored to you again, and good rewarded to you again.
For whatever you send out will return to you again and be restored. Therefore, the word restoration more fully condemns the sinner and does not justify them at all.

42.text: And now, my son, I can see that there is something else troubling your mind that you cannot understand—and that is about the justice of God in punishing the sinner. You are trying to suppose that it is unfair for the sinner to be sent to a state of misery.

Now, my son, I will explain this to you. After the Lord God sent our first parents out of the Garden of Eden to work the ground from which they were taken—yes, he drove the man out and placed at the east end of the Garden of Eden cherubim and a flaming sword that turned every direction to guard the tree of life—

Now, we see that the man had become like God, knowing good and evil. And to prevent him from reaching out and also taking from the tree of life and eating and living forever, the Lord God placed the cherubim and the flaming sword so he could not eat of the fruit—
And so we see that people were given time to repent—yes, a probationary time, a time to repent and serve God.

Because if Adam had immediately reached out and eaten from the tree of life, he would have lived forever, according to the word of God, with no opportunity for repentance. And the word of God would have been made meaningless, and the great plan of salvation would have been ruined.

But it was appointed for people to die—therefore, just as they were cut off from the tree of life, they were cut off from the face of the earth—and people became lost forever. Yes, they became fallen.
And now you see that our first parents were cut off both physically and spiritually from the presence of the Lord. And so we see that they became free to follow their own desires.
Now, it would not have been right for people to be rescued from this physical death, because that would destroy the great plan of happiness.

Therefore, since the soul can never die, and the fall had brought upon all people a spiritual death as well as a physical death—meaning they were cut off from the presence of the Lord—it was necessary for people to be rescued from this spiritual death.
Therefore, since they had become carnal, sensual, and devilish by nature, this probationary state became a time for them to prepare. It became a preparatory state.
And now remember, my son, if it were not for the plan of redemption—setting it aside—as soon as people died, their souls would be miserable, being cut off from the presence of the Lord.
And there was no way to rescue people from this fallen state, which they had brought upon themselves because of their own disobedience.

Therefore, according to justice, the plan of redemption could only be carried out on the condition that people repent during this probationary state—yes, this preparatory state. Because without these conditions, mercy could not take effect without destroying the work of justice. And the work of justice could not be destroyed, because if it were, God would cease to be God.
And so we see that all people had fallen and were in the grasp of justice—yes, the justice of God, which sentenced them to be forever cut off from his presence.

And now, the plan of mercy could not be carried out unless an atonement was made. Therefore God himself atones for the sins of the world, to bring about the plan of mercy, to satisfy the demands of justice, so that God might be a perfect, just God, and also a merciful God.

Now, repentance could not come to people unless there was a punishment—a punishment as eternal as the life of the soul—set opposite to the plan of happiness, which was also as eternal as the life of the soul.
Now, how could a person repent unless they had sinned? How could they sin if there was no law? How could there be a law unless there was a punishment?
Now, a punishment was established, and a just law was given, which brought feelings of guilt and remorse to people.
Now, if no law had been given—if a man murdered, should he die?—would he be afraid of dying if he committed murder?
And also, if no law had been given against sin, people would not be afraid to sin.
And if no law had been given, and people sinned, what could justice do, or mercy either? They would have no claim on the person.

But a law has been given, and a punishment established, and the opportunity to repent has been granted. Mercy claims those who repent; otherwise, justice claims the person and carries out the law, and the law brings the punishment. If this were not so, the works of justice would be destroyed, and God would cease to be God.

But God does not cease to be God, and mercy claims the repentant. Mercy comes because of the atonement, and the atonement brings about the resurrection of the dead, and the resurrection of the dead brings people back into the presence of God. And so they are restored into his presence to be judged according to their works, according to the law and justice.
For justice exercises all its demands, and mercy also claims everything that belongs to it. And so, only the truly repentant are saved.
What—do you think that mercy can rob justice? I tell you, no, not in the slightest. If it could, God would cease to be God.

And so God brings about his great and eternal purposes, which were prepared from the foundation of the world. And so the salvation and redemption of people comes about, and also their destruction and misery.

Therefore, my son, whoever wants to come may come and drink freely of the waters of life. And whoever will not come is not forced to come. But in the last day, it will be restored to them according to their deeds.
If a person has desired to do evil and has not repented during their lifetime, then evil will be done to them, according to the restoration of God.
And now, my son, I want you to stop letting these things trouble you. Only let your sins trouble you—with the kind of trouble that will lead you to repentance.

Oh my son, I want you to stop denying the justice of God. Do not try to excuse yourself even in the smallest way because of your sins by denying the justice of God. Instead, let the justice of God, and his mercy, and his patience have full power in your heart, and let it bring you down to the dust in humility.

And now, my son, you are called by God to preach the word to this people. And now, my son, go on your way. Declare the word with truth and sincerity, so that you may bring souls to repentance, that the great plan of mercy may have a claim on them. And may God bless you according to my words. Amen.

43.text: And now the sons of Alma went out among the people to preach the word to them. And Alma himself could not rest either, so he also went out.

Now we will say nothing more about their preaching, except that they preached the word and the truth according to the spirit of prophecy and revelation, and they preached according to the holy order of God by which they were called.
And now I return to an account of the wars between the Nephites and the Lamanites in the eighteenth year of the reign of the judges.

The Zoramites had become Lamanites, and so at the beginning of the eighteenth year, the Nephites saw that the Lamanites were coming against them. So they made preparations for war and gathered their armies together in the land of Jershon.
The Lamanites came with their thousands and entered the land of Antionum, which is the land of the Zoramites. A man named Zerahemnah was their leader.
Now, because the Amalekites were more wicked and bloodthirsty than the Lamanites themselves, Zerahemnah appointed chief captains over the Lamanites, and they were all Amalekites and Zoramites.
He did this to keep their hatred toward the Nephites strong, so that he could bring the Nephites under his control and accomplish his goals.
His goal was to stir up the Lamanites to anger against the Nephites. He did this so he could seize great power over them and also gain power over the Nephites by bringing them into slavery.

The goal of the Nephites was to protect their lands, their homes, their wives, and their children—to keep them safe from their enemies. They also wanted to preserve their rights and privileges and their liberty, so they could worship God as they chose.
Because they knew that if they fell into the hands of the Lamanites, the Lamanites would destroy anyone who worshipped God in spirit and truth—the true and living God.

They also knew how intensely the Lamanites hated their brothers, the people of Anti-Nephi-Lehi, who were called the people of Ammon. These people would not take up weapons—they had made a covenant and would not break it—so if they fell into the hands of the Lamanites, they would be destroyed.
And the Nephites would not allow them to be destroyed. So they gave them lands as their inheritance.

And the people of Ammon gave the Nephites a large portion of their possessions to support their armies. And so the Nephites had to stand alone against the Lamanites, who were made up of the descendants of Laman and Lemuel and the sons of Ishmael, along with all those who had left the Nephites—the Amalekites, Zoramites, and the descendants of the priests of Noah.
Now those who had left were nearly as numerous as the Nephites, and so the Nephites were forced to fight against their own brothers, even to the shedding of blood.
As the Lamanite armies gathered together in the land of Antionum, the Nephite armies were ready to meet them in the land of Jershon.
Now, the leader of the Nephites—the man who had been appointed chief captain over the Nephites—the chief captain took command of all the Nephite armies—and his name was Moroni.
Moroni took full command and leadership of their wars. He was only twenty-five years old when he was appointed chief captain over the Nephite armies.
He met the Lamanites at the borders of Jershon, and his people were armed with swords, curved blades, and all kinds of weapons of war.
When the Lamanite armies saw that Moroni had prepared his people with breastplates, arm guards, and also helmets to protect their heads, and that they also wore thick clothing—

Now the army of Zerahemnah had none of these things. They only had their swords, curved blades, bows, arrows, stones, and slings. They wore nothing but a skin tied around their waists. All of them were unarmored except for the Zoramites and the Amalekites—
But even they did not have breastplates or shields. So they were extremely afraid of the Nephite armies because of their armor, even though their own numbers were much greater than the Nephites.

Because of this, they did not dare attack the Nephites at the borders of Jershon. Instead, they left the land of Antionum and traveled through the wilderness, going around by the head of the river Sidon, so they could enter the land of Manti and take it over. They did not think that Moroni's armies would know where they had gone.

But as soon as they left for the wilderness, Moroni sent spies into the wilderness to watch their camp. And Moroni, knowing about the prophecies of Alma, also sent messengers to Alma, asking him to inquire of the Lord about where the Nephite armies should go to defend themselves against the Lamanites.

The word of the Lord came to Alma, and Alma told Moroni's messengers that the Lamanite armies were marching through the wilderness to come into the land of Manti, where they planned to attack the weaker part of the people. The messengers went and delivered this message to Moroni.
Moroni left part of his army in the land of Jershon in case some of the Lamanites came into that land to take the city. He took the rest of his army and marched to the land of Manti.

He ordered all the people in that part of the land to gather together to fight against the Lamanites, to defend their lands and their country, their rights and their freedoms. So they were prepared by the time the Lamanites arrived.
Moroni had his army hide in the valley near the bank of the river Sidon, on the west side of the river in the wilderness.
And Moroni placed spies all around so he would know when the Lamanite camp was approaching.
Now, Moroni knew the Lamanites' plan—that they intended to destroy their brothers or force them into slavery so they could set up a kingdom for themselves over all the land.

He also knew that the Nephites only wanted to preserve their lands, their liberty, and their church. So he felt it was no sin to defend them through strategy. He used his spies to learn which route the Lamanites would take.
So he divided his army and brought part of them into the valley, hiding them on the east and south sides of the hill Riplah.
The rest of his army he hid in the west valley, on the west side of the river Sidon, down toward the borders of the land of Manti.
Having positioned his army exactly as he wanted, he was ready to meet them.
The Lamanites came up on the north side of the hill, where part of Moroni's army was hidden.

As the Lamanites passed the hill Riplah and came into the valley and began to cross the river Sidon, the army hidden on the south side of the hill—led by a man named Lehi—came out and surrounded the Lamanites from behind on the east.
When the Lamanites saw the Nephites coming at them from behind, they turned around and began fighting the army of Lehi.

The killing began on both sides, but it was far worse for the Lamanites, because their exposed skin was open to the heavy blows of the Nephites' swords and curved blades, which brought death with almost every strike.

On the other hand, only an occasional Nephite fell by the Lamanites' swords and from blood loss, because their vital body parts were protected from the Lamanites' strikes by their breastplates, arm guards, and helmets. And so the Nephites continued their deadly work among the Lamanites.
The Lamanites became terrified because of the great destruction happening to them, and they began to flee toward the river Sidon.
Lehi and his men chased them and drove them into the waters of Sidon, and the Lamanites crossed the river. Lehi kept his armies on the bank of the river to prevent them from crossing back.
Moroni and his army met the Lamanites in the valley on the other side of the river Sidon and began attacking and killing them.
The Lamanites fled again, this time toward the land of Manti, but they were met once more by Moroni's armies.
Now the Lamanites fought with incredible ferocity. Never in all their history had the Lamanites been known to fight with such extraordinary strength and courage.

They were driven on by the Zoramites and the Amalekites, who were their chief captains and leaders, and by Zerahemnah, who was their supreme commander. They fought like wild beasts, and many Nephites were killed by their hands. They shattered many helmets, pierced through many breastplates, and cut off many arms. The Lamanites struck with fierce anger.

Nevertheless, the Nephites were driven by a better cause. They were not fighting for power or control—they were fighting for their homes and their freedoms, their wives and their children, and for everything they had. Yes, they fought for their right to worship and for their church.

And they were doing what they felt was their duty to God, because the Lord had said to them and to their ancestors: As long as you are not guilty of striking the first or second blow, you must not allow yourselves to be killed by your enemies.

And the Lord had also said: You must defend your families even if it means bloodshed. This is why the Nephites were fighting the Lamanites—to defend themselves, their families, their lands, their country, their rights, and their religion.

When Moroni's men saw how fierce and angry the Lamanites were, they were about to lose courage and run. But Moroni, seeing what they were thinking, sent word and inspired their hearts with these thoughts—yes, thoughts of their lands, their liberty, and their freedom from slavery.
They turned back against the Lamanites and cried out together to the Lord their God for their liberty and their freedom from slavery.

And they began to stand firm against the Lamanites with power. In that very hour when they cried out to the Lord for their freedom, the Lamanites began to flee before them, and they fled all the way to the waters of Sidon.
Now, the Lamanites outnumbered them by more than two to one. Nevertheless, they were driven back until they were gathered into one group in the valley on the bank by the river Sidon.
Therefore, Moroni's armies surrounded them on both sides of the river, because on the east side were the men of Lehi.
When Zerahemnah saw the men of Lehi on the east side of the river Sidon and Moroni's armies on the west side, and that they were completely surrounded by the Nephites, they were struck with terror.
When Moroni saw their terror, he commanded his men to stop shedding blood.

44.text: They stopped fighting and pulled back a short distance from them. And Moroni said to Zerahemnah: Zerahemnah, we do not want to be men of bloodshed. You know that you are in our power, yet we do not want to kill you.

We did not come out to battle against you so we could shed your blood for power, and we do not want to put anyone in chains of slavery. But that is the very reason you have come against us—and you are angry with us because of our religion.

But now you can see that the Lord is with us, and you can see that he has delivered you into our hands. And now I want you to understand that this has happened because of our religion and our faith in Christ. Now you can see that you cannot destroy our faith.

Now you can see that this is the true faith of God. You can see that God will support, protect, and preserve us as long as we are faithful to him and to our faith and our religion. And the Lord will never allow us to be destroyed unless we fall into sin and deny our faith.

And now, Zerahemnah, I command you, in the name of that all-powerful God who has strengthened our arms so that we have gained power over you—by our faith, by our religion, by our worship, by our church, by the sacred duty we owe to our wives and children, by the liberty that ties us to our lands and our country, and by the preservation of the sacred word of God, to which we owe all our happiness, and by everything that is most dear to us—

Yes, and that is not all. I command you by everything you desire about life itself: hand over your weapons of war to us, and we will not seek your blood but will spare your lives, if you will go on your way and never come to war against us again.

And now, if you do not do this, you are in our power, and I will command my men to attack you and strike you with death blows until you are wiped out. And then we will see who has power over this people. Yes, we will see who will be brought into slavery.

When Zerahemnah heard these words, he came forward and handed over his sword, his curved blade, and his bow to Moroni, and said to him: Here are our weapons of war. We will give them to you, but we will not swear an oath to you that we know we would break, and our children after us. Take our weapons of war, and let us leave into the wilderness. Otherwise, we will keep our swords and either die or conquer.

We are not of your faith. We do not believe it was God who delivered us into your hands. We believe it was your cleverness that protected you from our swords. It is your breastplates and your shields that have kept you safe.
When Zerahemnah had finished speaking, Moroni returned the sword and the weapons of war to Zerahemnah, saying: We will end the conflict now.

I cannot take back the words I have spoken. Therefore, as the Lord lives, you will not leave unless you leave with an oath that you will not return to war against us. Now since you are in our power, we will spill your blood on the ground, or you will agree to the conditions I have offered.

When Moroni had said this, Zerahemnah kept his sword and, furious with Moroni, rushed forward to kill him. But as he raised his sword, one of Moroni's soldiers struck it to the ground, and it broke at the hilt. He also struck Zerahemnah and cut off his scalp, and it fell to the ground. Zerahemnah pulled back from them into the middle of his soldiers.

The soldier who stood nearby, who had cut off Zerahemnah's scalp, picked it up from the ground by the hair and placed it on the tip of his sword. He stretched it out toward them and said in a loud voice:
Just as this scalp has fallen to the earth—the scalp of your chief—so will you fall to the earth unless you hand over your weapons of war and leave with a covenant of peace.

Many who heard these words and saw the scalp on the sword were struck with fear. Many came forward and threw down their weapons of war at Moroni's feet and entered into a covenant of peace. All who made the covenant were allowed to leave into the wilderness.
But Zerahemnah was extremely furious, and he stirred up the rest of his soldiers to anger, urging them to fight even harder against the Nephites.

Now Moroni was angry because of the stubbornness of the Lamanites, so he commanded his people to attack them and kill them. And they began to cut them down. The Lamanites fought back with their swords and all their strength.

But their exposed skin and bare heads were no match for the sharp swords of the Nephites. They were cut down and fell extremely fast before the Nephite swords. They began to be swept away, just as Moroni's soldier had prophesied.

When Zerahemnah saw that they were all about to be destroyed, he cried out desperately to Moroni, promising that he and his people would make a covenant with them if they would spare the rest of their lives—promising they would never come to war against them again.
Moroni ordered the killing to stop again. He took the weapons of war from the Lamanites, and after they had entered into a covenant of peace with him, they were allowed to leave into the wilderness.
The number of their dead was not counted because of how great it was. Yes, the number of dead was extremely large on both the Nephite and Lamanite sides.
They threw their dead into the waters of Sidon, and the bodies were carried away and buried in the depths of the sea.
The Nephite armies, the armies of Moroni, returned to their homes and their lands.
And so ended the eighteenth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi. And so ended the record of Alma, which was written on the plates of Nephi.

45.text: The Nephite people were filled with great joy because the Lord had once again rescued them from their enemies. They gave thanks to the Lord their God, and they fasted and prayed often, worshipping God with tremendous joy.
In the nineteenth year of the judges' rule over the Nephites, Alma came to his son Helaman and asked him, "Do you believe the things I told you about the sacred records that have been kept?"
Helaman answered, "Yes, I believe."
Alma asked again, "Do you believe in Jesus Christ, who will come?"
Helaman replied, "Yes, I believe everything you have said."
Alma asked him once more, "Will you keep my commandments?"
Helaman answered, "Yes, I will keep your commandments with all my heart."
Then Alma told him, "You are blessed, and the Lord will help you prosper in this land."
"But I have a prophecy to share with you. What I prophesy must not be made known until the prophecy is fulfilled. So write down the words I am about to say."

And these are the words: "I can see that this very people, the Nephites—according to the spirit of revelation that is in me—four hundred years after Jesus Christ appears to them, will fall away into unbelief."
"Yes, and then they will experience wars and plagues, famines and bloodshed, until the Nephite people are completely wiped out."

"This will happen because they will fall away into unbelief and turn to darkness, immoral behavior, and every kind of sin. I say to you, because they will sin against such great light and knowledge, even the fourth generation will not all pass away before this great wickedness comes."
"When that great day comes—and it will come very soon—those who are now counted among the Nephites, or their descendants, will no longer be counted as Nephites."

"But whoever survives that great and terrible day will be counted among the Lamanites and will become like them, except for a few who will be called disciples of the Lord. The Lamanites will hunt them down until they are completely destroyed. Because of wickedness, this prophecy will be fulfilled."
After Alma said these things to Helaman, he blessed him and also his other sons. He also blessed the earth for the sake of the righteous.

He said, "This is what the Lord God has declared: The land will be cursed—yes, this land—for every nation, people, and language that does wickedly when they are fully ripe in sin. As I have said, so it will be, for this is God's cursing and blessing upon the land, because the Lord cannot tolerate sin in the slightest degree."
After saying these words, Alma blessed the church—all those who would remain faithful from that time forward.
When Alma had finished this, he left the land of Zarahemla as if he were going to the land of Melek. He was never heard from again. We know nothing about his death or burial.

We do know that he was a righteous man. The word spread through the church that he was taken up by the Spirit or buried by the hand of the Lord, just like Moses. The scriptures say that the Lord took Moses to himself, and we believe he also received Alma in the spirit. For this reason, we know nothing about his death and burial.
At the beginning of the nineteenth year of the judges, Helaman went out among the people to preach the word of God.

Because of their wars with the Lamanites and the many disagreements and disturbances among the people, it was necessary for the word of God to be preached among them and for the church to be set in order.
So Helaman and his brothers went out to reestablish the church throughout the land, in every city the Nephites controlled. They appointed priests and teachers over all the churches.
After Helaman and his brothers had appointed priests and teachers, a division arose among the people, and they refused to listen to Helaman and his brothers.
They became proud and arrogant because of their great wealth. They became self-important and would not listen to the teachings or walk uprightly before God.

46.text: Those who refused to listen to Helaman and his brothers gathered together against their fellow believers.
They were extremely angry—so angry that they were determined to kill them.
The leader of those who were angry was a large and powerful man named Amalickiah.
Amalickiah wanted to be king, and the angry people wanted him to be their king too. Most of them were lower judges in the land who were seeking power.
Amalickiah had persuaded them with flattery that if they supported him and made him king, he would make them rulers over the people.
Through his flattery, Amalickiah led them into rebellion, despite the preaching of Helaman and his brothers, who were high priests over the church and cared deeply for it.

Many church members believed Amalickiah's flattering words and left the church. The situation among the Nephites became extremely dangerous, despite their recent great victory over the Lamanites and their rejoicing over being delivered by the Lord.
This shows us how quickly people forget the Lord their God—how quick they are to do wrong and to be led astray by the devil.
We also see how much wickedness one very wicked man can cause among the people.

We see that Amalickiah, because he was cunning and spoke flattering words, led many people to do wickedly—to try to destroy the church of God and to tear down the liberty that God had given them for the sake of the righteous.
When Moroni, the chief commander of the Nephite armies, heard about these rebellions, he was furious with Amalickiah.
He tore his coat and took a piece of it, and wrote on it: "In memory of our God, our religion, and freedom, and our peace, our wives, and our children." Then he fastened it to the end of a pole.

He put on his helmet, breastplate, and shields, and strapped on his armor. Then he took the pole with the torn piece of his coat on it—which he called the title of liberty—and bowed down to the ground, praying with all his heart to God that the blessings of liberty would rest upon his people, as long as there was a group of Christians left in the land.
For that is what all the true believers of Christ who belonged to the church of God were called by those who did not belong to the church.
Those who belonged to the church were faithful. All who truly believed in Christ gladly took upon themselves the name of Christ, or Christians, because of their belief in Christ who would come.
At that time, Moroni prayed that the cause of the Christians and the freedom of the land would be blessed.
After he had poured out his soul to God, he declared all the land south of the land Desolation—indeed, all the land both north and south—to be a chosen land, a land of liberty.
He said, "Surely God will not allow us, who are looked down on because we take upon ourselves the name of Christ, to be trampled and destroyed—unless we bring it upon ourselves through our own sins."
After saying these words, Moroni went out among the people, waving the torn piece of his garment in the air so everyone could see what he had written on it, and crying out with a loud voice:

"Whoever will defend this title upon the land, let them come forward in the strength of the Lord and make a covenant that they will protect their rights and their religion, so that the Lord God may bless them."

When Moroni proclaimed this, the people came running together with their armor on, tearing their own garments as a sign—a covenant—that they would not abandon the Lord their God. In other words, if they broke God's commandments or were ashamed to take upon themselves the name of Christ, the Lord could tear them apart just as they had torn their garments.

This was the covenant they made, and they threw their garments at Moroni's feet, saying, "We make a covenant with our God that we will be destroyed, just like our relatives in the land northward, if we fall into sin. He may cast us at the feet of our enemies, just as we have thrown our garments at your feet to be trampled, if we fall into sin."

Moroni said to them, "We are descendants of Jacob—yes, we are descendants of Joseph, whose coat was torn into many pieces by his brothers. Let us remember to keep God's commandments, or our garments will be torn by our own brothers, and we will be cast into prison, sold, or killed."

"Let us preserve our liberty as a remnant of Joseph. Let us remember the words of Jacob before his death, for he saw that part of Joseph's coat had been preserved and had not decayed. Jacob said, 'Just as this piece of my son's garment has been preserved, so a remnant of my son's descendants will be preserved by the hand of God and taken to himself, while the rest of Joseph's descendants will perish, just like the rest of his garment.'"
"This brings sorrow to my soul, but I still have joy in my son because of that part of his descendants who will be taken to God."
These were the words of Jacob.

"Who knows but that the remnant of Joseph's descendants who will perish like his garment are those who have rebelled against us? Yes, and it could even be us if we do not stand firm in the faith of Christ."

After saying these words, Moroni went out and sent messengers throughout the land wherever there were rebellions, gathering all the people who wanted to defend their liberty and stand against Amalickiah and his followers, who were called Amalickiahites.

When Amalickiah saw that Moroni's supporters outnumbered his own—and that his people were beginning to doubt their cause—he feared he would fail. So he took those who were willing to follow him and fled to the land of Nephi.

Moroni did not want the Lamanites to gain any more strength, so he decided to cut off Amalickiah's people, capture them, and bring them back. He wanted to put Amalickiah to death because he knew Amalickiah would stir up the Lamanites to attack the Nephites in order to accomplish his goals.
So Moroni took his armies, who had gathered together, armed themselves, and made a covenant to keep the peace, and marched into the wilderness to cut off Amalickiah's escape route.
He carried out his plan, marching into the wilderness and intercepting Amalickiah's armies.
Amalickiah escaped with a small number of his men, but the rest were captured and brought back to the land of Zarahemla.
Now, Moroni had been appointed by the chief judges and the voice of the people, so he had authority over the Nephite armies to establish order and command them.
Any of the Amalickiahites who refused to make a covenant to support the cause of freedom and maintain a free government were put to death. Only a few refused the covenant of freedom.
Moroni also had the title of liberty raised on every tower throughout all the land controlled by the Nephites. In this way, Moroni established the standard of liberty among the Nephites.
Peace returned to the land, and they maintained that peace until nearly the end of the nineteenth year of the judges.
Helaman and the high priests also maintained order in the church. For four years, there was much peace and rejoicing in the church.
Many people died during this time, firmly believing that their souls were saved by the Lord Jesus Christ. They left this world rejoicing.

Some died from fevers, which were common at certain times of year in the land—but fevers were not as deadly because of the excellent medicinal plants and roots that God had provided to treat the diseases that people were prone to because of the climate.
Many others died of old age, and those who died in the faith of Christ are happy in him, as we can surely believe.

47.text: Now we return to Amalickiah and those who had fled with him into the wilderness. He had taken his followers and gone up to the land of Nephi among the Lamanites, where he stirred them up to anger against the Nephites. The king of the Lamanites sent a proclamation throughout all his land, calling all his people to gather for war against the Nephites.

When the people heard the proclamation, they were terrified. They feared displeasing the king, but they also feared going to battle against the Nephites and losing their lives. Most of them refused to obey the king's command.
The king was furious at their disobedience, so he gave Amalickiah command over the part of the army that had obeyed and ordered him to go force the others to take up arms.
This was exactly what Amalickiah wanted, because he was extremely cunning and evil, and he had already made plans in his heart to overthrow the king of the Lamanites.

Now Amalickiah commanded the king's loyal soldiers, but he also wanted to win over those who had disobeyed. So he marched to the place called Onidah, where all the disobedient Lamanites had fled, thinking the approaching army was coming to destroy them.
These Lamanites had appointed a man to be their king and leader, firmly resolved that they would not be forced to fight against the Nephites.
They had gathered on top of the mountain called Antipas, preparing for battle.

Amalickiah did not actually intend to fight them as the king had commanded. Instead, his plan was to win the loyalty of these armies, put himself at their head, and overthrow the king to take the kingdom for himself.
He ordered his army to set up camp in the valley near Mount Antipas.
That night, he sent a secret message up the mountain to Lehonti, the leader of those on the mountain, asking him to come down to the foot of the mountain to talk.

When Lehonti received the message, he did not dare go down to the foot of the mountain. Amalickiah sent a second message, but Lehonti still refused. He sent a third message, but Lehonti would not come down.

When Amalickiah realized he could not get Lehonti to come down, he went up the mountain himself, nearly to Lehonti's camp. He sent a fourth message, asking Lehonti to come down and to bring his guards with him.

When Lehonti came down with his guards to meet Amalickiah, Amalickiah asked him to bring his army down during the night and surround the soldiers the king had placed under Amalickiah's command. In exchange, Amalickiah asked to be made second-in-command over the entire army.
Lehonti came down with his men and surrounded Amalickiah's soldiers, so that before dawn they were completely encircled by Lehonti's armies.
When the surrounded soldiers woke up and saw they were trapped, they begged Amalickiah to let them join their brothers rather than be destroyed. This was exactly what Amalickiah wanted.
Amalickiah handed over his men, going against the king's orders. This was all part of his plan to overthrow the king.
It was the Lamanite custom that if their chief leader was killed, the second-in-command would become the new chief leader.
Amalickiah had one of his servants slowly poison Lehonti little by little until he died.
When Lehonti was dead, the Lamanites appointed Amalickiah as their leader and chief commander.
Amalickiah then marched with his armies—having achieved his goals—to the land of Nephi, to the city of Nephi, which was the capital city.
The king came out to meet him with his guards, assuming Amalickiah had carried out his orders and had gathered this great army to fight against the Nephites.
But as the king came out to meet him, Amalickiah sent his servants ahead to meet the king. They bowed before the king as if paying respect to him because of his greatness.
The king reached out his hand to help them up, which was the Lamanite custom as a sign of peace—a custom they had adopted from the Nephites.
But when the king raised the first servant from the ground, that servant stabbed the king in the heart, and the king fell to the earth dead.
The king's own servants fled, and Amalickiah's servants raised a cry, shouting:
"Look! The king's servants have stabbed him in the heart! He has fallen, and they have fled! Come and see!"

Amalickiah ordered his armies to march forward and see what had happened to the king. When they arrived and found the king lying in his blood, Amalickiah pretended to be outraged and said, "Whoever loved the king, go and chase down his servants so they can be killed!"
Everyone who loved the king heard these words and went out to chase the king's servants.
When the king's servants saw an army chasing them, they were terrified and fled into the wilderness. They eventually reached the land of Zarahemla and joined the people of Ammon.
The pursuing army returned without catching them. In this way, Amalickiah won the hearts of the people through his deception.
The next day, he entered the city of Nephi with his armies and took control of the city.
When the queen heard that the king had been killed—because Amalickiah had sent a message telling her that the king had been murdered by his own servants and that he had chased them but they escaped—
the queen sent a message to Amalickiah, asking him to spare the people of the city. She also asked him to come to her and to bring witnesses who could testify about how the king died.

Amalickiah brought the very servant who had killed the king, along with the others who were with him, and went to the queen. They all testified to her that the king was killed by his own servants and that the servants had fled. They said, "Doesn't their fleeing prove their guilt?" In this way, they convinced the queen about the king's death.

Amalickiah then sought the queen's favor and married her. Through his treachery and with the help of his cunning servants, he seized the kingdom. He was recognized as king throughout all the land, among all the Lamanites—which included the Lamanites, Lemuelites, Ishmaelites, and all the Nephite dissenters from the time of Nephi down to that day.

Now these Nephite dissenters had received the same teaching and knowledge as the Nephites—the same knowledge of the Lord. Yet strangely, not long after they left, they became more hardened, stubborn, wild, wicked, and violent than the Lamanites—adopting Lamanite traditions, giving in to laziness and every kind of immoral behavior, and completely forgetting the Lord their God.

48.text: As soon as Amalickiah had taken the kingdom, he began to turn the hearts of the Lamanites against the Nephites. He appointed men to speak from their towers against the Nephites.

He stirred up their hearts against the Nephites so effectively that by the end of the nineteenth year of the judges—having accomplished his plans so far and having been made king over the Lamanites—he also sought to rule over the entire land, including the Nephites.
He had accomplished his goal by hardening the hearts of the Lamanites, blinding their minds, and stirring them up to anger until he had gathered a massive army to go to war against the Nephites.
He was determined, because of the enormous size of his army, to overpower the Nephites and bring them into slavery.

He appointed Zoramite chief captains, because they were most familiar with the strength of the Nephites, their gathering places, and the weakest parts of their cities. So he made them commanders over his armies.
They broke camp and marched through the wilderness toward the land of Zarahemla.
While Amalickiah had been gaining power through fraud and deception, Moroni had been preparing his people to be faithful to the Lord their God.

He had been strengthening the Nephite armies and building small forts and defensive positions. He had his soldiers pile up walls of earth around their positions and also build stone walls around their cities and along their borders—throughout the entire land.
At the weakest defensive positions, he placed the largest number of soldiers. In this way, he fortified and strengthened the land controlled by the Nephites.

He was doing all of this to protect their liberty, their lands, their wives and children, and their peace, so they could worship the Lord their God and maintain what their enemies called the cause of Christians.

Moroni was a strong and mighty man. He was a man of great understanding who did not enjoy bloodshed—a man whose soul found joy in the liberty and freedom of his country and in freeing his people from bondage and slavery.
His heart overflowed with gratitude to God for the many privileges and blessings given to his people. He worked tirelessly for the welfare and safety of his people.
He was a man firm in the faith of Christ, and he had sworn an oath to defend his people, his rights, his country, and his religion, even if it cost him his life.

The Nephites were taught to defend themselves against their enemies, even to the shedding of blood if necessary. But they were also taught never to attack first—never to draw the sword except against an enemy and only to save their own lives.

This was their belief: that by doing this, God would help them prosper in the land. In other words, if they faithfully kept God's commandments, he would help them prosper, warn them to flee, or help them prepare for war according to their danger.

They also believed that God would show them where to go to defend themselves, and that the Lord would deliver them. This was Moroni's faith, and his heart took joy in it—not in shedding blood, but in doing good, protecting his people, keeping God's commandments, and resisting evil.
Truly, I say to you, if all people had been, were, and ever would be like Moroni, the very powers of hell would have been shaken forever. The devil would never have power over the hearts of anyone.
He was a man like Ammon, the son of Mosiah, and the other sons of Mosiah, and also like Alma and his sons—for they were all men of God.
Helaman and his brothers were just as valuable to the people as Moroni was, for they preached the word of God and baptized everyone who would listen to their words.

They went among the people, and the people humbled themselves because of their teachings, so that the Lord greatly blessed them. They were free from wars and conflicts among themselves for four years.
But as I mentioned, at the end of the nineteenth year, despite the peace among themselves, they were reluctantly forced to fight against their Lamanite brothers.
Their wars with the Lamanites continued for many years, despite their deep reluctance to fight.

They were sorry to take up arms against the Lamanites because they did not enjoy shedding blood. And that was not all—they were grieved to be the cause of sending so many of their brothers out of this world and into eternity unprepared to meet their God.

Nevertheless, they could not stand by and allow their wives and children to be slaughtered by the brutal cruelty of those who were once their brothers—people who had left their church and joined the Lamanites to destroy them.

They could not bear to let their enemies celebrate over the blood of the Nephites, as long as there were people who kept God's commandments. For the Lord had promised that if they kept his commandments, they would prosper in the land.

49.text: In the eleventh month of the nineteenth year, on the tenth day of the month, the Lamanite armies were spotted approaching the land of Ammonihah.

The city had been rebuilt, and Moroni had stationed an army at its borders. They had piled up dirt around them to protect them from the Lamanites' arrows and stones, since the Lamanites fought with stones and arrows.

I should mention that the city of Ammonihah had only been partly rebuilt. Because the Lamanites had destroyed it once before due to the people's wickedness, they assumed it would be easy to conquer again.

But they were in for a huge disappointment! The Nephites had built up a ridge of earth so high around them that the Lamanites could not throw stones or shoot arrows effectively, and they could only approach through the entrance.
The Lamanite chief captains were absolutely astonished at the wisdom of the Nephites in preparing their defenses.

The Lamanite leaders had assumed that because of their superior numbers, they could overrun the Nephites as they had done before. They had also equipped themselves with shields, breastplates, and thick animal-skin garments for protection.
With these preparations, they thought they could easily overpower the Nephites, put them in bondage, or slaughter them at will.
But to their utter amazement, the Nephites were prepared in ways never before seen among the descendants of Lehi. They were ready for battle according to Moroni's instructions.
The Lamanites—the Amalickiahites—were completely stunned by the Nephites' preparations for war.

If King Amalickiah had come down from the land of Nephi to lead his army personally, he might have ordered the Lamanites to attack the city of Ammonihah, because he did not care about the lives of his people.

But Amalickiah did not come down to battle himself. His chief captains did not dare attack the Nephites at Ammonihah, because Moroni had so completely changed the Nephites' defenses that the Lamanites found no weakness to exploit.
So they retreated into the wilderness, broke camp, and marched toward the land of Noah, thinking that would be the next best place to attack the Nephites.

They did not know that Moroni had built defensive fortifications around every city in the land. So they marched toward the land of Noah with firm determination. Their chief captains even swore an oath that they would destroy the people of that city.
But to their astonishment, the city of Noah—which had previously been a weak point—had now, thanks to Moroni, become even stronger than the city of Ammonihah.

This was Moroni's strategy: he had anticipated that the Lamanites would be scared away by the defenses at Ammonihah, and since Noah had always been the weakest point, they would march there instead. Everything happened exactly as he had planned.
Moroni had appointed Lehi as chief captain over the men of that city. This was the same Lehi who had fought the Lamanites in the valley east of the river Sidon.

When the Lamanites discovered that Lehi commanded the city, they were discouraged again because they feared Lehi greatly. Nevertheless, their chief captains had sworn an oath to attack, so they brought their armies forward.
The Lamanites could not enter the fortress by any way except the entrance, because the earthen walls were too high and the surrounding ditches were too deep.
The Nephites were prepared to destroy anyone who tried to climb over the walls by throwing stones and shooting arrows at them.

They had their strongest men stationed with swords and slings, ready to strike down anyone who tried to enter through the entrance. They were fully prepared to defend themselves against the Lamanites.
The Lamanite captains brought their armies to the entrance and began fighting the Nephites, trying to break into the fortress. But they were driven back again and again, suffering enormous losses.

When they realized they could not break through the entrance, they tried to dig down the earthen walls to create other openings for their armies so they could fight on equal ground. But as they tried, they were cut down by the stones and arrows thrown at them. Instead of filling up the Nephites' ditches by tearing down the walls, the ditches were partly filled with their own dead and wounded.

The Nephites had complete power over their enemies. The Lamanites kept attacking until all their chief captains were killed—more than a thousand Lamanites died. On the Nephite side, not a single person was killed.

About fifty Nephites were wounded, having been exposed to arrows through the entrance, but their shields, breastplates, and helmets protected them so that their wounds were mostly on their legs—some of which were quite serious.

When the Lamanites saw that all their chief captains were dead, they fled into the wilderness. They returned to the land of Nephi to tell their king, Amalickiah—who was a Nephite by birth—about their devastating defeat.
Amalickiah was furious with his people because he had not conquered the Nephites or brought them into bondage.

He was so angry that he cursed God and also cursed Moroni, swearing an oath that he would drink Moroni's blood—and this because Moroni had kept God's commandments by preparing for the safety of his people.
On the other hand, the Nephites thanked the Lord their God for his incredible power in delivering them from their enemies.
And so ended the nineteenth year of the judges over the Nephites.

There was continued peace among them and great prosperity in the church because of their attention and dedication to the word of God, which was taught to them by Helaman, Shiblon, Corianton, Ammon and his brothers, and all those who had been ordained by the holy order of God, baptized unto repentance, and sent out to preach among the people.

50.text: Moroni did not stop preparing for war or defending his people against the Lamanites. At the start of the twentieth year of the judges, he had his armies begin digging up mounds of earth around all the cities throughout the entire land controlled by the Nephites.
On top of these ridges of earth, he had them build wooden frameworks as tall as a man, all around the cities.
On these wooden frameworks, he had them build a fence of sharpened stakes, which were strong and tall.
He had towers built that overlooked the fences, and on top of those towers he had sheltered positions built so the stones and arrows of the Lamanites could not reach the defenders.
From the top of these towers, the defenders could throw stones down on anyone who approached the city walls, with as much force as they desired.
In this way, Moroni prepared strongholds against the coming of their enemies around every city in the land.
Moroni then sent his armies into the east wilderness, where they drove out all the Lamanites and pushed them back into their own lands south of Zarahemla.
The land of Nephi ran in a straight line from the east sea to the west sea.

After Moroni had driven all the Lamanites out of the east wilderness—which was north of Lamanite territory—he had the people from Zarahemla and the surrounding areas move into the east wilderness, all the way to the seashore, to settle the land.
He also stationed armies on the southern borders of their territory and had them build fortifications to protect their armies and people from their enemies.

In this way, he eliminated all the Lamanite strongholds in the east wilderness and also in the west, fortifying the border between the Nephites and the Lamanites—between the land of Zarahemla and the land of Nephi—from the west sea, along the headwaters of the river Sidon. The Nephites controlled all the land to the north, including everything north of the land Bountiful.

Moroni's armies, which grew daily because people felt safe under his protection, worked to cut off the Lamanites' strength and power from the surrounding lands so they would have no influence on Nephite territory.
The Nephites began building a new city by the east sea, on the southern border of Lamanite territory, and named it Moroni.
They also started building another city between the city of Moroni and the city of Aaron, on the border between the two, and called it Nephihah.
That same year, they also began building many cities in the north. One in particular, called Lehi, was in the north near the seashore.
And so ended the twentieth year.
The Nephites were prospering at the start of the twenty-first year of the judges.
They prospered greatly and became very wealthy. They multiplied and grew strong in the land.

In this way we see how merciful and just the Lord is in everything he does, fulfilling all his promises to his children. We can see that his words are being proven true even now—the words he spoke to Lehi:

"You and your children are blessed. As long as they keep my commandments, they will prosper in the land. But remember, if they will not keep my commandments, they will be cut off from the presence of the Lord."

We can see that these promises have come true for the Nephites. It was their quarreling and fighting, their murders and robberies, their idol worship and immorality among themselves that brought wars and destruction upon them.

Those who faithfully kept the Lord's commandments were always delivered, while thousands of their wicked brothers were brought into slavery, killed by the sword, or fell away into unbelief and mixed with the Lamanites.
There was never a happier time among the Nephites, since the days of the first Nephi, than during the days of Moroni—at this time, in the twenty-first year of the judges.
The twenty-second year of the judges also ended in peace, and so did the twenty-third year.
At the start of the twenty-fourth year, there would have been peace if not for a dispute that arose over the land of Lehi and the land of Morianton, which bordered each other near the seashore.
The people of Morianton claimed part of the land of Lehi. A heated conflict broke out between them, and the people of Morianton took up arms against their neighbors, determined to kill them.
But the people of Lehi fled to Moroni's camp and asked for his help, because they were not in the wrong.
When the people of Morianton—led by a man named Morianton—learned that the people of Lehi had gone to Moroni for help, they became terrified that Moroni's army would come and destroy them.
So Morianton convinced his people that they should flee to the land northward, which was covered with large bodies of water, and take it over.
They would have carried out this plan—which would have been a disaster—but Morianton, a man with a terrible temper, became angry with one of his female servants and beat her severely.
She escaped and went to Moroni's camp, where she told him everything—including their plan to flee to the land northward.
Moroni feared that if the people in the land of Bountiful listened to Morianton and joined him, he would gain control of strategic territory that could lead to the downfall of the Nephites' liberty.
So Moroni sent an army to cut off Morianton's people before they could escape to the land northward.

They did not catch up to them until they reached the borders of the land Desolation, where they blocked them at the narrow pass that led by the sea into the land northward—with the sea on both the west and east sides.

The army sent by Moroni was led by a man named Teancum. He intercepted Morianton's people, and because Morianton's followers were stubbornly loyal to him due to his wickedness and flattering words, a battle broke out. Teancum killed Morianton, defeated his army, and took them prisoner. He then returned to Moroni's camp. This ended the twenty-fourth year of the judges.

The people of Morianton were brought back, and after they made a covenant to keep the peace, they were returned to their land. The people of Lehi and Morianton were reunited, and their lands were restored to them.
That same year, Nephihah, the second chief judge, died after serving with perfect integrity before God.
Nephihah had declined to take custody of the sacred records and objects that Alma and his ancestors considered most sacred, so Alma had given them to his son Helaman instead.

Nephihah's son was appointed to fill the judgment seat in his father's place. He became chief judge and governor over the people, taking an oath and sacred commitment to judge righteously, maintain peace and freedom, protect the people's right to worship the Lord their God, uphold the cause of God throughout his days, and bring the wicked to justice for their crimes.
His name was Pahoran. Pahoran took his father's seat and began his rule at the end of the twenty-fourth year over the Nephites.

51.text: At the start of the twenty-fifth year of the judges, peace had been established between the people of Lehi and the people of Morianton over their lands, and the twenty-fifth year began peacefully.
However, they did not maintain peace for long. A conflict arose among the people regarding Chief Judge Pahoran, because some people wanted certain parts of the law changed.
But Pahoran refused to change the law or allow it to be altered. He would not listen to those who petitioned him to change it.
Those who wanted the law changed became angry with Pahoran and demanded that he be removed as chief judge. A heated dispute arose, though it did not lead to bloodshed.
Those who wanted Pahoran removed from the judgment seat were called king-men, because they wanted to change the law in order to overthrow the free government and establish a king over the land.

Those who wanted Pahoran to remain as chief judge called themselves freemen. This is how the people were divided: the freemen had sworn a covenant to maintain their rights and religious freedoms through a free government.

The dispute was settled by a vote of the people. The people voted in favor of the freemen, and Pahoran kept the judgment seat. This brought great rejoicing among Pahoran's supporters and many of the liberty-loving people, and it silenced the king-men so that they did not dare oppose and were forced to support the cause of freedom.
Those who favored kings were people of high social status who wanted to be kings themselves. They were supported by people who sought power and authority over others.

This was a terrible time for such divisions among the Nephites, because Amalickiah had again stirred up the Lamanites against the Nephites. He was gathering soldiers from every part of his kingdom, arming them, and preparing for war with full effort. He had sworn to drink the blood of Moroni.
We shall see that his vow was reckless. Nevertheless, he prepared himself and his armies to come fight against the Nephites.

His armies were not as large as before because thousands had been killed by the Nephites. But despite those heavy losses, Amalickiah had gathered an incredibly large army—so large that he was not afraid to march down to the land of Zarahemla.
Amalickiah himself came down at the head of the Lamanite army. It was the twenty-fifth year of the judges, at the very time the Nephites were trying to settle the dispute over Chief Judge Pahoran.

When the king-men heard that the Lamanites were coming to attack, they were actually glad. They refused to take up arms because they were so angry with the chief judge and the freemen that they would not fight to defend their country.

When Moroni saw this—and saw that the Lamanites were entering the borders of the land—he was furious at the stubbornness of the people he had worked so hard to protect. His soul was filled with anger toward them.
He sent a petition, backed by the voice of the people, to the governor, requesting the authority to force these dissenters to either defend their country or be put to death.
His top priority was to end these conflicts and divisions among the people, because this had been the cause of all their past destruction. His request was granted by the voice of the people.
Moroni ordered his army to march against the king-men—to break their pride and bring them down, or else they would take up arms and support the cause of liberty.
The armies marched against them, and they crushed their pride and arrogance. When the king-men raised their weapons to fight against Moroni's soldiers, they were cut down.
Four thousand of these dissenters were killed by the sword. Their leaders who survived the battle were arrested and thrown into prison, since there was no time for trials during this crisis.

The rest of the dissenters, rather than be cut down by the sword, surrendered to the standard of liberty. They were required to raise the title of liberty on their towers and in their cities and to take up arms in defense of their country.

In this way, Moroni put an end to the king-men. No one was known by that name anymore. He ended the stubbornness and pride of those who claimed noble blood, forcing them to humble themselves like everyone else and fight bravely for their freedom from bondage.

While Moroni was dealing with the wars and conflicts among his own people, bringing them under control and establishing order to prepare for war against the Lamanites, the Lamanites had already entered the land of Moroni near the seashore.
The Nephites were not strong enough to hold the city of Moroni, so Amalickiah drove them out, killing many. Amalickiah took control of the city and all its fortifications.
Those who escaped from the city of Moroni went to the city of Nephihah. The people of the city of Lehi also gathered together and prepared to face the Lamanites in battle.
But Amalickiah would not let the Lamanites attack the city of Nephihah. Instead, he kept them along the seashore, leaving soldiers in every captured city to hold and defend it.
He continued this way, capturing many cities: Nephihah, Lehi, Morianton, Omner, Gid, and Mulek—all of which were on the eastern coast by the seashore.

Through Amalickiah's cunning, the Lamanites had taken all these cities with their vast armies. Every one of these cities had been strongly fortified using Moroni's own methods, and they now served as Lamanite strongholds.
They marched to the borders of the land Bountiful, driving the Nephites before them and killing many.
But they were met by Teancum—the same man who had killed Morianton and stopped his people from fleeing.
Teancum blocked Amalickiah as he marched with his massive army to seize the land of Bountiful and the land northward.

Amalickiah met disappointment when Teancum and his men stopped him, for they were great warriors. Every one of Teancum's men surpassed the Lamanites in strength and fighting skill, giving them a clear advantage.

They harassed the Lamanites, fighting them until dark. Teancum and his men set up camp at the borders of the land Bountiful, while Amalickiah camped at the borders along the beach by the seashore. That is how far they had been pushed back.
When night came, Teancum and his servant crept out and went into Amalickiah's camp. The entire camp had fallen into deep sleep because they were exhausted from the day's labor and heat.
Teancum quietly entered the king's tent and drove a javelin into his heart, killing him instantly without waking his servants.
He returned secretly to his own camp, where his men were sleeping. He woke them and told them everything he had done.
He ordered his armies to stand ready in case the Lamanites woke up and attacked.
And so ended the twenty-fifth year of the judges over the Nephites, and so ended the days of Amalickiah.

52.text: In the twenty-sixth year of the judges over the Nephites, when the Lamanites woke up on the first morning of the first month, they discovered that Amalickiah was dead in his own tent. They also saw that Teancum was ready to fight them that day.

When the Lamanites saw this, they were terrified. They abandoned their plan to march into the land northward and retreated with their entire army into the city of Mulek, seeking protection behind its fortifications.
Amalickiah's brother was appointed king over the people. His name was Ammoron, and he was appointed to rule in his brother's place.
Ammoron commanded his people to hold the cities they had taken through bloodshed, since they had suffered heavy losses taking every one of them.

Teancum could see that the Lamanites were determined to hold the cities and territories they had captured. Seeing how huge their numbers were, he decided it would not be wise to try attacking them in their fortified positions.
So he kept his men nearby, appearing to prepare for war. And in truth, he was preparing to defend against them by building up walls and preparing defensive positions.
He continued these preparations until Moroni sent a large number of men to strengthen his army.

Moroni also sent orders that Teancum should keep all prisoners he captured. Since the Lamanites had taken many Nephite prisoners, Teancum was to hold Lamanite prisoners as a bargaining tool to exchange for those the Lamanites had taken.

Moroni also ordered him to fortify the land of Bountiful and secure the narrow pass leading into the land northward, so the Lamanites could not seize that strategic point and attack them from every direction.

Moroni also sent word urging Teancum to be faithful in defending that part of the land and to look for every opportunity to strike back at the Lamanites, hoping to recapture the lost cities through strategy or other means. Moroni also told him to fortify and strengthen the cities that had not yet fallen to the Lamanites.
Moroni also said, "I would come to you, but the Lamanites are attacking us along the west sea border, and I am going to fight them. That is why I cannot come to you."
King Ammoron had left the land of Zarahemla, informed the queen about his brother's death, gathered a large army, and marched against the Nephites along the west sea border.

His strategy was to harass the Nephites and draw part of their forces to the west, while the men he left behind in the captured cities would also pressure the Nephites along the east sea border and take as much territory as they could with their forces.
This was the dangerous situation the Nephites faced at the end of the twenty-sixth year of the judges.

But in the twenty-seventh year, Teancum received orders from Moroni—who had positioned armies to protect the south and west borders and had begun marching toward the land of Bountiful to help Teancum retake the lost cities—
Teancum received orders to attack the city of Mulek and retake it if possible.

Teancum prepared to attack the city of Mulek and march his army against the Lamanites, but he realized it was impossible to defeat them while they were inside their fortifications. So he gave up the plan and returned to the city of Bountiful to wait for Moroni's arrival and reinforcements.
Moroni arrived with his army at the land of Bountiful near the end of the twenty-seventh year of the judges.

At the start of the twenty-eighth year, Moroni, Teancum, and many of the chief captains held a war council to discuss how they could lure the Lamanites out of their strongholds to fight in the open, or somehow trick them into leaving so they could gain an advantage and retake the city of Mulek.
