They sent messengers to the Lamanite army defending the city of Mulek, asking their leader Jacob to come out and meet them on the plains between the two cities. But Jacob, who was a Zoramite, refused to come out with his army.
Since Moroni had no hope of meeting the Lamanites on open ground, he devised a plan to lure them out of their strongholds.

He had Teancum take a small group of men and march near the seashore. Meanwhile, Moroni and his army moved through the wilderness at night, west of the city of Mulek. The next morning, when the Lamanite guards spotted Teancum, they ran and reported it to their leader Jacob.

The Lamanite armies marched out against Teancum, thinking their superior numbers would easily overwhelm his small force. When Teancum saw the Lamanites coming, he began retreating along the seashore to the north.

When the Lamanites saw Teancum retreating, they grew bold and chased him eagerly. While Teancum led the pursuing Lamanites on a futile chase, Moroni ordered part of his army to march into the city of Mulek and seize it.
They did exactly that, killing all the defenders who had been left to protect the city—everyone who refused to surrender their weapons.
Moroni now held the city of Mulek with part of his army, while he marched with the rest to intercept the Lamanites when they returned from chasing Teancum.
The Lamanites chased Teancum all the way to the city of Bountiful, where they were met by Lehi and a small army that had been left to defend Bountiful.

When the Lamanite chief captains saw Lehi and his army coming toward them, they panicked and fled in confusion, afraid they would not make it back to the city of Mulek before Lehi caught them. They were exhausted from their march, while Lehi's men were rested and fresh.
The Lamanites did not know that Moroni and his army were behind them. Their only fear was Lehi and his men.
Lehi deliberately held back, not wanting to overtake them until they ran into Moroni and his army.

Before the Lamanites had retreated far, they found themselves surrounded by Nephites—Moroni's men on one side and Lehi's men on the other. The Nephites were all rested and full of strength, while the Lamanites were worn out from their long march.
Moroni ordered his men to attack and keep fighting until the Lamanites surrendered their weapons.
Jacob, their leader—who was also a Zoramite—had an unconquerable fighting spirit and led the Lamanites into battle against Moroni with intense fury.

Moroni stood in their path, and Jacob was determined to cut through Moroni's forces and fight his way back to the city of Mulek. But Moroni and his men were stronger and did not give ground to the Lamanites.
Both sides fought with tremendous fury, and many were killed on each side. Moroni was wounded and Jacob was killed.

Lehi pressed the Lamanites from behind with his powerful soldiers so fiercely that the Lamanites in the rear surrendered their weapons. The rest of them became completely confused, not knowing where to go or who to fight.
When Moroni saw their confusion, he called out to them, "If you bring out your weapons of war and surrender them, we will stop the bloodshed."
When the Lamanites heard this, their surviving chief captains came forward and threw their weapons at Moroni's feet. They also ordered their men to do the same.
But many refused. Those who would not give up their swords were seized and bound, their weapons taken from them, and they were forced to march with their fellow prisoners to the land of Bountiful.
The number of prisoners taken was greater than the number of those killed—greater even than the total killed on both sides combined.

53.text: They assigned guards over the Lamanite prisoners and made them go out and bury the dead -- both the Lamanite dead and the Nephite dead. Moroni placed men over them to watch them while they worked.

Moroni went to the city of Mulek with Lehi and took command of the city, then gave it to Lehi. Now this Lehi was a man who had been with Moroni in most of his battles. He was a man like Moroni, and they were glad for each other's safety. They loved each other, and they were also loved by all the people of Nephi.

After the Lamanites had finished burying the dead of both sides, they were marched back into the land Bountiful. Teancum, following Moroni's orders, had them begin digging a ditch around the land, or the city, of Bountiful.

He had them build a wall of timbers on the inner bank of the ditch, and they piled up the dirt from the ditch against the timber wall. In this way, they made the Lamanites work until they had surrounded the city of Bountiful with a strong wall of timbers and earth, built to a great height.

This city became an extremely strong fortress from that time on, and in this city they guarded the Lamanite prisoners -- behind a wall the prisoners had been made to build with their own hands. Moroni was forced to put the Lamanites to work because they were easier to guard while they were laboring. He also wanted all his own forces available for when he attacked the Lamanites.

Moroni had won a victory over one of the greatest Lamanite armies and had taken control of the city of Mulek, which was one of the strongest Lamanite positions in the land of Nephi. He had also built a stronghold to hold his prisoners.

He did not try to fight the Lamanites again that year, but instead he kept his men busy preparing for war, building fortifications to defend against the Lamanites, and rescuing their women and children from famine and suffering, and providing food for their armies.

Now the Lamanite armies on the west sea, to the south, while Moroni was away because of conflicts among the Nephites that had caused divisions among them, had gained ground over the Nephites. They had even taken control of several cities in that part of the land.
Because of the wickedness among them -- the divisions and scheming among their own people -- the Nephites were placed in an extremely dangerous situation.

Now I have something to say about the people of Ammon, who were originally Lamanites, but through Ammon and his brothers -- or rather by the power and word of God -- they had been converted to the Lord. They had been brought down into the land of Zarahemla and had been protected by the Nephites ever since.

Because of their oath, they had been kept from taking up weapons against their brothers. They had sworn an oath that they would never shed blood again. According to their oath, they would have let themselves die -- they would have allowed themselves to fall into the hands of their enemies -- if Ammon and his brothers had not shown such compassion and great love for them.
For this reason they had been brought down into the land of Zarahemla, and they had always been protected by the Nephites.

But when they saw the danger and the many hardships and troubles the Nephites were suffering on their behalf, they were moved with compassion and wanted to take up weapons in defense of their country.
But just as they were about to take their weapons of war, they were persuaded not to by Helaman and his brothers, because they were about to break the oath they had made.

Helaman was afraid that if they broke their oath, they might lose their souls. So all those who had made this covenant were forced to watch their brothers struggle through hardship and danger during this time.

But they had many sons who had not made a covenant against taking up weapons to defend themselves. So these sons gathered together -- as many as were old enough to bear arms -- and they called themselves Nephites.

They made a covenant to fight for the freedom of the Nephites, to protect the land even at the cost of their lives. They covenanted that they would never give up their freedom but would fight in every situation to protect the Nephites and themselves from slavery.
Now there were two thousand of these young men who made this covenant and took their weapons of war to defend their country.
Up to this point they had never been a burden to the Nephites, and now at this time they became a great source of support. They took up their weapons of war and wanted Helaman to be their leader.

They were all young men, and they were extremely brave and courageous, as well as strong and active. But that was not all -- they were men who were faithful and trustworthy in everything they were given to do.
Yes, they were men of truth and self-discipline, for they had been taught to keep the commandments of God and to live uprightly before him.
And so Helaman marched at the head of his two thousand young soldiers to support the Nephite people near the borders of the land on the south by the west sea.
And so ended the twenty-eighth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

54.text: Now at the beginning of the twenty-ninth year of the judges, Ammoron sent a message to Moroni asking him to exchange prisoners.

Moroni was very glad about this request, because he wanted the supplies that were being used to feed the Lamanite prisoners to be used for his own people instead. He also wanted his own people back to strengthen his army.

Now the Lamanites had captured many women and children, but there was not a single woman or child among the prisoners Moroni had taken. So Moroni came up with a plan to get back as many Nephite prisoners from the Lamanites as possible.
So he wrote a letter and sent it back with the servant of Ammoron -- the same person who had brought Ammoron's letter to Moroni. These are the words he wrote to Ammoron:
Ammoron, I have written to you about this war that you have waged against my people -- or rather that your brother started against them and that you are determined to continue even after his death.

I would tell you something about the justice of God and the sword of his almighty wrath that hangs over you unless you repent and withdraw your armies into your own lands, or the land of your possessions, which is the land of Nephi.

I would tell you these things if you were capable of listening to them. I would tell you about the terrible hell that waits to receive murderers like you and your brother, unless you repent, abandon your murderous plans, and return with your armies to your own lands.
But since you have already rejected these things and have fought against the people of the Lord, I expect you will do it again.
And now, we are prepared for you. Unless you abandon your plans, you will bring down the wrath of the God you have rejected upon yourselves, even to your complete destruction.

But as the Lord lives, our armies will come against you unless you withdraw, and you will soon face death, because we will hold our cities and our lands. We will defend our religion and the cause of our God.

But I suppose I am talking to you about these things for nothing, because I suppose you are a child of hell. So I will close my letter by telling you this: I will not exchange prisoners unless you give up a man and his wife and his children for each one prisoner. If you agree to this, I will make the exchange.

And if you do not do this, I will come against you with my armies. I will even arm my women and children, and I will come against you and follow you all the way into your own land, which is the land of our first inheritance. It will be blood for blood, life for life. I will fight you until you are destroyed from the face of the earth.

I am angry, and so are my people. You have tried to murder us, and we have only tried to defend ourselves. But if you keep trying to destroy us, we will try to destroy you. We will take back our land, the land of our first inheritance.
Now I close my letter. I am Moroni; I am a leader of the people of the Nephites.
When Ammoron received this letter, he was angry. He wrote another letter to Moroni, and these are the words he wrote:

I am Ammoron, the king of the Lamanites. I am the brother of Amalickiah, whom you have murdered. I will avenge his blood upon you, and I will come against you with my armies, for I do not fear your threats.
Your fathers wronged their brothers by robbing them of their right to govern, when the government rightly belonged to them.
Now if you will lay down your weapons and submit to being governed by those to whom the government rightfully belongs, then I will have my people lay down their weapons and stop making war.
You have made many threats against me and my people, but we do not fear your threats.

Nevertheless, I will gladly exchange prisoners as you requested, so that I can save my food for my warriors. And we will wage an eternal war, either until the Nephites submit to our rule or until they are completely destroyed.

As for the God you say we have rejected -- we do not know of any such being, and neither do you. But if there is such a being, we do not know that he has not created us just as much as he created you.
And if there is a devil and a hell, won't he send you there to live with my brother, whom you murdered and whom you hinted has gone to such a place? But these things do not matter.
I am Ammoron, and a descendant of Zoram, whom your fathers forced and brought out of Jerusalem.
And now, I am a bold Lamanite. This war has been fought to avenge their wrongs and to take back and keep their rights to the government. And I close my letter to Moroni.

55.text: When Moroni received this letter he was even more angry, because he knew that Ammoron was perfectly aware of his own dishonesty. He knew that Ammoron knew it was not a just cause that had led him to wage war against the people of Nephi.
And Moroni said: I will not exchange prisoners with Ammoron unless he abandons his plans, as I stated in my letter. I will not give him any more power than he already has.
I know where the Lamanites are keeping my people as prisoners. Since Ammoron would not agree to my terms, I will carry out what I said. I will fight among them until they beg for peace.
After Moroni said this, he ordered a search among his men to find someone who was a descendant of Laman.
They found one whose name was Laman, and he was one of the servants of the king who had been murdered by Amalickiah.
Moroni had Laman and a small group of his men go out to the guards who were watching over the Nephite prisoners.
The Nephites were being held prisoner in the city of Gid. So Moroni chose Laman and had a small number of men go with him.

When evening came, Laman went to the guards who were watching over the Nephites. When they saw him coming, they called out to him. But he said to them: Do not be afraid. I am a Lamanite. We have escaped from the Nephites, and they are asleep. We took some of their wine and brought it with us.
When the Lamanites heard this, they welcomed him gladly and said: Give us some of your wine so we can drink! We are glad you brought wine with you, because we are tired.
But Laman said to them: Let us save our wine until we go to fight the Nephites. But this only made them want to drink the wine even more.
They said: We are tired, so let us drink the wine. Later we will get wine with our rations, which will give us strength to fight the Nephites.
And Laman said to them: You may do as you wish.
So they drank the wine freely, and it tasted good to them, so they drank even more. It was strong, having been made extra potent.
They drank and celebrated, and soon they were all drunk.
When Laman and his men saw that they were all drunk and in a deep sleep, they went back to Moroni and told him everything that had happened.

This was exactly what Moroni had planned. Moroni had prepared his men with weapons of war. He went to the city of Gid while the Lamanites were in a deep sleep from their drunkenness, and he threw weapons of war in to the prisoners until they were all armed.
Yes, even the women and all the children who were old enough to use a weapon of war were armed by Moroni. All of this was done in complete silence.
Even if they had woken the Lamanites, the Lamanites were so drunk that the Nephites could have killed them.

But this was not what Moroni wanted. He did not take pleasure in murder or bloodshed. Instead, he took pleasure in saving his people from destruction. To avoid doing anything unjust, he would not attack the Lamanites and destroy them while they were drunk.
But he had gotten what he wanted: he had armed the Nephite prisoners inside the city walls and given them the power to take control of the areas within the walls.
Then he had the men who were with him pull back a short distance and surround the Lamanite armies.
All of this was done during the night, so when the Lamanites woke up in the morning, they saw that they were surrounded by Nephites on the outside and that their prisoners were armed on the inside.

They realized the Nephites had power over them. In this situation, they saw it was pointless to fight the Nephites. So their chief captains surrendered their weapons of war, throwing them at the feet of the Nephites and begging for mercy.

This was exactly what Moroni wanted. He took them as prisoners of war, took control of the city, and ordered that all the Nephite prisoners be set free. They joined Moroni's army and were a great strength to it.
He then put the captured Lamanites to work strengthening the fortifications around the city of Gid.
After he had fortified the city of Gid as he wanted, he had the prisoners taken to the city of Bountiful. He also guarded that city with an extremely strong force.
Despite all the Lamanites' schemes, the Nephites kept and protected all the prisoners they had taken and also held on to all the territory and advantages they had regained.
The Nephites began to be successful again and to reclaim their rights and freedoms.
Many times the Lamanites tried to surround them at night, but in these attempts they lost many prisoners.
Many times the Lamanites also tried to give their wine to the Nephites, hoping to destroy them with poison or drunkenness.

But the Nephites were not slow to remember the Lord their God in their time of trouble. They could not be caught in the Lamanites' traps. They would not drink the Lamanites' wine unless they had first tested it on some of the Lamanite prisoners.
They were careful in this way so that no poison would be given to them, because if the wine would poison a Lamanite, it would also poison a Nephite. So they tested all their drinks this way.

Now it became necessary for Moroni to prepare to attack the city of Morianton, because the Lamanites had, through their labor, fortified the city of Morianton until it had become an extremely strong fortress.
They were continually bringing new forces and new supplies into that city.
And so ended the twenty-ninth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

56.text: Now at the beginning of the thirtieth year of the reign of the judges, on the second day of the first month, Moroni received a letter from Helaman describing the state of affairs among the people in that part of the land.

These are the words Helaman wrote: My dearly beloved brother Moroni, both in the Lord and in the hardships of our warfare -- my beloved brother, I have something to tell you about our war in this part of the land.
Two thousand of the sons of those men whom Ammon brought down out of the land of Nephi -- now you know that these were descendants of Laman, who was the eldest son of our father Lehi --
I do not need to go over their traditions or their unbelief with you, because you already know about all of that.
So it is enough for me to tell you that two thousand of these young men have taken their weapons of war and wanted me to be their leader, and we have come out to defend our country.
And you also know about the covenant their fathers made -- that they would not take up weapons against their brothers to shed blood.
But in the twenty-sixth year, when they saw our suffering and hardships on their behalf, they were about to break the covenant they had made and take up weapons to fight in our defense.
But I would not let them break this covenant, believing that God would strengthen us so that we would not suffer more because of their keeping the oath they had taken.

But here is one thing that gives us great joy: In the twenty-sixth year, I, Helaman, marched at the head of these two thousand young men to the city of Judea to help Antipus, whom you had appointed as leader over the people in that part of the land.

I added my two thousand sons (for they are worthy to be called sons) to the army of Antipus, and Antipus was extremely glad for this added strength. His army had been reduced by the Lamanites, because the enemy forces had killed a great number of our men, which we mourn deeply.
Nevertheless, we can comfort ourselves knowing that they died for the cause of their country and their God, and they are happy.
The Lamanites had also taken many prisoners, all of whom were chief captains, because they had spared no others alive. We believe they are now in the land of Nephi, unless they have been killed.
Now these are the cities that the Lamanites had taken at the cost of so many of our valiant men:
The land of Manti (or the city of Manti), and the city of Zeezrom, and the city of Cumeni, and the city of Antiparah.
These are the cities they held when I arrived at the city of Judea. I found Antipus and his men working with all their strength to fortify the city.
They were worn out in body and in spirit, because they had fought hard during the day and worked through the night to maintain their cities. They had suffered terrible hardships of every kind.
They were now determined to conquer in this place or die. So you can imagine that the small force I brought with me -- these sons of mine -- gave them great hope and much joy.
When the Lamanites saw that Antipus had received more strength for his army, Ammoron ordered them not to attack the city of Judea or come against us in battle.
In this way, the Lord favored us, because if they had attacked us in our weakened state, they might have destroyed our small army. But we were preserved.
Ammoron commanded them to hold on to the cities they had taken. And so ended the twenty-sixth year. At the beginning of the twenty-seventh year, we had prepared our city and ourselves for defense.
We wanted the Lamanites to come attack us, because we did not want to attack them in their fortified positions.
We sent out spies to watch the Lamanites' movements, so they could not slip past us by night or by day to attack our other cities to the north.

We knew those cities were not strong enough to fight the Lamanites on their own. So our plan was this: if the Lamanites passed by us, we would attack them from behind while they were also being met from the front. We thought we could overpower them this way, but we were disappointed in this plan.
They did not dare march past us with their whole army, and they did not dare split their forces either, for fear that a smaller group would not be strong enough and would be defeated.
They also did not dare march down against the city of Zarahemla, or cross the headwaters of the river Sidon to the city of Nephihah.
And so, with their forces, they were determined to hold on to the cities they had captured.
In the second month of this year, many supplies were brought to us from the fathers of my two thousand sons.
Two thousand more men were also sent to us from the land of Zarahemla. So we were prepared with ten thousand men and supplies for them and for their wives and children.
When the Lamanites saw our forces growing daily and supplies arriving for our support, they began to worry. They started to make raids, trying to stop us from receiving supplies and reinforcements.

When we saw the Lamanites becoming nervous, we wanted to use a strategy against them. So Antipus ordered me to march out with my young soldiers toward a nearby city, as if we were delivering supplies.
We were to march near the city of Antiparah, as if heading to a city beyond it, near the borders by the seashore.
So we marched out, acting as though we were carrying supplies to that city.
Antipus marched out with part of his army, leaving the rest to defend the city. But he did not march out until after I had left with my small army and come near the city of Antiparah.
Now the strongest and most numerous Lamanite army was stationed in the city of Antiparah.
When their spies told them what was happening, they came out with their army and marched against us.
We fled from them, heading northward. In this way, we led away the most powerful Lamanite army.

We led them a great distance, until they saw the army of Antipus chasing them with full force. But they did not turn to the right or the left -- they continued marching straight after us. We believe they intended to kill us before Antipus could catch up, so they would not be surrounded by our people.
Antipus, seeing our danger, sped up his army's march. But it was nightfall, so they did not catch us, and Antipus did not catch the Lamanites either. So we all camped for the night.
Before dawn, the Lamanites were pursuing us again. We were not strong enough to fight them. I would not let my young sons fall into their hands, so we kept marching and headed into the wilderness.

The Lamanites did not dare turn right or left for fear of being surrounded. And I would not turn either direction for fear they would overtake us, because we could not stand against them and would be killed while they escaped. So we fled all that day into the wilderness until dark.
When morning light came again, we saw the Lamanites behind us, and we fled from them.
But they did not chase us far before they stopped. It was the morning of the third day of the seventh month.
We did not know whether Antipus had caught up to them, but I said to my men: We do not know why they stopped. Maybe they halted so that we would attack them and they could trap us.
So what do you say, my sons -- will you go fight them?
And now I tell you, my beloved brother Moroni, I had never seen such great courage -- not even among all the Nephites.

Because I had always called them my sons (since they were all very young), they said to me: Father, our God is with us, and he will not let us fall. Let us go forward. We would not kill our brothers if they would leave us alone. So let us go, before they overpower the army of Antipus.

They had never fought before, yet they did not fear death. They thought more about their fathers' freedom than about their own lives. They had been taught by their mothers that if they did not doubt, God would deliver them.
And they repeated to me the words of their mothers, saying: We do not doubt that our mothers knew it.
So I turned back with my two thousand to face the Lamanites who had been chasing us. The armies of Antipus had caught up with them, and a terrible battle had begun.

The army of Antipus was exhausted from their long march in such a short time and was about to be overpowered by the Lamanites. If I had not returned with my two thousand, the Lamanites would have achieved their goal.

Antipus had been killed by the sword, along with many of his leaders, because they were worn out from the speed of their march. So the men of Antipus, confused by the loss of their leaders, began to give way before the Lamanites.

The Lamanites took courage and began to chase them. The Lamanites were pursuing them with great force when Helaman came up behind them with his two thousand and began to cut them down, until the entire Lamanite army stopped and turned to face Helaman.
When the people of Antipus saw that the Lamanites had turned around, they rallied their men and attacked the Lamanites from behind.

And so we -- the people of Nephi, the people of Antipus, and I with my two thousand -- surrounded the Lamanites and defeated them. They were forced to surrender their weapons and themselves as prisoners of war.
After they had surrendered, I counted my young men who had fought with me, afraid that many of them had been killed.

But to my great joy, not one of them had fallen. They had fought as if with the strength of God. Never had men been known to fight with such miraculous strength. With such mighty power they fell upon the Lamanites that they terrified them, and that is why the Lamanites surrendered as prisoners of war.

Since we had no place to safely guard our prisoners from the Lamanite armies, we sent them to the land of Zarahemla along with some of the surviving men of Antipus. I took the rest and added them to my stripling Ammonites, and we marched back to the city of Judea.

57.text: Then I received a letter from Ammoron, the king, saying that if I would hand over the prisoners of war we had taken, he would give up the city of Antiparah to us.

But I sent a letter back to the king saying that we were confident our forces were strong enough to take the city of Antiparah on our own. Trading prisoners for a city we could capture ourselves seemed unwise, and we would only give up our prisoners in a fair exchange.
Ammoron refused my letter, because he would not exchange prisoners. So we began making preparations to attack the city of Antiparah.
But the people of Antiparah left the city and fled to their other cities to strengthen them. So the city of Antiparah fell into our hands.
And so ended the twenty-eighth year of the reign of the judges.

At the beginning of the twenty-ninth year, we received supplies and additional soldiers from the land of Zarahemla and the surrounding areas -- six thousand men, plus sixty more sons of the Ammonites who had come to join their brothers, my little band of two thousand. So now we were strong and had plenty of supplies.
We wanted to fight the army that was stationed to protect the city of Cumeni.
Now I will show you that we soon accomplished this goal. With our strong force, or part of it, we surrounded the city of Cumeni at night, just before they were about to receive a supply delivery.

We camped around the city for many nights, sleeping with our swords ready and posting guards so the Lamanites could not attack us at night and kill us. They tried this many times, but every time they tried, they were cut down.
Finally their supply delivery arrived, and the carriers were about to enter the city at night. But we, not the Lamanites, were waiting. So we captured them and their supplies.
Even though the Lamanites were cut off from their supplies, they were still determined to hold the city. So we sent their captured supplies to Judea and sent our prisoners to the land of Zarahemla.
It was not many days before the Lamanites lost all hope of receiving help, so they surrendered the city to us. In this way, we accomplished our goal of capturing the city of Cumeni.
But our prisoners were so numerous that, despite the size of our forces, we had to use all our men to guard them or else put them to death.

They would break out in large groups and fight with stones, clubs, or anything they could get their hands on. We killed more than two thousand of them after they had already surrendered as prisoners of war.

So it became necessary for us either to kill them or to guard them at sword point all the way down to the land of Zarahemla. Besides, our food supplies were barely enough for our own people, even counting what we had captured from the Lamanites.

In these difficult circumstances, it was a very serious matter to decide what to do with these prisoners of war. Nevertheless, we decided to send them down to the land of Zarahemla. We chose some of our men and put them in charge of escorting the prisoners.

But the next day those men came back. We did not ask them about the prisoners, because the Lamanites were attacking us, and the men returned just in time to save us from being overrun. Ammoron had sent a new supply of food and a large army to support the attack.
Those men we had sent with the prisoners arrived just in time to stop the Lamanites, who were about to overpower us.
But my little band of two thousand and sixty fought with incredible determination. They stood firm before the Lamanites and brought death to all who opposed them.
As the rest of our army was about to give way before the Lamanites, those two thousand and sixty remained firm and fearless.
They obeyed every command with perfect obedience. Just as their faith promised, it was done for them. And I remembered the words they had told me -- the things their mothers had taught them.

Now it was these my sons, along with the men who had been sent to escort the prisoners, to whom we owe this great victory. They were the ones who defeated the Lamanites, and the enemy was driven back to the city of Manti.
We held on to our city of Cumeni and were not completely destroyed by the sword, though we had suffered great losses.
After the Lamanites fled, I immediately ordered that my wounded men be found among the dead and that their wounds be treated.

Two hundred of my two thousand and sixty had fainted from blood loss. Yet through the goodness of God, and to our great astonishment and the joy of our entire army, not one of them died. And there was not one among them who had not received many wounds.

Their survival was astonishing to our whole army, especially since a thousand of our other soldiers had been killed. We rightly credit this to the miraculous power of God, because of the stripling warriors' extraordinary faith in what they had been taught to believe -- that there was a just God, and whoever did not doubt would be preserved by his marvelous power.
Now this was the faith of those I have been speaking of. They are young, and their minds are firm, and they put their trust in God continually.
After we had taken care of our wounded and buried our dead and the many Lamanite dead, we asked Gid about the prisoners he had been escorting to the land of Zarahemla.
Gid was the chief captain over the group assigned to guard and escort the prisoners.
Now these are the words Gid said to me: We started heading down to the land of Zarahemla with our prisoners. We met scouts from our armies who had been sent to watch the Lamanite camp.
The scouts cried out to us: The Lamanite armies are marching toward the city of Cumeni! They are going to attack and destroy our people!
Our prisoners heard their cries, which gave the prisoners courage. They rose up in rebellion against us.
Because of their rebellion, we were forced to use our swords. They charged at our swords in a mass, and most of them were killed. The rest broke through and escaped from us.
When they had fled and we could not catch them, we marched quickly toward the city of Cumeni. We arrived in time to help our brothers defend the city.
And so we were delivered once again from the hands of our enemies. Blessed is the name of our God, for it is he who has delivered us. He has done this great thing for us.

When I, Helaman, heard Gid's report, I was filled with great joy because of God's goodness in preserving us so that we would not all die. And I trust that the souls of those who were killed have entered into the rest of their God.

58.text: Now our next goal was to capture the city of Manti. But there was no way to lure the Lamanites out of the city with our small forces, because they remembered our previous tactics. So we could not trick them into leaving their strongholds.
Their army was so much larger than ours that we did not dare attack them in their fortified positions.
We needed to use our men to defend the areas we had already recaptured. So we had to wait for more reinforcements and supplies from the land of Zarahemla.
So I sent a message to the governor of our land to tell him about our situation. We waited for supplies and reinforcements from the land of Zarahemla.
But this helped us very little, because the Lamanites were also receiving large reinforcements and many supplies every day. That was our situation at this time.
The Lamanites kept making raids against us from time to time, trying to destroy us through strategy. Yet we could not fight them in open battle because they would retreat to their strongholds.
We waited in these difficult circumstances for many months, until we were about to starve from lack of food.

But then we did receive food, guarded by an army of two thousand men sent to help us. And this was all the help we received to defend ourselves and our country from falling to our enemies -- to fight against an army too large to count.

We did not know why the government was not sending us more help, and the men who came to us did not know either. So we were filled with grief and fear that perhaps God's judgments would come upon our land, leading to our overthrow and complete destruction.

So we poured out our souls in prayer to God, asking him to strengthen us and deliver us from our enemies, and to give us the strength to keep our cities, our lands, and our possessions, for the support of our people.
And the Lord our God did visit us with assurances that he would deliver us. He spoke peace to our souls and gave us great faith, causing us to hope for our deliverance through him.
We took courage with our small force and were filled with determination to conquer our enemies and to protect our lands, our possessions, our wives, our children, and the cause of our freedom.
So we went out with all our strength against the Lamanites who were in the city of Manti. We set up our tents near the wilderness, close to the city.
The next day, when the Lamanites saw we were camped near the wilderness by the city, they sent their spies around us to discover the size and strength of our army.

When they saw we were not strong in numbers, and fearing we might cut them off from their supply lines unless they came out and fought us, and thinking they could easily destroy us with their much larger army, they began preparing to come out and fight us.
When we saw them preparing to attack, I had Gid and a small group of men hide in the wilderness, and I also had Teomner and a small group hide in the wilderness.

Gid and his men were on the right, and the others were on the left. After they were hidden, I stayed with the rest of my army in the same spot where we had first set up camp, waiting for the Lamanites to come out and fight.
The Lamanites came out with their large army against us. When they were about to attack us with their swords, I had my men retreat into the wilderness.

The Lamanites chased after us at great speed, desperately wanting to overtake and kill us. So they followed us into the wilderness, and we passed right between the hiding places of Gid and Teomner without the Lamanites noticing them.
After the Lamanite army had passed by, Gid and Teomner came out of their hiding places and cut off the Lamanite scouts so they could not return to the city.
After cutting off the scouts, they ran to the city and attacked the guards who had been left behind. They destroyed the guards and took control of the city.
This worked because the Lamanites had allowed almost their entire army, except for a few guards, to be lured out into the wilderness.
In this way, Gid and Teomner took control of the Lamanite strongholds. Meanwhile, we traveled through the wilderness toward the land of Zarahemla.

When the Lamanites realized they were marching toward the land of Zarahemla, they became very afraid that it was a trap to lead them to destruction. So they began retreating back through the wilderness the same way they had come.

It was nighttime, and they set up their tents. The Lamanite captains assumed the Nephites were tired from their march, and thinking they had chased away our entire army, they did not worry about the city of Manti.
During the night, I ordered my men not to sleep but to march by a different route toward the land of Manti.
Because of our nighttime march, we got ahead of the Lamanites the next day and arrived at the city of Manti before them.
Through this strategy, we captured the city of Manti without shedding any blood.
When the Lamanite armies arrived near the city and saw that we were prepared to meet them, they were utterly astonished and struck with great fear. They fled into the wilderness.
The Lamanite armies fled out of this entire region of the land. But they carried with them many women and children.

All the cities that the Lamanites had taken are now back in our possession. Our fathers, women, and children are returning to their homes, except for those who were captured and carried off by the Lamanites.
But our armies are too small to defend so many cities and such large areas.
But we trust in our God, who has given us victory over these lands, allowing us to regain our cities and our territory.
We do not know why the government has not sent us more help, and the men who came to us do not know either.
Perhaps you have been unsuccessful and have had to pull your forces into your area of the land. If so, we do not wish to complain.
But if that is not the case, we fear there may be a faction in the government that is not sending us more men. We know they have more men available than they have sent.
But it does not matter -- we trust that God will deliver us, despite the weakness of our armies, and deliver us from our enemies.
It is now the end of the twenty-ninth year, and we are in possession of our lands. The Lamanites have fled to the land of Nephi.

And those sons of the people of Ammon, whom I have spoken so highly of, are with me in the city of Manti. The Lord has supported them and kept them from being killed by the sword -- not even one soul has died.

But they have received many wounds. Nevertheless, they stand firm in the freedom that God has given them. They are careful to remember the Lord their God every day and to keep his statutes, his judgments, and his commandments continually. Their faith is strong in the prophecies about what is to come.

And now, my beloved brother Moroni, may the Lord our God, who has redeemed us and made us free, keep you always in his presence. May he bless this people and give you success in regaining everything the Lamanites have taken from us, which was meant for our support. And now I close my letter. I am Helaman, the son of Alma.

59.text: Now in the thirtieth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, after Moroni had received and read Helaman's letter, he was extremely glad because of the welfare and the great success Helaman had had in regaining the lands that had been lost.
He made it known to all his people in the region around him so they could rejoice as well.

He immediately sent a letter to Pahoran, asking him to have men gathered together to reinforce Helaman and his armies, so that Helaman could easily maintain the territory he had been so miraculously blessed in regaining.
After Moroni sent this letter to the land of Zarahemla, he began planning again to retake the remaining territory and cities the Lamanites had taken.

While Moroni was making preparations to go fight the Lamanites, the people of Nephihah -- who had gathered from the city of Moroni, the city of Lehi, and the city of Morianton -- were attacked by the Lamanites.
Even those Lamanites who had been driven from the land of Manti and the surrounding area had come over and joined the Lamanites in this part of the land.
Being extremely numerous and growing stronger every day, under the command of Ammoron they attacked the people of Nephihah and began killing them in a terrible slaughter.
Their armies were so large that the remaining people of Nephihah were forced to flee, and they joined Moroni's army.

Moroni had assumed that men would be sent to the city of Nephihah to help the people defend it, knowing it was easier to keep a city than to retake one. He thought they would have no trouble holding that city.
So he had kept all his forces to defend the areas he had already recovered.
Now when Moroni saw that the city of Nephihah had been lost, he was deeply sorrowful. He began to worry, because of the wickedness of the people, that they might fall into the hands of their enemies.
All of his chief captains felt the same way. They were troubled and amazed because of the people's wickedness and the Lamanites' success against them.
And Moroni was angry with the government because of their indifference to the freedom of their country.

60.text: He wrote again to the governor of the land, who was Pahoran, and these are the words he wrote: I am writing this letter to Pahoran, in the city of Zarahemla, who is the chief judge and the governor over the land, and also to all those who have been chosen by this people to govern and manage the affairs of this war.

I have something to say to you by way of condemnation. You yourselves know that you have been appointed to gather men together and arm them with swords, scimitars, and all kinds of weapons of war, and send them against the Lamanites wherever they invade our land.
Now I say to you that I, my men, Helaman, and his men have all suffered greatly -- hunger, thirst, exhaustion, and hardships of every kind.
But if this were all we had suffered, we would not complain.

But the slaughter among our people has been great. Thousands have fallen by the sword, which might not have happened if you had given our armies enough strength and support. Your neglect toward us has been terrible.
And now we want to know the reason for this extremely great neglect. We want to know why you have been so thoughtless.
Can you sit on your thrones in a state of thoughtless laziness while your enemies are spreading death all around you? While they are murdering thousands of your people --

People who have looked to you for protection? People who have placed you in a position where you could have helped them? You could have sent armies to strengthen them and saved thousands of them from being killed.

But that is not all -- you have withheld supplies from them, so that many have fought and bled to death because of their great desire for the welfare of this people. And this they have done while nearly starving to death, because of your extreme neglect toward them.

And now, my beloved brothers -- for you should be beloved, and you should have worked harder for the welfare and freedom of this people -- but you have neglected them so much that the blood of thousands will cry out against you for justice. God knew of all their cries and all their suffering --
Could you really think you could sit on your thrones and, because of God's great goodness, do nothing while he delivered you? If you have thought this, you have thought wrong.

Do you think that so many of your people have been killed because of their own wickedness? I tell you, if you have thought this, you have thought wrong. Many have fallen by the sword, and it is to your condemnation.

For the Lord allows the righteous to be killed so that his justice and judgment may come upon the wicked. So do not think the righteous are lost just because they are killed. They enter into the rest of the Lord their God.

And now I tell you, I am deeply afraid that God's judgments will come upon this people because of their extreme laziness -- the laziness of our government and their terrible neglect of their brothers, of those who have been killed.
If it were not for the wickedness that started at the top of our leadership, we could have resisted our enemies so they would have had no power over us.

If it had not been for the war that broke out among ourselves -- if not for these king-men who caused so much bloodshed among us -- at the time when we were fighting among ourselves, if we had united our strength as we used to do -- if those king-men had not sought power and authority over us but had been loyal to the cause of our freedom and had united with us to fight our enemies instead of turning their swords against us -- if we had gone out against our enemies in the strength of the Lord, we would have scattered them, for it would have been done according to his word.

But now the Lamanites are coming against us, taking our lands, and murdering our people with the sword -- our women and children -- and carrying them away as captives, causing them to suffer all kinds of afflictions. And all this is because of the great wickedness of those seeking power and authority -- those king-men.
But why should I say much about this? For all we know, you yourselves may be seeking authority. We do not know whether you are also traitors to your country.
Or have you neglected us because you are in the heart of our country, surrounded by safety, and so you do not bother to send food or men to strengthen our armies?
Have you forgotten the commandments of the Lord your God? Have you forgotten the captivity of our fathers? Have you forgotten the many times we have been delivered from our enemies?
Or do you think the Lord will still deliver us while we sit on our thrones and do not use the means he has provided for us?
Will you sit in idleness while thousands surround you who also sit idle, and while thousands at the borders of the land are falling by the sword, wounded and bleeding?

Do you think God will consider you guiltless while you sit still and watch these things happen? I say to you, no. Now I want you to remember that God has said that the inner vessel must be cleansed first, and then the outer vessel will be cleansed as well.

Unless you repent of what you have done and start taking action, and send food and men to us and to Helaman so he can hold the areas he has regained, and so we can recover the rest of our territory -- if you will not do this, then we should stop fighting the Lamanites until we have first cleansed our inner vessel -- the very head of our government.

If you do not answer my letter and come out and show me a true spirit of freedom, and work to strengthen and supply our armies and give them food -- then I will leave some of my free men to defend this part of our land, and I will leave the strength and blessings of God with them, so that no other power can prevail against them --
And this because of their extraordinary faith and their patience in their sufferings --

And I will come to you, and if there is anyone among you who still desires freedom -- if even a spark of freedom remains -- I will stir up an uprising among you until those who seek to seize power and authority are destroyed.

I do not fear your power or your authority. It is my God whom I fear. It is by his commandments that I take up my sword to defend the cause of my country, and it is because of your wickedness that we have suffered so much loss.
It is time -- the time is now -- that unless you get up and act in defense of your country and your children, the sword of justice hangs over you. It will fall upon you and destroy you completely.

I am waiting for your help. If you do not send us relief, I will come to you in the land of Zarahemla and strike you with the sword, so that you can no longer stand in the way of this people and their cause of freedom.
For the Lord will not allow you to live and grow strong in your wickedness in order to destroy his righteous people.

Can you really think the Lord will spare you and fight against the Lamanites, when it is the traditions of their fathers that caused their hatred -- a hatred that has been doubled by those who have deserted from us -- while your wickedness comes from your love of glory and the empty things of the world?

You know that you are breaking the laws of God. You know that you are trampling them under your feet. The Lord has said to me: If those whom you have appointed as your leaders do not repent of their sins and wickedness, you shall go up to battle against them.
Now I, Moroni, am bound by the covenant I have made to keep the commandments of my God. So I ask that you obey the word of God and quickly send me your supplies and your men, and also to Helaman.

And if you will not do this, I will come to you quickly, because God will not allow us to starve. He will give us your food, even if it must be taken by the sword. Now make sure you obey the word of God.

I am Moroni, your chief captain. I do not seek power -- I seek to pull it down. I do not seek the honor of the world, but the glory of my God and the freedom and welfare of my country. And so I close my letter.

61.text: Now soon after Moroni had sent his letter to the chief governor, he received a letter from Pahoran, the chief governor. And these are the words he received:

I, Pahoran, who am the chief governor of this land, send these words to Moroni, the chief captain over the army. I say to you, Moroni, that I do not take joy in your great suffering. It grieves my soul.

But there are those who do take joy in your suffering. In fact, they have risen up in rebellion against me and against those of my people who are free men. Those who have risen up are extremely numerous.

It is those who have tried to take the judgment seat from me who have caused this great wickedness. They have used flattery and have led away the hearts of many people, which will cause terrible suffering among us. They have withheld our supplies and have frightened our free men so they have not come to join you.
They have driven me out, and I have fled to the land of Gideon with as many men as I could gather.
I have sent out a proclamation throughout this part of the land, and people are flocking to us every day, taking up arms in defense of their country and their freedom, and to avenge our wrongs.
So many have come to us that those who have rebelled against us are afraid of us and do not dare come out against us to fight.

They have taken control of the land, or the city, of Zarahemla. They have appointed a king over them, and he has written to the king of the Lamanites, making an alliance with him. In this alliance, he has agreed to hold the city of Zarahemla, which he thinks will allow the Lamanites to conquer the rest of the land. Then he will be made king over this people when they are conquered by the Lamanites.

Now in your letter you criticized me, but it does not matter. I am not angry, but I rejoice in the greatness of your heart. I, Pahoran, do not seek power except to keep my judgment seat so I may preserve the rights and freedom of my people. My soul stands firm in the freedom God has given us.
And now, we will resist wickedness even to the point of bloodshed. We would not shed the blood of the Lamanites if they would stay in their own land.
We would not shed the blood of our own people if they would not rise up in rebellion and take the sword against us.
We would submit to bondage if it were required by the justice of God, or if he commanded us to do so.
But he does not command us to submit to our enemies. Instead, he tells us to put our trust in him, and he will deliver us.

Therefore, my beloved brother Moroni, let us resist evil. Whatever evil we cannot resist with our words -- such as rebellions and divisions -- let us resist with our swords, so that we may keep our freedom and rejoice in the great privilege of our church and in the cause of our Redeemer and our God.

So come to me quickly with a few of your men, and leave the rest under the command of Lehi and Teancum. Give them the authority to lead the war in that part of the land, according to the Spirit of God, which is also the spirit of freedom within them.
I have sent some supplies to them so they will not die before you can come to me.
Gather whatever forces you can along your march here, and we will move quickly against those rebels, in the strength of our God and according to the faith that is in us.
We will retake the city of Zarahemla so we can get more food to send to Lehi and Teancum. We will go against the rebels in the strength of the Lord and put an end to this great wickedness.
And now, Moroni, I was glad to receive your letter, because I had been somewhat worried about what we should do and whether it would be right for us to fight against our own people.
But you have said that the Lord has commanded us to fight against them unless they repent.

Strengthen Lehi and Teancum in the Lord. Tell them not to be afraid, for God will deliver them and all those who stand firm in the freedom God has given them. And now I close my letter to my beloved brother, Moroni.

62.text: When Moroni received this letter, his heart took courage and was filled with great joy because of the faithfulness of Pahoran -- that he was not a traitor to the freedom and cause of his country.
But he also mourned deeply because of the wickedness of those who had driven Pahoran from the judgment seat -- those who had rebelled against their country and their God.
Moroni took a small number of men, as Pahoran had requested, and gave Lehi and Teancum command over the rest of his army. Then he marched toward the land of Gideon.
Wherever he went, he raised the standard of liberty and gathered as many men as he could during his march to the land of Gideon.
Thousands flocked to his banner and took up their swords to defend their freedom, so they would not be brought into bondage.

When Moroni had gathered all the men he could during his march, he arrived at the land of Gideon. Joining his forces with Pahoran's, they became extremely strong -- even stronger than the men of Pachus, who was the king of the rebels who had driven the free men out of the land of Zarahemla and taken control of the land.
Moroni and Pahoran marched down with their armies into the land of Zarahemla, went out against the city, and met the men of Pachus in battle.
Pachus was killed and his men were taken prisoner, and Pahoran was restored to his judgment seat.

The men of Pachus were put on trial according to the law, along with those king-men who had been captured and thrown into prison. They were executed according to the law. Those men of Pachus and those king-men who would not take up arms in defense of their country, but would fight against it, were put to death.
It was necessary for this law to be strictly enforced for the safety of their country. Whoever was found opposing their freedom was quickly executed according to the law.

And so ended the thirtieth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi. Moroni and Pahoran had restored peace to the land of Zarahemla among their own people, having put to death all those who were not loyal to the cause of freedom.
At the beginning of the thirty-first year of the judges over the people of Nephi, Moroni immediately sent supplies and an army of six thousand men to Helaman to help him hold that part of the land.
He also sent an army of six thousand men with plenty of food to the armies of Lehi and Teancum. This was done to strengthen the land against the Lamanites.
Moroni and Pahoran, leaving a large body of men in the land of Zarahemla, marched with a large army toward the land of Nephihah, determined to defeat the Lamanites in that city.
As they marched toward the land, they encountered a large group of Lamanites. They killed many of them and took their supplies and weapons.
After capturing them, they made them enter into a covenant that they would never again take up weapons against the Nephites.
After the Lamanites entered this covenant, they were sent to live with the people of Ammon. There were about four thousand of them who had not been killed.
After sending them away, they continued their march toward the land of Nephihah. When they reached the city of Nephihah, they set up their tents in the plains of Nephihah, near the city.

Moroni wanted the Lamanites to come out and fight them on the plains. But the Lamanites, knowing of their great courage and seeing how large their army was, did not dare come out. So there was no battle that day.
When night came, Moroni went out in the darkness and climbed to the top of the wall to find out where in the city the Lamanites had camped with their army.

They were on the east side, near the entrance, and they were all asleep. Moroni returned to his army and had them quickly prepare strong ropes and ladders to be lowered from the top of the wall into the inner part of the city.
Moroni had his men march to the top of the wall and lower themselves down into the part of the city on the west side, where the Lamanites had not camped.
They were all lowered into the city by night using their strong ropes and ladders. So when morning came, they were all inside the city walls.
When the Lamanites woke up and saw that Moroni's armies were inside the walls, they were terrified and fled out through the pass.

When Moroni saw them fleeing, he sent his men after them. They killed many, surrounded many others and took them prisoner. The rest fled to the land of Moroni, which was near the borders by the seashore.
In this way, Moroni and Pahoran captured the city of Nephihah without losing a single man, though many Lamanites were killed.
Many of the Lamanite prisoners wanted to join the people of Ammon and become a free people.
All who desired this were granted their wish.

So all the Lamanite prisoners joined the people of Ammon and began to work hard, farming the land and raising grain, flocks, and herds of every kind. The Nephites were relieved of a great burden, being freed from all their Lamanite prisoners.

After capturing the city of Nephihah and taking many prisoners -- which greatly reduced the Lamanite armies -- and after recovering many Nephites who had been taken prisoner -- which greatly strengthened his army -- Moroni went from the land of Nephihah to the land of Lehi.
When the Lamanites saw Moroni coming against them, they were frightened again and fled before his army.

Moroni and his army chased them from city to city until they were met by Lehi and Teancum. The Lamanites fled from Lehi and Teancum, heading down along the borders by the seashore until they reached the land of Moroni.
The Lamanite armies all gathered together in the land of Moroni. Ammoron, the king of the Lamanites, was with them.
Moroni, Lehi, and Teancum camped with their armies around the borders of the land of Moroni, so that the Lamanites were trapped by the wilderness on the south and on the east.

They camped for the night. Both the Nephites and the Lamanites were exhausted from the long march, so they did not plan any strategy that night -- except for Teancum. He was extremely angry with Ammoron, because he considered that Ammoron and his brother Amalickiah had been the cause of this great and lasting war between them and the Lamanites, which had caused so much warfare, bloodshed, and famine.

Teancum, in his anger, went into the Lamanite camp and lowered himself over the city walls. He went from place to place with a rope until he found the king. He threw a javelin at him, which pierced him near the heart. But the king woke his servants before he died, and they chased Teancum and killed him.

When Lehi and Moroni learned that Teancum was dead, they were extremely sorrowful. He had been a man who had fought bravely for his country, a true friend to liberty. He had suffered many terrible hardships. But he was dead, having gone the way of all the earth.
The next day, Moroni marched out and attacked the Lamanites, killing them in great numbers. He drove them out of the land, and they fled and did not return to fight the Nephites at that time.
And so ended the thirty-first year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi. They had experienced wars, bloodshed, famine, and suffering for many years.

There had been murders, conflicts, divisions, and all kinds of wickedness among the people of Nephi. Nevertheless, because of the righteous -- because of the prayers of the righteous -- they were spared.

Because the war between the Nephites and Lamanites had gone on for so long, many had become hardened. But many others had been humbled by their suffering and bowed themselves before God in deep humility.

After Moroni had fortified the parts of the land most vulnerable to the Lamanites until they were strong enough, he returned to the city of Zarahemla. Helaman also returned to his homeland. And peace was once again established among the people of Nephi.
Moroni handed over the command of his armies to his son, whose name was Moronihah, and he retired to his own home to spend the rest of his days in peace.
Pahoran returned to his judgment seat. Helaman began preaching the word of God to the people again, because after so many wars and conflicts, it was necessary to restore order in the church.

So Helaman and his brothers went out and declared the word of God with great power, convincing many people of their wickedness. This caused them to repent of their sins and be baptized to the Lord their God.
They reestablished the church of God throughout all the land.
Rules were made about the law, and judges and chief judges were chosen.
The people of Nephi began to prosper again in the land. They began to multiply and grow extremely strong again. They also began to become very wealthy.
But despite their wealth, strength, and prosperity, they were not lifted up in pride. They were not slow to remember the Lord their God, but they humbled themselves greatly before him.

They remembered the great things the Lord had done for them -- that he had delivered them from death, from chains, from prisons, and from all kinds of afflictions. He had delivered them from the hands of their enemies.
They prayed to the Lord their God continually, and the Lord blessed them according to his word, so that they grew strong and prospered in the land.
All these things were accomplished. And Helaman died in the thirty-fifth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

63.text: At the beginning of the thirty-sixth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi, Shiblon took possession of the sacred things that had been given to Helaman by Alma.
He was a righteous man who lived uprightly before God. He continually tried to do good and to keep the commandments of the Lord his God. And so did his brother.
Moroni also died. And so ended the thirty-sixth year of the reign of the judges.

In the thirty-seventh year of the reign of the judges, a large group of men -- as many as five thousand four hundred, along with their wives and children -- left the land of Zarahemla and traveled to the land in the north.

Hagoth, being an extremely curious and inventive man, went and built a very large ship on the borders of the land Bountiful, near the land Desolation. He launched it into the west sea, near the narrow neck of land that led to the land northward.
Many Nephites boarded the ship and sailed out with plenty of supplies, along with many women and children. They headed northward. And so ended the thirty-seventh year.
In the thirty-eighth year, this man built more ships. The first ship returned, and many more people boarded it. They also brought plenty of supplies and set out again for the land northward.
They were never heard from again. We believe they were drowned in the depths of the sea. One other ship also sailed out, and where it went, we do not know.
In this year, many people went out to the land northward. And so ended the thirty-eighth year.
In the thirty-ninth year of the reign of the judges, Shiblon also died. Corianton had gone to the land northward in a ship, carrying supplies to the people who had traveled to that land.
So it was necessary for Shiblon, before his death, to give the sacred things to the son of Helaman, who was also called Helaman, having been named after his father.
Now all the records that Helaman had were written and distributed among the people throughout all the land, except for the parts that Alma had commanded should not be shared.
Nevertheless, these things were to be kept sacred and handed down from one generation to another. So in this year, they had been given to Helaman before Shiblon died.
Also in this year, some dissenters went over to the Lamanites. They stirred the Lamanites up to anger against the Nephites again.

Also in this same year, the Lamanites came down with a large army to fight against the people of Moronihah, or rather against Moronihah's army. But they were defeated and driven back to their own lands, suffering great losses.
And so ended the thirty-ninth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.
And so ended the account of Alma, and Helaman his son, and also Shiblon, who was his son.
